content:
Prerequisites You need to have:
A GitHub account A Hugo website in a private repository, pushed to GitHub A Linux homeserver with Caddy Reverse Proxy running. A domain (or duckdns) If not, then this guide is not the perfect match for you (yet). However, you can get a GitHub account and set it up with this guide Git & GitHub Essentials . Another of my blogs covers How to create a blog with Hugo . Getting your own Linux homeserver is also doable, e.g. with a Raspi. Come back to this afterwards.
Summary of Workflow [Local Desktop] --(Push Markdown)--> [Private Source Repo] │ ▼ (GitHub Actions Build) | ▼ [Home Server] <--(Every 5 Mins)---- [Private Public Repo] (Port 22 CLOSED) "git pull" hugo-source (Private Repo): Holds your markdown, configuration, and themes. hugo-public (Private Repo): Contains only the compiled static HTML/CSS files. GitHub Actions: When you push to hugo-source, the action compiles the site and automatically pushes the raw HTML into hugo-public. Home Server: A lightweight system (like a script or systemd timer) pulls down the compiled code from hugo-public over HTTPS/SSH. Step 1: Bridge the Two GitHub Repositories Because GitHub Actions are strictly confined to their own repository by default, you need to give your hugo-source action permission to write into hugo-public.
Go to your GitHub profile settings -> Developer Settings -> Personal Access Tokens -> Fine-grained tokens. Generate a new token. Name it something like hugo-compiler-token. Under Repository access, select Only select repositories and choose your hugo-public repo. Under Permissions, go to Repository permissions -> Contents and change it to Read and Write. Copy the generated token. Go to your hugo-source repository -> Settings -> Secrets and variables -> Actions -> New repository secret. Name it PUBLIC_REPO_TOKEN and paste the token. Now, add this workflow file to your hugo-source repository at .github/workflows/compile.yml: Option A (Recommended) name: Compile and Push to Public Repo on: push: branches: - main jobs: build-and-push: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - name: Checkout Source uses: actions/checkout@v4 with: submodules: true - name: Setup Hugo uses: peaceiris/actions-hugo@v3 with: hugo-version: 'latest' extended: true - name: Build Static Files # working-directory: SUBDIR #<-- Use if Hugo does not live directly in the repo, but in a subdir run: hugo --minify #--baseURL "https://YOUR-DOMAIN.COM" #<-- allows to overwrite the baseURL in hugo.toml - name: Push to Public Repo uses: cpina/github-action-push-to-another-repository@main env: API_TOKEN_GITHUB: ${{ secrets.PUBLIC_REPO_TOKEN }} with: source-directory: 'public' destination-github-username: 'GITHUB_USERNAME' destination-repository-name: 'hugo-public' user-email: 'ME@EXAMPLE.COM' target-branch: 'main' Option B (Let’s tweak) If you followed the guide How to create a blog with Hugo , then your website/GITHUB_USERNAME/public folder might already be git initialized and contains a .git folder. This will cause several issues while running the GitHub Action, as the default cpina will try to copy your .git folder (and so the git history) from that repo to hugo-public as well. To avoid it, we change two things:
rsync -av --exclude='.git': Instead of a blind copy (cp), this uses rsync with an explicit exclusion rule. It takes your compiled Hugo site from hugo-source/GITHUB_USERNAME/public but completely ignores its underlying .git folder, preventing the File exists crash.
! -name 'README.md': Before copying the new files over, the script cleans out the old HTML files from the target repository so deleted pages don’t pile up. The find … ! -name 'README.md' command explicitly tells the runner: “Delete the old stuff, but do not touch the README.md or the .git folder.”
Finally, the .yaml file looks as following:
name: Compile Hugo website Repo and Push to jakob.harthaus.net Repo on: push: branches: - main jobs: # --- Job 1 --- # deploy-to-homeserver: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - name: Checkout Source uses: actions/checkout@v6 with: submodules: true fetch-depth: 0 # Fetches all history, safer for submodules - name: Setup Hugo uses: peaceiris/actions-hugo@v3 with: hugo-version: 'latest' extended: true - name: Build Static Files working-directory: GITHUB_USERNAME run: hugo --minify --baseURL "https://YOUR-DOMAIN.COM" - name: Deploy to Target Repo env: # Using your existing secret API_TOKEN_GITHUB: ${{ secrets.PUBLIC_REPO_TOKEN }} run: | # 1. Configure Git git config --global user.name "github-actions[bot]" git config --global user.email "ME@EXAMPLE.COM" # 2. Clone the target repository into a temporary deployment folder git clone https://x-access-token:${API_TOKEN_GITHUB}@github.com/jokobus/jakob.harthaus.net.git deploy_dir # 3. Delete old website files in the target repo root, but KEEP .git and README.md find deploy_dir -maxdepth 1 ! -name 'deploy_dir' ! -name '.git' ! -name 'README.md' -exec rm -rf {} + # 4. Copy the newly built Hugo files into the target repo, EXCLUDING the source's .git folder rsync -av --exclude='.git' GITHUB_USERNAME/public/ deploy_dir/ # 5. Commit and push the changes cd deploy_dir git add -A # Only commit and push if there are actual changes to prevent workflow errors if ! git diff-index --quiet HEAD; then git commit -m "Deploy website - Animated by GitHub Actions (${{ github.sha }})" git push origin main else echo "No changes to deploy." fi Commit .gitignore/workflows/compile.yml to hugo-source and push to GitHub. Now, it should compile and finish within ~1 Minute. The public code will automatically appear in hugo-public. Troubleshoot: If you get fatal: Remote branch main not found in upstream origin on hugo-source GitHub -> Actions: This means hugo-public has no main branch. Fix: Create directly on the hugo-public GitHub website a Readme.md, write some text and commit it. This will automatically create a main branch. Then: Manually trigger the GitHub Action again in hugo-source.
Tipp: If you want to deploy the same site to a GitHub Page YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME.github.io as well, you can do so by 1) Create a new repo called YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME.github.io with public access right. 2) Follow the instructions as described above in Step 1 to add a new Fine-grained token and add another Actions Secret to hugo-source on GitHub. 3) Duplicate everything that is under # --- Job 1 --- # in the .yaml file. 4) Replace the job name deploy-to-homeserver by deploy-to-github-pages and replace all other placeholders accordingly (e.g. YOUR-DOMAIN.COM will be YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME.github.io). If you followed my guide How to create a blog with Hugo , where we added the public folder as a submodule, make sure to 1) git rm --cached 2)YOUR-GIT-USERNAME/public, 2) Remove the public folder and 3) Remove all lines in .gitignores that belong to that submodule. Afterwards, all you need to do is to commit to hugo-source repo and push it to its main. GitHub Actions will take care of the compilation and pushing to YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME repo.
Step 2: Set Up Read-Only Access for Your Server To pull down the files securely from your private hugo-public repository without using your main GitHub password, you will use a Deploy Key. This acts as a single-purpose, read-only identity for your home server.
On your home server:
Generate an SSH key:
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_github_blog -C "homeserver-blog-puller" Press enter twice to leave the passphrase blank so it can run automatically.
Keypair Excursion:
id_github_blog (The Private Key): This is the file without an extension. It is your server’s ultimate secret. You must never share this file, upload it to the internet, or let anyone else see it. Your server uses this secret file to prove its identity when it talks to GitHub. id_github_blog.pub (The Public Key): This is the file ending in .pub. This is the “lock” that matches your private key card. It is completely safe to share. In the next steps you will take the text inside this file and paste it into GitHub as a Deploy Key. Copy the output of the public key: cat ~/.ssh/id_github_blog.pub
Go to your hugo-public repository on GitHub -> Settings -> Deploy Keys -> Add deploy key.
Paste the public key, name it hugo_public_to_homeserver, and leave Allow write access unchecked.
Next, configure your server’s SSH client to use this specific key for GitHub by editing or creating ~/.ssh/config:
Note: If you don’t have a ~/.ssh/ directory and ~/.ssh/config/ file yet, create them with mkdir -p ~/.ssh, chmod 700 ~/.ssh. If you have both: continue.
Host github-blog HostName github.com User git IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_github_blog IdentitiesOnly yes Adjust file permissions (safeguard):
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config ~/.ssh/id_github_blog Test it by cloning the repo onto your home server into your Caddy folder (e.g., /var/www/hugo-public):
sudo mkdir -p /var/www/hugo-public sudo chown -R $USER:$USER /var/www/hugo-public git clone git@github-blog:YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/hugo-public.git /var/www/hugo-public Verify that the html/css/javascript is there:
ls -l /var/www/hugo-public Step 3: Automate the Pull Since we don’t want a setup where the server is pinged by GitHub (as we would need to open a Port for it), it needs to check for updates on its own.
Option A: The Simple Webhook (Recommended if Caddy is Public) Caddy can actually trigger local commands when it receives a specific URL request. You can configure a secret path in Caddy that runs git pull whenever it’s visited, then use GitHub Webhooks to ping it.
Note: While this does not open port 22, it does use your existing open HTTP/HTTPS ports (80/443) which Caddy already uses to serve your site. Therefore, I recommend to continue with Option B
Option B: The Pure Pull Method (Cron Job) If you want zero outside dependencies triggering your server, set up a cron job to check for updates every few minutes. It uses almost zero overhead because git pull only fetches data if something actually changed.
Step 3.B.1: Open the Cron Configuration Tool Log into your Server terminal (e.g. via ssh) and run the following command to edit your user’s automated tasks schedule:
crontab -e Note: If it is the first time you are running this command, Linux will ask you to choose a text editor. Type the number next to nano (usually 1) and hit Enter.
Step 3.B.2: Add the Automation Line Scroll all the way down to the very bottom of the file using your arrow keys. On a new line at the end, paste this exact configuration:
*/5 * * * * cd /var/www/hugo-public && git pull origin main >/dev/null 2>&1 */5 * * * *: The timing interval. It means “run this command every 5 minutes, every hour, every day.” cd /var/www/hugo-public: Changes your Pi’s working directory to your website folder so Git knows which repository to update. && git pull origin main: If the directory change succeeds, it runs git pull using your Deploy Key to fetch new website files. >/dev/null 2>&1: This silently discards normal output and errors, preventing your Raspberry Pi from generating unnecessary system log emails every 5 minutes. Step 3.B.3: Save and Exit Press Ctrl + O (Write Out) and hit Enter to save the file. Press Ctrl + X to exit the editor. You should see a message in your terminal that says: crontab: installing new crontab. This means your background scheduler has accepted the task.
Step 3.B.4: Test the Pull Loop Your secure pipeline is now officially complete. The workflow functions like an active loop:
To make sure everything is working perfectly right now:
Make a small edit to a markdown file in your local source project and git push it to your hugo-source repo. Verify that your GitHub Action runs successfully and updates your hugo-public repo. Wait 5 minutes for the cron job to trigger on your Pi. Run this command on your Raspberry Pi to check if it fetched the update on its own: cd /var/www/hugo-public && git log -1 If you see your latest commit message and timestamp from GitHub, your automation is working flawlessly! Now you can point your Caddy web server directly to /var/www/hugo-public.
Step 4: Configure Caddy Step 4.1: Configure the Caddyfile Caddy stores its configuration in a file called Caddyfile. Open it using nano with administrative privileges:
sudo nano /etc/caddy/Caddyfile or
sudo nano /etc/hs_docker/Caddyfile depending on where you house the Caddyfile.
Add your website block.
your-website.com { # Point Caddy to your automatically updated Hugo directory root * /var/www/hugo-public # Enable standard file serving for static HTML/CSS/JS file_server # Optional: Enable Gzip/Zstd compression for faster page loads encode zstd gzip } (Make sure to replace your-website.com with the actual domain name you used for in hugo.toml or the Hugo –baseURL override).
Save and exit (Ctrl + O, Enter, then Ctrl + X).
Step 4.2: Grant Caddy Permission to Read the Directory Linux security requires that the Caddy background service has permission to read the files inside /var/www/hugo-public.
By default, Caddy runs under a system user named caddy. You can grant it access without breaking your user ownership by adding the caddy user to your personal user group, or by making the folder globally readable:
# Ensure directories are readable and executable, and files are readable chmod -R 755 /var/www/hugo-public Step 4: Reload Caddy If Caddy was registered as systemctl service:
sudo systemctl reload caddy If Caddy was installed via binaries:
caddy reload --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile If Caddy was installed via docker:
# If using standard Docker: docker exec -w /etc/caddy caddy caddy reload # If using Docker Compose (run this inside your docker-compose.yml folder): docker compose exec caddy caddy reload Congratulations! Your modern, ultra-secure hosting stack is fully operational. Let’s look at what you’ve built:
Port 22 is completely closed to the outside world, rendering your Raspberry Pi invisible to SSH brute-force bots. Your raw markdown source files and content drafts stay hidden away in your private source repo. GitHub Actions handles the heavy lifting of compiling your Hugo site. Your Pi proactively pulls updates via an encrypted SSH Deploy Key every 5 minutes. Caddy serves the static assets with blazing speed and handles your SSL renewals natively. Open up a browser, head to your domain, and enjoy your fully automated, production-grade website!
Whenever you want to update your blog, just create or edit it on your PC, revise the blog locally by looking at localhost:1313 in your browser (while running hugo server in your terminal). Once you’re done, commit and push to hugo-source. GitHub Actions and the Workflow we created will take care of the rest.
title: Deploy Hugo on your Homserver via GitHub Actions

content:
Overview When using Linux machines where you lack root privileges (sudo), you can still install applications locally. This guide covers how to set up standalone binaries (like VS Code, Obsidian, or custom tools) and integrate them into your desktop environment’s app launcher using .desktop files.
Step-by-Step Installation 1. Download and Prepare Download the executable or compressed archive (.tar.gz, .zip, .AppImage) for your application. If it’s a folder, move it to a dedicated local bin directory.
Example Path:
/home_local/username/bin/myapp/ but you can of course use another directory as well.
2. Make the application executable Find the main binary executable file in the folder.
ls /home_local/username/bin/myapp Use chmod to grant execution permissions to it
Let’s say we found the executable to be named myapp as well. If you downloaded a .AppImage, use this file instead.
chmod +x /home_local/username/bin/myapp/myapp 3. Add to System PATH (Optional) If you want to run the application from any terminal by just typing its name, you can add it to your .bashrc.
Note: On some systems, the bin folder might already be in your PATH.
Open your bash configuration: nano ~/.bashrc Add this line at the end: export PATH="/home_local/username/bin/myapp:$PATH" Apply changes: source ~/.bashrc 4. Create a Desktop Entry To make the application appear in your app launcher (Super key / Start menu), create a .desktop file.
Create the file:
nano ~/.local/share/applications/myapp.desktop Paste the following template and adjust the paths:
[Desktop Entry] Version=1.0 Type=Application Name=My Application Comment=A short description of the app Exec=/net/rmc-lx0181/home_local/fuer_jk/bin/myapp/executablefile Icon=/net/rmc-lx0181/home_local/fuer_jk/bin/myapp/icon.png Terminal=false Categories=Utility;Application; 5. Finalize and Refresh Make the desktop entry itself executable and update the desktop database.
chmod +x ~/.local/share/applications/myapp.desktop update-desktop-database ~/.local/share/applications 6. Search for your App Press the Super key (Windows key) and search for your application name.
Troubleshooting Tips Binary not found: Ensure the Exec path is absolute and points to the actual file, not its containing folder. Icon not showing: Ensure the Icon path points to a valid .png or .svg file. Validation: Run desktop-file-validate ~/.local/share/applications/myapp.desktop to check for syntax errors.
title: Install Custom Applications on Linux

content:
“Git is a distributed version control software system that is capable of managing versions of source code or data. It is often used to control source code by programmers who are developing software collaboratively.” [Wikipedia as of 2025-05-30 ]
I. Setup & Configuration 1. Install Git (Test if installed: git --version)
Windows
winget install --id Git.Git -e --source winget macOS
brew install git Linux
# Debian, Ubuntu, Raspberry Pi OS sudo apt install git # Arch Linux sudo pacman -S git # Fedora sudo dnf install git # NixOS / Nix nix-env -iA nixpkgs.git 2. Install GitHub CLI (gh) Essential for PRs and repo management from the terminal.
Windows: winget install --id GitHub.cli
macOS: brew install gh
Linux: sudo apt install gh or sudo pacman -S github-cli
Authenticate:
gh auth login 3. Basic Config Set your identity for all local repositories:
git config --global user.name "jokobus" git config --global user.email "jokobus@example.com" Note: Remove --global to use a different identity for just the current folder.
If using a credential manager, clear stored credentials if needed:
git credential-cache exit 4. SSH Authentication (Optional) Recommended for secure, passwordless access.
Create Key: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "jokobus@example.com" Path: Press Enter for default or specify a path (e.g., ~/.ssh/id_ed25519). Add to Agent: ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 Add to GitHub: Copy the public key (id_ed25519.pub) content to GitHub Settings -> SSH and GPG keys. Test: ssh -T git@github.com Switch Remote: git remote set-url origin git@github.com:user/repo.git II. Local Git Basics Init Initiate an empty git repository (in your current working directory, so cd/mkdir first). Afterwards git can track file/folder changes (add/remove/edit) in that directory.
git init Status & Diff git status: See what changes are staged/unstaged. git diff: See actual changes in unstaged files. git diff --staged: See changes waiting to be committed. Stage Add (stage) changes to the next commit.
git add <file> # Stage a specific file git add . # Stage all changes in current directory git add -A # Stage all changes in the entire repository (including deletions) Commit git commit -m "Some message" git commit -a -m "Some message" # -a commits all changes directly, skipping stage (for small changes, not recommended), or use -am Log & Help Log:
git log: Detailed history. git log --oneline: Compact history. git log --oneline --graph --all: Visual branch structure. git log -5: Show last five commits Tip: Use the arrow keys to navigate up/down and type :q for quit.
Help:
git <command> --help: Get help for a specific command. git help --all: List all available commands. Branches Create a branch (~copy) of the main branch, edit your branch and merge it with the main branch.
git branch fix-small-error # create new branch called fix-small-error git branch # verify in which branch you are (* main) git checkout fix-small-error # therefore switch to new branch # alternative: create and move to new branch in one line git checkout -b fix-small-error After making changes, add, commit and merge.
git add -A git commit -m "Some message" # Commit to fix-small-error git checkout main # Switch back to main git merge fix-small-error git branch -d fix-small-error # Delete temporary branch after successful merge If the merge fails due to a conflict, edit the file and commit again.
III. GitHub Integration Important: Always stay up to date (git pull origin main)! Working on outdated versions makes merging a nightmare.
1. Connect to Remote Clone existing repo: git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git # OR via SSH (preferred, if you have already created an SSH keypair) git clone git@github.com:user/repo.git Add remote to local repo: git remote add origin https://github.com/user/repo.git git push -u origin main 2. Useful Commands git fetch origin: Download latest version from GitHub (no merge). git pull origin: Download and merge (Fetch + Merge). git status: Show state of current repository. git branch -a: Show all local and remote branches. git branch -d <name>: Delete a merged branch. git branch -D <name>: Force delete an unmerged branch. git diff origin/main: Show differences between current branch and main. git remote -v: List connected remotes (URL). git remote show origin: Detailed remote information. 3. Disconnect from a GitHub Repository git remote remove origin git remote -v # If nothing printed, you are disconnected. IV. Guided Workflows 1. Simple Local Workflow Use this for solo projects or small fixes. Tip: use branches for no matter how small the change is, as this is only a small extra overhead but an excellent practice and avoids many issues (or saves your life) when anything goes wrong.
Keep updated and in sync with main (very important!): git pull origin main Create new branch: git branch mybranch git checkout mybranch or as a one-liner: git checkout -b mybranch Make changes: In that branch, do whatever changes and commit them. Merge back to main: git checkout main git pull origin main git merge mybranch If you get a Merge Conflict -> resolve manually and decide whether to keep HEAD/main or edits from mybranch. git push origin main Post-merge: Option A: Delete branch git branch -d mybranch Option B: Reuse branch git checkout mybranch git pull origin main git merge main (After merging mybranch into main, merge main back into mybranch to keep it updated). Start again from step 1 for the next feature. 2. Workflow with GitHub / Pull Requests (PRs) via GitHub Website Create, checkout and commit to a new branch. Push branch: Push your branch to GitHub. git push origin mybranch Create PR: Open the repo on GitHub.com and create a new PR from your branch. Review and Merge: Once reviewed, merge the PR on GitHub. If changes are requested (from a coworker, repo-maintainer, etc.), keep working on that branch and commit the changes to it: the PR is updated automatically. Wait until the PR is merged, before you delete the branch. Clean up: GitHub has a “Delete branch” button once the PR is closed. Start again from step 1. via Terminal (CLI) Pull latest: via Terminal Pull git pull origin main Create new Branch: git checkout -b mybranch Work: Make changes and commit locally. Push & PR: Push branch and create the PR from the terminal: git push -u origin mybranch gh pr create --base main (Follow the interactive terminal prompts for Title/Body, or use --title "..." --body "..." flags). Iterate: If changes are requested, keep working locally and push. The PR updates automatically. Merge: Once approved, merge from the terminal:bash gh pr merge --merge --delete-branch Start again from step 1. V. Utilities & Fixing Mistakes 1. Saving Work (Stash) Use when you need to switch branches quickly but aren’t ready to commit.
git stash save "working on feature x" # Save changes git stash list # View saved work git stash pop # Bring changes back git stash pop stash@{X}	# specifying which stash to pop (replace X) 2. Fixing Mistakes Undo file change: git checkout -- <filename> (Discard local changes). Amend: git commit --amend -m "New message" (Modify last commit message). Revert: git revert HEAD (Undoes last commit safely via a new “undo” commit). Reset: git reset <hash> (Moves back to a previous commit, discarding newer work). Caution: Destructive! Emergency Rescue: git reflog (Shows history of all actions; find “lost” commits here). 3. Ignoring Files (.gitignore) Create a .gitignore file in your repository root to skip logs or private files:
# Ignore all .log files except main.log *.log !main.log # Ignore a specific directory temp/ Note: Use .git/info/exclude for local-only ignores.
4. Re-evaluating .gitignore If you added files to .gitignore that are already being tracked, you must untrack them to enforce the rules:
Remove cached files: git rm -r --cached . Re-add everything: git add . Commit changes: git commit -m "Untrack ignored files and apply .gitignore"
title: Git & GitHub

content:
If you are working with Conda environments, you might notice that VSCodium or VS Code shows “import not found” errors (red squiggly lines) even though your code runs perfectly fine in the terminal.
This happens because the IDE’s Python extension doesn’t automatically know which Conda environment to use for its background linting and IntelliSense. By explicitly setting the interpreter path, you tell the IDE exactly where to look for your installed packages.
How to fix it Option A) Set default interpreter In your project root, create a .vscode folder and add a settings.json file inside it. Paste the following configuration, replacing the path with the location of your specific environment’s conda python executable:
{ "python.defaultInterpreterPath": "/Users/username/miniconda3/envs/my-env-name/bin/python" } Option B) Ignore all missing imports If you don’t want to specify an interpreter and simply want to hide the error messages, you can add this to your settings.json instead:
{ "python.analysis.diagnosticSeverityOverrides": { "reportMissingImports": "none" } } This tells Pylance/Pyright (the language servers) to stop reporting missing imports as errors, though it won’t help with autocompletion.
title: Avoid Python Package Import-Errors in VSCodium or VSCode

content:
VSCodium is the Open-Source binaries version of the popular Visual Studio Code IDE, mainly developed by Microsoft.
However, VSCodium lacks some nice features such as code auto complete, nice code coloring, funtcion preview at howver, etc. The good news is: You can easily enhance your VSCodium Experience, by following the following steps.
1. Install Extensions Install the
Python and Pyright (by ms-pyright) Extensions via the Extensions Store at the left, vertical bar in VSCodium.
2. Tweak your settings.json In the search bar enter: > settings.json. Choose the User Settings.json and paste:
{ "editor.hover.enabled": true, "editor.hover.delay": 150, "editor.parameterHints.enabled": true, "editor.suggestOnTriggerCharacters": true, "editor.bracketPairs.enabled": true, "editor.guides.bracketPairs": "active", "editor.suggest.preview": true, "editor.suggest.showDetails": true, "python.analysis.typeCheckingMode": "basic" }
title: Tune VSCodium for Python

content:
This is a short summary of the essentials of the keybord layout neo-layout variant bone for faster and healtier text (and math) typing on your PC.
Windows To use it on Windows follow these steps.
Install Copy the kbdbone.dll file in System32 to C:\Windows\System32 and the one in SysWOW64 to C:\Windows\SysWOW64 respectively. Grant access rights. (On 64-bit Windows you have to copy it with a 64-bit Programm, best by the Windows Explorer)
Execute the kbdneo_win10_install.reg file, which will add the layout to the registry for you. Again, grant access.
Reboot your system
In System Settings under Time&Language/Language choose the System Language and choose Bone as your keyboard layout.
At each boot: execute reneo.exe (e.g. via Tasksheduler)
Uninstall The uninstallation is best to be performed in reversed order of the installation
Stop reneo.exe
In the System Settings, remove the layout from the list
Execute the Registry uninstall file kbd_win10_uninstall.reg
Delete both kbdneo.dll files from C:\Windows\System32 and _C:\Windows\SysWOW64_s
Reboot your computer
Linux In most Distro’s you can choose Neolayout Bone in your System Settings.
If not (or if it get’s reset at every reboot) you can use the following command:
setxkbmap -layout de -variant bone or set it back to the normal german
setxkbmap -layout de I found that it is most convenient to add aliases to my bashrc
nano ~/.bashrc add the lines (doesn’t matter where, e.g. put it in the first lines)
alias setbone=setxkbmap -layout de -variant bone alias lfshgjf=setxkbmap -layout de -variant bone alias setde=setxkbmap -layout de alias lfswf=setxkbmap -layout de Finally source the bashrc
source ~/.bashrc Learn the Layout Well, the beginning will be tough, but definitely worth it.
For the start, I recommend tipp10 which has the option to learn bone in the online-version (but not in the desktop version). That is definitely a good start for the first few days, where you have to look up every second character.
Afterwards you can set the keymapping of the first layer as your Desktop background image, so you can always look characters up.
Good luck and stay with it!
title: Use Neoylayout

content:
What is OOBE? OOBE is the Windows Out of Box Experience, that you have to click through after a fresh installation of Windows on your Computer. There you can set the system language, keyboard layout, connect to the internet and
Problem 1: Windows wants to force you to use a Microsoft Account Microsoft wants you to use a Microsoft Account that is connected to their Cloud - to use all the amazing online Microsoft Features (and conveniently collect your data. Mpf). But instead of adding a Button “Use local account instead” they lay stones in your path. What a surprise! Solution 1 helps
Problem 2: You don’t have a internet connection at hand If you don’t have an Internet connection at hand, Solution 1 works for you as well. Note: Now you have to create a Local Account, a Microsoft Account is not possible without an Internet connection. (But you can always switch/convert to a Microsoft Account in the account settings once you have finished setting Windows up - for whatever reason you should like to do that. )
Solution 1 & 2: Install Windows with a Local Account Once booted a screen will show up to ask you to connect to your Wifi.
Instead, press Ctrl + F10 (or Fn + Ctrl + F10 if Fn always needs to be pressed for the function keys).
This will showm up a Command Prompt window. There type:
oobe \bypassnro The computer will restart automatically. Afterwards, there will be a button Continue with limited setup in the step where you should setup the wifi. Click it and continue with setting up your Local Account.
Problem 3: Bypass OOBE temporarily If you want to setup the computer with a Local Account, follow solution 1. This guide is for a different use case: You want to sell / give away your computer. You have Windows freshly installed and want to enter it (temporarily) to install drivers, updates or just see that everything is working fine. But you want that the person who gets the computer enters the OOBE and can setup everything else for themselve - with a fresh Feeling and no leftover config / wifi connection passwords from your side. In that case you can bypass OOBE.
Solution 3: Bypass OOBE temporarily Method 1 - Key combination Press the key combination Ctrl+Shift+F3. That should normally be the easiest way.
Note: Sometimes the Fn key is locked in the Bios Settings, then you would have to press Ctrl+Fn+Shift+F3.
Method 2 On the first OOBE screen, press Ctrl+F10 (or Ctrl+Fn+F10)
This will show up a Command Prompt window. There type:
cd \Windows\System32\oobe msobe.exe /bypass Restart the computer
shutdown /r /t After the reboot, Windows should directly take you to the login screen.
Method 3 - Registry Entry You can also try to set a OOBE entry in the Windows Registry.
Press Shift+F10 to open the command prompt during the Windows setup. Add a registry dword
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Setup" /v "OOBEComplete" /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f Restart your computer with
shutdown /r /t 0 On the next boot Windows should skip OOBE and log you in as a temporary administrator.
After you’re done, open Command Prompt (e.g. via the Startmenu) and execute
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Setup" /v "OOBEComplete" /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f or delete the key entirely
reg delete "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Setup" /v "OOBEComplete" /f Restart the computer and you should be directly taken back to the OOBE.
title: OOBE

content:
This post is my personal SQL Cheat Sheet, based on the SQL tutorial by W3Schools .
tl;dr SQL let’s you access and manipulate databases.
Why SQL SQL can execute queries, retrieve data, insert records, update records, delete record to/from a database as well as create/delete a database, create/delete tables in a database, create views and stored procedures in a database and finally set permissions on tables, procedures and views.
Many database programs build on SQL, but add some (proprietary) functionalities.
If you want to embedd database information via SQL on a website you use a database program such as MySQL. Besides you will need a server-side scripting language (PHP) and HTML and CSS for the frontend.
Basics Column = A Feature or observation criteria Row = A Record or observation Tipp: SQL keywords are not case sensitive
SELECT column1, column2 FROM mytable WHERE condition; SELECT * FROM mytable; select column1, column2 from mytable; Some of the most important SQL commands Command Description SELECT extracts data from a database UPDATE updates data in a database DELETE deletes data from a database INSERT INTO inserts new data into a database CREATE DATABASE creates a new database ALTER DATABASE modifies a database CREATE TABLE cretes a new table ALTER TABLE modifies a table DROP TABLE deletes a table CREATE INDEX creates an index (search key) DROP INDEX deletes an index SELECT DISCTINCT SELECT DISTINCT returns only distinct (non-duplicate) values
SELECT DISTINCT column1 FROM mytable; You can combine it with the COUNT argument, to count how many distinct entries are there, e.g. in the column Country.
SELECT COUNT (DISTINCT Country) FROM mytable; ORDER BY Use the ORDER BY statement to order the order of the rows, e.g. py ascending price.
SELECT column1, column2 FROM mytable ORDER BY column1, column2 ASC|DESC; SELECT ProductName, Price FROM mytable WHERE Price>=50 ORDER BY Price DESC, ProductName ASC; WHERE Appending WHERE you can filter the returned entities. E.g. the following example only returns records where the Country feature is Germany. (Of course the table has to have columns called ‘Country’ and ‘Revenue’. ) Use single quotes for Text / “Strings”.
SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Country='Germany'; SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Revenue > 100000; As in other programming languages, you can use
Operator Description = equal > greater than < less than >= greater than or equal <= less than or equal <> not equal != not equal (in some SQL versions) BETWEEN between a certain range LIKE search for a pattern IN to specify multiple possible values for a column AND, OR, NOT operator The WHERE clause can contain one or multiple AND, OR and NOT operators. AND and OR are rather self explaining. NOT is used with other operators to invert their result. You can combine multiple operators and use brackets for semantics.
SELECT column1, column2, FROM mytable WHERE condition1 AND (condition2 OR condition3) AND NOT condition4; SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Country='Germany' AND Revenue > 1000000; Some Examples: First, select all entries from countries that start with G (Gambia, Germany, Greece, etc.) or F (Finnland, France, Fidji, etc.) and where the revenue is greater than 100.
SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Revenue > 100 AND Country LIKE 'G%' OR Country LIKE 'F%'; SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Country IN ('Germany', 'France'); Example: All entrise with a revenue that is not between specific boundaries shall be returned.
SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Revenue NOT BETWEEN 100 AND 1000; INSERT INTO INSERT INTO is used to insert new records in a table. There are two different ways to write it:
By using the first command you can specify only specific columns, the rest is left blank (null value). INSERT INTO mytable (column1, column2, column3) VALUES (value1, value2, value3); And with this command you can enter multiple lines (here it’s three) with one call. INSERT INTO mytable VALUES (value11, value12, value13), (vaule21, value22, value23), (value31, value32, value33); NULL Value A field with a NULL is a field without a value. You can use the IS NULL and IS NOT NULL operators to check for nulls. Something like column1 == NULL does not work.
SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE column1 IS NULL; SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE column1 IS NOT NULL; UPDATE SET Use the UPDATE to modify an existing record. Be careful that the WHERE condition is exact (e.g. use some distinct ID)!
UPDATE mytable SET column1 = value1, column2 = value2 WHERE condition; DELETE Of course it is also possible to delete records again. To clear a table, just ommit the WHERE condition.
DELETE FROM mytable WHERE condition; DROP TABLE (Delete Table) To delete a table again, use
DROP TABLE mytable; SELECT TOP The SELECT TOP clause is used to specify the number of records to return. (E.g. the table has 100 records but you want just the first three returned. )
Note: This command may differ for different database systems.
SELECT TOP 3 * FROM mytable; SELECT TOP 50 PERCENT * FROM mytables; In MySQL for example the syntax is
SELECT * FROM mytable LIMIT 3; Aggregate Functions An aggregate function performs a calculation on a set of values and returns a single value. They are often used together with GROUP BY which splits the return into groups first. Aggregate functions ignore null values.
Aggregate Function Description MIN() returns the smallest value within the selected column MAX() returns the largest value within the selected column COUNT() returns the number of rows in a set SUM() retruns the total sum of a numerical column AVG() returns the average value of a numerical column MIN() and MAX() SELECT MIN(columnname) FROM mytable WHERE condition; SELECT MIN(Price) FROM mytable; SELECT MAX(Price) FROM mytable; AS When using MIN() or MAX() the returned column does not have a descriptive name, but with the AS keyword one can set a name.
SELECT MIN(Price) AS SmallestPrice FROM Products; COUNT() Use COUNT to count the number of rows that match a specific condition. If you ommit WHERE and use a column without NULLs (e.g. an ID column) this will effectively return the length of the table. Add DISTINCT to don’t count duplicates in.
SELECT COUNT(columnname) FROM mytable WHERE condition; SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT columnname) FROM mytable WHERE condition; You can even use an ALIAS to name the returned column “Number of rows”
SELECT COUNT(*) AS [Number of rows] FROM mytable; Often it is useful to combine COUNT and GROUP BY. This example will return how many entries are there for each country.
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM mytable GROUP BY Country; SUM() The SUM clause is used to sum all entries in a numeric column.
SELECT SUM(Price) AS [Total Cost] FROM mytable; SELECT OrderID, SUM(Price) AS [Cost per Order] FROM mytable GROUP BY OrderID; AVG() The AVG clause takes the average of a numeric column. You can use it e.g. to find all rows where with a value greater than the average.
SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Price > (SELECT AVG(Price) FROM mytable); LIKE The LIKE operator is used in a WHERE clause to search for a specified pattern in a column, often in combination with a Wildcard.
The percent sign % represents zero, one or multiple characters The underscore sign _ represents one and only one single character E.g. select all rows where the country’s name starts with G. % is a wildcard for th rest of the name (otherwise SQL would only return countries whose whole name is ‘G’).
SELECT column1, column2 FROM mytable WHERE column1 LIKE pattern; SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Country LIKE 'G%';
title: SQL

content:
Why Conda? Conda is an environment and package management system, widely used in scientific computing and data science. Very handy if you program in Python. Conda features:
Cross-Platform Compatibility Package Management Environment Management (you can switch easily) Version control Dependency Management The handy thing about conda is that you can create a new environment for each project that you have. Then you install all the packages for each project in it’s own dedicated environment. Then you can swap back and forth to another project that might need totally different packages and versions. The different packages and versions are kind of ‘containerized’ and don’t interfer with each other.
Installation You can install Anaconda (GUI) or miniconda (minimalistic). However, I recommend the community-driven miniforge ‘version’.
Download the Miniforge Installer from the conda-forge website or use
wget https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge/releases/latest/download/Miniforge3-Linux-x86_64.sh Then run the script
bash Miniforge3-Linux-x86_64.sh -p /path/where/to/install/miniforge This will add the path to PATH. If interested, take a look at the ~/.bashrc file.
Test Conda is installed
conda info conda --version conda --help # To get an overview Customization You can skip this chapter, if you don’t want to customize the installation. In most cases the default is all you need.
If you want to manually specify where conda should create new environments and store packages, write it into a .condarc file.
Depending on the scope you want this file to have you can either put it into a project folder or place it under root. For global:
nano ~/.condarc Paste
envs_dirs: - /path/to/your/custom/environments pkgs_dirs: - /path/to/your/custo/packages You can also use specify channels there, just append the following:
channels: - conda-forge conda-forge is the (only) default channel.
To verify your configuration:
conda config --show Environments List Environments To see which environments are installed let conda list them
conda env list Create a new environment Environments are handy! You can create a new environment for each project you have and each environment will be independent from the other environments. E.g. you can download for a specific project python 3.9 and matplotlib and for another python 13.3 and tensorflow. Just create two different environments and install all the packages. See the introducion why Conda is useful.
You can create a new environment (here called my_environment) with one of the following commands:
conda create -n my_environment # Specify -n or --name conda create -n my_environment python=3.9 # Specify python version There are a bunch of possibilites how to specify the version, for both the python version and the package version.
Constraint Specification Result Fuzzy python=3.9 3.9.0, 3.9.1, 3.9.2, … Exact python==3.9.0 3.9.0 Greater than or equal to python>=3.9 3.9.0 or higher, e.g. 3.10 OR python=3.9.1 3.9.22 AND python>=3.9,<3.10" 3.9.x, 3.12.x, but not 3.10.x You can also clone an already existing environment.
conda create --clone my_environment --name my_environment2 It is also possible to specify where the environment should be created (environment will be called according to the deepest subfolder, in this case my_environment).
conda create -p /path/to/where/to/install/my_environment Furthermore, you can save an existing environment to a .txt file
conda list --explicit > saved_environment.txt or create an environment from such a .txt file.
conda create --name my_environment saved_environment.txt You can delete an environment and all it’s installed packages
conda env remove --name my_environment Enter/Leave an environment In your console you see on the very left of each input line in which environment you currently are. E.g. on Linux
(base) jokobus@MyComputer:-$ (my_environment) jokobus@MyComputer:-$ and Windows
(base) PS C:\Users\jokobus> (my_environment) PS C:\Users\jokobus> You can activate an environment
conda activate my_environment and switch back to the base environment.
conda deactivate Disable Base Environment You can permanentely disable the base environment with:
conda config --set auto_activate_base false Rename/Delete an environment You can rename a conda environment with
conda rename --name old_name new_name An alternative (the old) approach is to clone the repository and remove the original. Create the clone
conda cerate --name new_name --clone old_name Remove the environment
conda remove --name old_name --all Update/Install specific python version If you have forgotten to install the right python version at the creation of the environment or want to update, then you can install a different python version in your existing conda environment. Just keep in mind that the packages need to be compatible with the new python version. First, let’s see which python version you are currently using.
python --version You can update python to the latest version
conda install python Alternatively you can install a specific python version (e.g. 3.12) with
conda install python=3.12 After installing a new python version I recommend to conda list all packages and see that all packages you need are still there. Also it might be a good idea to update the packages or reinstall them if they were removed. Continue reading for more info how to do it.
Packages Install/Uninstall packages Now comes the interesting part. The Installation of packages!
It is as simple as
conda install mypackagename You can even stack the packages.
conda install mypackagename1 mypackagename2 mypackagename3 You can also specify the version you want to use.
conda install mypackage=3.12 If you want to uninstall a package
conda remove mypackage Update installed packages You can update a package
conda update mypackage or install (update/downgrade) a specific version.
conda install mypackage=1.1.1 or update all packages
conda update --all List installed packages You can list the packages that are installed in your current environment
conda list That’s basically all you need to know for using conda. Have fun coding - conda will help you with the necessary package management.
title: Conda

content:
Today, I came accross my old Notes from my German Class at School. I decided to learn the stylistic devices (dt. “Stilmittel”) again. To do so, I want to use Anki and create a custom card with the three fields Name, Definition and Example. In this guide, I will explain how to create a custom Anki card (template) with multiple fields and cross-learning. When the cards are created, I have to learn each three times.
Name is given, find Definition and Example Definition is given, find Name and Example Example is given, find Name and Definition 1. Create Template Go to Tools>Manage Note Types (Ctrl+Shift+N) Click Add Choose Add:Basic (and reversed card) Name it something like Cross Learning (Name, Definition, Example) Select the newly created Note Type in the list (scroll down) Click Fields Rename and Add, so that you have 1:Name, 2:Definition, 3:Example. Save Click Cards Choose Card Type 1
Front Template
{{Name}} is a placeholder for the content written in the Name field.
Wrapping it by {{#Name}} … {{/Name}} ensures that the card with the Name of the stylistic device on the front is only created if Name is non-empty.
{{#Name}} {{Name}} {{/Name}} Back Template
{{Name}}<br><br> {{Definition}}<br><br> {{Example}} Choose Card Type 2
Front Template {{#Definition}} <br><br> {{Definition}} {{/Definition}} Back Template {{Name}}<br><br> {{Definition}}<br><br> {{Example}} Click on Options (upper right corner) and Add Card Type
Choose Card Type 3
Front Template {{#Example}} <br><br><br><br> {{Example}} {{/Example}} Back Template {{Name}}<br><br> {{Definition}}<br><br> {{Example}} Save all 2. Create a new Card using the Template Choose Cross Learning (Name, Definition, Example) as your Card Type
Add context
Save
Repeat for more Cards
3. Learn When you are done with creating cards you can start to learn them. You will be asked every card three times, with only the Name or the Definition or the Example given. (This sentence is a Polysyndeton by the way). You can also leave fields empty, then Anki won’t ask you those fields.
Have fun learning stylistic devices or any other content with the same structure.
title: Custom Anki Card

content:
This is my personal Java Summary. There’s nothing special about it, I just published it, because I already have it in markdown format, so why not?
A huge Thank You to https://www.w3schools.com/java/java_scope.asp . Most examples are more or less directly from the public Java Tutorial. Some of the more interesting I copied into my summary.
1. Basics Compile & Run C:\Users\Yourname\Path>javac Main.java	// Compile C:\Users\Yourname\Path>java Main	// Execute IO int number = 5; System.out.println("bla"+number); Type casting int x = 5; double z = x; // Widening cast: z = 5.0, done automatically int y = (int) z; // Narrowing cast: x = 5, need to specify the cast manually Strings String firstname = "Peter "; String lastname = "Pan"; firstname.toUpperCase(); //PETER firstname.toLowerCase(); //peter firstname.length(); // 5 String name = firstname + lastname; // Peter Pan, or String name = firstname.concat(lastname); // Peter Pan String <-> Integer Conversion String mystring = String.valueOf(myint); // if int myint = 5; -> mystring = "5" int myint = Integer.valueOf(mystring); // if mystring = "10"; -> myint = 10; Math helpers Math.max(myint); Math.max(5,10); //10 Math.min(myint); Math.abs(myint); Math.sqrt(myint); int randomNum = (int)(Math.random() * 101); // 0 to 100 2. Conditions & Loops If, else, elseif if, else, else if as in C
if (x>y){ // do … } else if (x==y){ // do … } else { // (x<y) // do … } The available logical conditions are
Symbol Meaning == equal to < less than <= less than or equal to > greater than >= greater than or equal to != not equal to Ternary operators if (x > y) { System.out.prinln("x>y"); } else { System.out.println("x<=y"); } Can be written as:
(x>y) ? System.out.println("x>y") : System.out.println("x<=y"); while loop while(condition){ // do … } do while loop do { // do … } while (condition); Switch switch (condition) { switch x: // do … break; switch y: // do … // if here no break, then condition z will also be executed switch z: // do … break; default: // do … }	For loop for (int i = 0; i < i; i++){ // do … } For-each loop String cars[] = {"Volvo", "BMW", "Audi"}; for (i : cars){ System.out.println(i); } Arrays String[] cars; String[] cars = {"Volvo", "BMW", "Audi"}; int[] myNum = {10, 20, 30}; 3. Methods Basic example Methods must always be declared inside a class.
public class Main { static void myMethod(String fname, String lname, int age) { System.out.println(fname + " " + lname + " is " + age); } static int myAddition(int a, int b) { return a + b; } public static void main(String[] args) { myMethod("Liam", "Miller", 5); myMethod("Jenny","Barker", 8); myMethod("Anja", "Meier", 31); myMethod("Peter", "the Pointer", 0); int sum = myAddition(5,3); //5+3=8 } } Method overloading You can declare multiple methods with the same name, but different parameters. Depending on the provided parameters the consequent method will be called.
int myMethod(int x) float myMethod(float x) double myMethod(double x, double y) Hint: Widening casting will be done automatically, but narrowing casting not.
4. Classes A class is a template for objects, and an object is an instance of a class. When the individual objects are created, they inherit all the variables and methods from the class.
public class Main { int x = 0; public static void main(String[] args) { Main myObj1 = new Main(); Main myObj2 = new Main(); myObj1.x = 5; myObj2.x = 3; System.out.println(myObj1.x); // 5 System.out.println(myObj2.x); // 3 } } Multiple Classes Each java class has to be stored in a file with the name of that class. So the Main class is stored in a file called Main.java and the class Second has to be stored in a different file called Second.java. One needs to compile both.
static vs public functions static functions can be called without creating an object (but creating an object is also okay) public functions must be called by creating objects public class Main() { static void myStaticFunction(){ // do … } public void myPublicFunction(){ // do … } public static void main(String[] args) { myStaticFunction(); // myPublicFunction(); // would throw a compile error Main myObj = new Main(); myObj.myPublicFunction(); } } Constructor The constructor is used to generate a new object. The constructor is autogenerated by java. If you explicitely write down the constructor, you can specify parameters (inputs at constrution and default values) public class Main(){ // This is the constructor public Main(int y){ int x = y; } public static void main(String[] args){ Main myObj = new Main(5); System.out.println(myObj.x); //5 } } Modifiers Access-Modifiers Access-Modifiers modifies access level of a class.
For classes
public: The class is accessible by another class. default: The class is only accessible by other classes in the same package. For attributes, functions & constructors
public: The code is accessible for all classes. private: The code is only accessible within the declared class. default: The code is only accessible in the same package. protected: The code is accessible in the same package and subclass. Non-access Modifiers Non-Access Modifiers do not control access level, but provide other functionality.
For classes
final: The class cannot be inherited by other classes. abstract: The class cannot be used to create objects. (To access an abstract class, it must be inherited from another class.) For attributes and methods
final: Attributes and methods cannot be overriden/modified. (final int x = 5; x = 10; // Compile error) static: Attributes and methods belongs to the class, rather than an object. abstract: Can only be used in an abstract class, and can only be used on methods. The method does not have a body, for example abstract void run();. The body is provided by subclass (inherited from). transient: Attributes and methods are skipped when serializing the object containing them. synchronized: Methods can only be accessed by one thread at a time volatile: The value of an attribute is not cached thread-locally, and is always read from the “main memory”. Encapsulation Method to ensure that sensitive data cannot be read by any class. Declare variables as private Write a public get-method which returns the variable value, and a public set-method which sets the value. Packages & API import package.name.Class; // Import a single class import package.name.*; // Import the whole package E.g. the useful Scanner-package, used to read user input on the console.
import java.util.scanner; class MyClass{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner myObj = new Scanner(System.in); System.out.println("Enter username: "); String userName = myObj.nextline(); System.out.println("Your username is: "+userName); } } Declare custom packages package mypack; // Package name lowercase to avoid confusion with classnames class MyPackageClass { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("This is my package!"); } } Compile the MyPackageClass.java
C:\Users\Yourname\Path>javac MyPackageClass.java Compile the package
C:\Users\Yourname\Path>javac -d . MyPackageClass.java -d keyword specifies the directory where the package should be stored, . means same directory.
Run the package
C:\Users\Yourname\Path>java mypack.MyPackageClass This is my package! Inheritance (Subclass and Superclass) handy for code reusability: reuse attributes and methods of an existing class when creating a new class.
// Superclass class Vehicle { protected String brand = "BMW"; // … } // Subclass inherits Attributes/Methods from Superclass class Car extends Vehicle { private String modelName = "X6"; public static void main(String[] args) { Car myCar = new Car(); System.out.println("I have a "+myCar.brand+" "+myCar.modelName); } } If you don’t want a class to be inheritable, use the final keyword, e.g. final class Vehicle.
Polymorphism The following example is very interesting. It uses inheritance and method overloading.
class Animal { public void animalSound() { System.out.println("The animal makes a sound"); } } class Cat extends Animal { public void animalSound() { System.out.println("The cat says: miau"); } } class Dog extends Animal { public void animalSound() { System.out.println("The dog says: wau"); } } class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Animal myAnimal = new Animal(); // Create a Animal object Animal myCat = new Cat(); // Create a Pig object Animal myDog = new Dog(); // Create a Dog object myAnimal.animalSound(); // The animal makes a sound myCat.animalSound(); // The cat says: miau myDog.animalSound(); // The dog says: wau } } Nested classes class OuterClass { int x = 1; class InnerClass{ int y = 2; } } public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { OuterClass myOuterObject = new OuterClass(); // New outer object OuterClass.InnerClass myInnerObject = new OuterClass.InnerClass(); // New inner object System.out.println(myOuterObject.x + myInnerObject.y) } } If you declare the InnerClass as private, an outside class can’t access it. When you use static, you don’t need to create an inner object. Abstract class Abstraction is the process of hiding certain details and showing only essential information to the user.
Abstract class: cannot be used to create objects, must be inherited Abstract method: can only be used in an abstract class, doesn’t have a body. The body is provided by the subclass. abstract class Animal { public abstract void animalSound(); // does not have a body } class Cat extends Animal { public void animalSound() { System.out.println("The cat says: miau"); } } class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { // Animal myAnimal = new Animal(); // Create a Animal object not possible Animal myCat = new Cat(); // Create a Pig object // myAnimal.animalSound(); // Execute the abstract function is not possible myCat.animalSound(); // The cat says: miau } } Interfaces Notes on Interfaces from w3schools .
Like abstract classes, interfaces cannot be used to create objects (in the example above, it is not possible to create an “Animal” object in the MyMainClass) Interface methods do not have a body - the body is provided by the “implement” class On implementation of an interface, you must override all of its methods Interface methods are by default abstract and public Interface attributes are by default public, static and final An interface cannot contain a constructor (as it cannot be used to create objects) Why And When To Use Interfaces?
To achieve security - hide certain details and only show the important details of an object (interface).
Java does not support “multiple inheritance” (a class can only inherit from one superclass). However, it can be achieved with interfaces, because the class can implement multiple interfaces. Note: To implement multiple interfaces, separate them with a comma (see example below).
interface FirstInterface { public void myMethod(); // interface method, no body } interface SecondInterface { public void myOtherMethod(); // interface method, no body } class DemoClass implements FirstInterface, SecondInterface { public void myMethod() { System.out.println("Some text…"); } public void myOtherMethod() { System.out.println("Some other text…"); } } class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { DemoClass myObj = new DemoClass(); myObj.myMethod(); myObj.myOtherMethod(); } } Enums An enum represents a group of constants (public, final, static variables). Used for known, fix values, such as month names, weekdays, colors, etc. Handy if combined with the switch statement.
public class Main { enum Level { LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH } public static void main(String[] args){ Level myVar = Level.MEDIUM; System.out.println(myVar); //MEDIUM for (Level myVar : Level.values()){ System.out.print(myVar+", "); //LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, } } } Scanner import java.util.Scanner; class Main { public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner myScanner = new Scanner(System.in); String inputString = myObj.nextLine(); } } Method Description nextBoolean() Reads a boolean value from the user nextByte() Reads a byte value from the user nextDouble() Reads a double value from the user nextFloat() Reads a float value from the user nextInt() Reads a int value from the user nextLine() Reads a String value from the user nextLong() Reads a long value from the user nextShort() Reads a short value from the user Date & Time import java.time.LoclaDate; public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { LocalDate myDateObj = LocalDate.now(); // Create a date object LocalTime myTimeObj = LocalTime.now(); LoclaDateTime myDateTimeObj = LocalDateTime.now(); System.out.println(myDateObj); // 2025-04-01 System.out.println(myTimeObj); // 20:20:01.123456 System.out.println(myDateTimeObj); // 2025-04-01T20:20.01.123456 DateTimeFormatter myFormatObj = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("dd-MM-yy HH:mm:ss"); String formattedDate = myDateObj.format(myFormatObj); System.out.println(formattedDate); // 01-04-2025 20:20:01 } } 5. Java Data Structures ArrayList ArrayList class is a resizable arry in the java.util package (normal arrays are not resizable), where you can add/remove elements on the go.
Use Collections in the java.util package to sort lists. Will sort by default in ascending order. Use option Collections.reverseOrder() for descending order.
import java.util.ArrayList; import java.util.Collections; // Used to sort the ArrayList (alphabetically, ascending, etc. ) public class Main { public static void main(String[] args){ ArrayList<String> animals = new ArrayList<String>(); // Create new ArrayList object storing Strings // Instead of <String> one can use <Boolean>, <Character>, <Double>, <Integer>, etc. animals.add("Zebra"); animals.add("Mephitidae"); animals.add("Cat") System.out.println(animals); // [Zebra, Mephitidae, Cat] animals.add(2,"Elephant"); // Specify position, start with 0 System.out.println(animals); // [Zebra, Mephitidae, Elephant, Cat] System.out.println(animals.get(2)); // Elephant animals.set(2,"Dog"); // Overwrite Elephant animals.size(); for (int i = 0; i < animals.size(); i++){ // do … } Collections.sort(animals); System.out.println(animals); // [Cat, Dog, Mephitidae, Zebra] Collections.sort(animals, Collections.reverseOrder()); // Reverse the order System.out.println(animals); // [Zebra, Mephitidae, Dog, Cat] animals.remove(1); // Delete Mephitidae animals.clear(); // Empty ArrayList } } List of all useful ArrayList commands LinkedList Built as a linked list (like in C: structs referenced by pointers), whereas an ArrayList is a real array and if the array’s size is not sufficient anymore, a new, bigger array is reserved and all content copied over. Frontend-functionality is mostly the same.
List of all usefull LinkedList commands HashMap Hash maps are key/value pairs of same or different type, e.g. HashMap<String, String>, HashMap<String, boolean>, Hashmap<int, double> etc.
import java.util.HashMap(); public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ HashMap<String, String> Capitals = new HashMap<String, String>(); Capitals.put("Germany", "Berlin"); Capitals.put("England", "London"); Capitals.put("France", "Paris"); Capitals.put("Austria", "Vienna"); System.out.println(Capitals); // {Germany=Berlin, England=London, France=Paris} System.out.println(Capitals.get("England")); // London System.out.println(Capitals.remove("Austria")); System.out.println(Capitals.size()); // 3 for (String myCountry : Capitals.keySet()){ System.out.println(myCountry); // Germany, England, France System.out.println(myCountry+" is in "+myCountry.get(myCountry)); } for (String myCapital : Capitals.value()){ System.out.println(myCapital); // Berlin, London, Paris } } } HashSet Purpose: Like an array, where each element exists only once, even if added multiple times.
Iterator Used to iterate through an array, arraylist, hashmap, etc.
import java.util.Iterator; Iterator<String> myiterator = animals.Iterator(); System.out.println(myiterator.next()); // Print first item System.out.pirtnln(myiterator.next()); // Print second item while (myiterator.hasNext() == true){ currentanimal = myiterator.next(); // [Dog, Gnu, Zebra, ...] if(currentanumal == "Gnu"){ myiterator.remove() } } System.out.println(myiterator); // [Dog, Zebra, ...] but no Gnu Using a for loop or for-each loop would not work, as we are removing items from the array in situ (thus change it’s length), while we try to loop through it.
Wrapper Classes Wrapper classes provide a way to use primitive variables as objects. The first character of the type classifier is written Uppercase, e.g. Char, Int, Double, etc. Useful if you work with arrays, arraylists where you can only store objects.
// ArrayList<int> mylist = new Arraylist<int>(); // Error ArrayList<Integer> mylist = new ArrayList<Integer>(); // Works // Create a new wrapper object Integer myint = 5; To get the object wrapper’s value: intValue(), byteValue(), shortValue(), longValue(), floatValue(), doubleValue(), charValue(), booleanValue().
To convert it to a String: .toString().
To get the length of the string
Integer myint = 1000; System.out.println(myint.toString().length()); // 4, as "1000" has four characters 6. Advanced stuff Java Exceptions try
If the block of code fails to be executed then run some failback code.
public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { try { // Initial code tried to get executed int[] myNumbers = {1, 2, 3}; System.out.println(myNumbers[10]); // Out of bound error } catch (Exception e) { // error in try{} so execute this: System.out.println("Something went wrong."); } finally { // executed when try-catch is finished System.out.println("The 'try catch' is finished."); } } } throw
Use throw to throw a custom error of type ArithmeticException, FileNotFoundException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, SecurityException.
throw new ArithmeticException("Some error message");
title: Java

content:
You may know the popular LaTeX package graphicx which you can use as:
\includegraphics[options]{filename} graphicx does not support svg, but there is the package called svg that does the job.
How to use the svg package: Install Inkscape On Windows you can use winget.
winget install -e --id Inkscape.Inkscape Copy your image.svg file into your document root folder (= right next to your .tex file)
This is a MWE for your .tex file:
\documentclass{article} \includepackage{svg} \begin{document} \begin{figure} \includesvg[]{image} \end{figure} \end{document} Notes:
File ending is optional (image or image.svg both work) There are many parameters one can set in the square brackets (e.g. width=1cm, height=1cm, scale=0.5, and many more) Download and read the svg package documentation on CTAN for more information Oh no! It would have been too easy. If you click on Build, you will run into an error:
example.tex: error: 120: Package svg Error: File `image_svg-tex.pdf' is missing. \includesvg{image} You can solve this error by opening (in TeXstudio) Settings>Commands and adding the flag
--shell-escape to your PdfLaTeX run command. The command now should look like:
pdflatex.exe -synctex=1 -interaction=nonstopmode --shell-escape %.tex If you are using XeLaTeX, LulaTeX, etc. you should add the flag there (probably, I didn’t test it).
If you are using Texmaker, the whole idea is the same, but the exact settings may be somewhere else.
Save the configuration and run again.
More oh no! We are not there yet. We need to solve the error message
example.tex: error: 119: Package svg Error: Inkscape version not detected. ...ncludesvg{image} This error means that LaTeX doesn’t know where to find Inkscape. Therefore we add the path whereever inkscape was installed as a custom command. To do so, first locate the right folder (on Windows the default is: C:\Program Files\Inkscape\bin) then open Settings>Build and add the path to Commands.
That’s it, now it should run.
title: How to include Scalable Vector Graphics (svg) in $\LaTeX$

content:
This is a short guide how to create a Hugo blog, using the tailwind theme, which is the one I’m currently using myself. This guide was written with unexperienced users in mind. If you follow this guide 1:1 you should get a running instance (no guarantee of course). If you are confident with the topic, you can use this guide to get inspired and modify the steps to your own preference.
Although the following steps are for Windows, the whole process is the same for Linux/MacOS. Only some of the console commands are slightly different. E.g. instead of the powershell command Start-process you can use nano or vim and instead of winget you may use your distribution’s package manager or homebrew.
Important concept to understand The blog will be hosted on github and will be stored in two different repositories. The first repository (let’s call this repo website) is private and stores all the code and content needed to build the blog. The second repository has to be named according to your github username yourusername.github.io, where you replace yourusername with your actual github username (Please also replace yourusername in all the following code by your actual username. In my case the second repository is called jokobus.github.io). The yourusername.github.io repository contains all the public html (and other stuff) that Hugo created and is nested inside your website repository. Most of your code can be private, but the subdirectory called public (yes, that is it’s name) has to be public. Once the website is built by Hugo, we only need to publish the stuff in the public subdirectory. We publish it by pushing the content to the yourusername repository on github. All other content outside the public folder is just needed to build the content of the public folder. Finally your blog will be available on https://yourusername.github.io .
Prerequisites On Windows, we should use the Windows Powershell and not the built in Command Prompt. If you don’t have Powershell installed, you can follow this guide . The quick way via winget is:
winget install -e --id Microsoft.PowerShell On Linux and Mac your default Terminal should do the job.
Secondly you need to have a Github account and git installed . Create a new github account here and install git by executing this in the powershell.
winget install -e --id Git.Git Then authenticate git to Github by one of the options mentioned in the official article .
The easiest way is to install the Github Desktop Client and authenticate there:
winget install -e --id GitHub.GitHubDesktop Follow the installation guide and open the Github Desktop Application. Login with your Github credentials there. You will automatically be able to use your credentials with the git command.
All prerequistes met? Let’s go!
Step 1: Install Hugo Follow the official Hugo guide . As a service I added the steps for Windows below. However, it is definitely a good idea to look at the official guide as well.
If you use winget, it is as simple as:
winget install Hugo.Hugo.Extended Step 2: Create github repositories Go to https://github.com and log in to your account. Create a new private repository called website and a public repository called yourusername.github.io (where you off course replace yourusername with your actual username).
Private repository First, we clone the website repository.
Move into the folder where you want to store your website and clone the repo. cd "C:/path/to/where/you/want/to/store/your/website" git clone https://github.com/yourusername/website.git Create a new Hugo instance. cd website hugo new site yourusername cd yourusername Optional: To see all the folders that were created, type:
ls Do you see a config.toml or hugo.toml file? This is the configuration file, where all the settings are stored. Later, we will modify it.
Public repository Now we clone the public yourusername.github.io repository and add it as a submodule. (Here is some unofficial, but good explanation what a submodule is. )
Clone the public repository next to your private one, add a readme and push it to github cd "C:/path/to/where/you/want/to/store/your/website" git clone https://github.com/yourusername/yourusername.github.io.git cd yourusername.github.io git checkout -b main notepad Readme.md Add a short description like “This is the public content for my Hugo website. " to the Readme file.
git add -A git commit -m "First commit to add Readme. " Switch back into your private repository cd ../ cd website/yourusername and add a submodule.
git submodule add -b main https://github.com/yourusername/yourusername.github.io.git public Check that everything worked by: cd public ls You should see a file called Readme.md and if you open it with
notepad Readme.md you will find exactly the text you wrote in the Readme.md file a few lines above.
Step 3: Install theme We use the Tailwind theme , but you can pick whichever you want. There is an extensive list of free themes on the hugo website.
cd "C:/path/to/where/you/want/to/store/your/website/website/yourusername" git submodule add https://github.com/tomowang/hugo-theme-tailwind.git themes/tailwind You can always update the theme by:
git submodule update --remote Step 4: Adjust to your needs First, we open the config.toml file and paste some prebuilt configuration.
Start-process config.toml Paste the following text (originally from tailwind and extended by me). Play around with it and adjust to your needs.
baseURL = 'https://yourusername.github.io' title = 'My website' subtitle ="A Blog about random topics. " author = "Your Name" enableInlineShortcodes = true theme = "tailwind" # Enables the robots.txt file, default is to allow all crawling, see step 8 on how to modify it. enableRobotsTXT = true # Please enable this feature if the site is available in Japanese, Chinese or Korean. #hasCJKLanguage = true jsonLD = true # If you're using Google Analytics, you can enable this feature. #googleAnalytics = "G-1234567890" # Disqus Comment System #disqusShortname = "" #Emoji support enableEmoji = true #Paginate, how many sites before break paginate=50 [markup] [markup.highlight] # lineNos = true noClasses = false [markup.goldmark] [markup.goldmark.renderer] unsafe = true [markup.goldmark.extensions] [markup.goldmark.extensions.cjk] enable = true eastAsianLineBreaks = true escapedSpace = true [markup.goldmark.extensions.passthrough] enable = true [markup.goldmark.extensions.passthrough.delimiters] block = [['\[', '\]'], ['$$', '$$']] inline = [['\(', '\)'], ['$', '$']] [params] # Base color of the website. # https://tailwindcss.com/docs/background-color base_color = "bg-gray-800" subtitle = "A Blog about random topics" #Site description description = "My Website" #Enable LaTeX math support math=true # this enables KaTeX, see https://katex.org/ for how to use it # Number of tags to be displayed. tagsLimit = 20 # Show or not to show the reading time showReadingTime = true [params.author] name = "Your Name" email = "" [params.header] logo = "logo.jpg" # Place logo.jpg in static folder sticky = false [params.footer] since = 2024 poweredby = true [params.ad] # Google AdSense # ex. ca-pub-0000000000000000 googleAdSense = "" # ex. 000000000 adslot = "" # Placement of ads postTop = false postBottom = false [params.post] # Show thumbnails thumbnail = true # Disable the "NO IMAGE" image. noimage = true # See the Tailwind CSS documentation to set it up. # Recommended settings are h-32 to h-48. # https://tailwindcss.com/docs/height thumbnail_height = "lg:h-44" # https://tailwindcss.com/docs/width thumbnail_width = "lg:w-auto" # Number of characters in description description = "130" [params.widgets] # Display widgets display = true # Order of widgets to display widgets = ["search", "archive", "category", "tags", "recent"] # If you're using Yandex.Metrica, you can enable this feature. #[params.analytics.yandexmetrica] # enabled = true # tagNumber = '' [[params.social_media.items]] enabled = false title = 'Github' icon = 'brand-github' link = 'https://github.com/yourusername' [languages] [languages.en] contentDir = 'content/en' languageCode = 'en-US' languageName = 'English' weight = 1 disabled = false [languages.en.params] reading_speed = 228 [[languages.en.menu.main]] identifier="home" name = 'Home' url = '/en' weight = 1 [[languages.en.menu.main]] identifier="about" name="About" url='/about/' weight = 2 [[languages.en.menu.main]] identifier="contact" url='/contact/' name="Contact" weight = 3 [languages.de] contentDir = 'content/de' languageCode = 'de-DE' languageName = 'Deutsch' weight = 2 disabled = false [languages.de.params] reading_speed = 179 [[languages.de.menu.main]] identifier="home" name = 'Start' url='/de' weight = 1 [[languages.de.menu.main]] identifier="about" name="Über mich" url='/de/ueber/' weight = 2 [[languages.de.menu.main]] identifier="contact" name="Kontakt" url='/de/kontakt/' weight = 3 [taxonomies] category = "categories" tag = "tags" archive = "archives" [privacy] [privacy.vimeo] disabled = false simple = true [privacy.twitter] disabled = false enableDNT = true simple = true [privacy.instagram] disabled = false simple = true [privacy.youtube] disabled = false privacyEnhanced = true [services] [services.instagram] disableInlineCSS = true [services.twitter] disableInlineCSS = true [sitemap] changefreq = "weekly" priority = 0.5 Also, it is a good idea to personalize the default post header. This is the header that will be created by default each time you create a new post.
Start-process /archetypes/default.md Replace the code in the file by the following code:
+++ title = '{{ replace .File.ContentBaseName "-" " " | title}}' description = "" translationKey = '{{ replace .File.ContentBaseName "-" " " | title | lower}}' author = "Your Name" date = {{ .Date }} archives = ["{{ dateFormat "2006-01" .Date }}"] tags = ["", ""] draft = false toc = true +++ Step 5: Add content To add content in markdown format you have to do the following:
Create a new blog entry via hugo new /en/posts/yourpostname.md or hugo new /en/posts/yourpostname/index.md both methods work the same, but in the second variant you can also store images inside the /yourpostname/ folder. Use your favourite text editor to open the newly created markdown file and add content. You may look at some examples in /themes/tailwind/exampleSite/content/ and read this useful guide about the Markdown syntax. All the content is written in the simple Markdown format. Multilanguage support Tailwind supports a blog with multiple languages. That’s a really nice feature. The code for the config.toml file I provided makes your website ready for multiple languages (in this case english and german, but you can simply add another language).
The important step is, to store two .md files in the correct directories with the same translation key in their headers.
The following graphic shows the most important parts of the file-tree inside your website repository.
