├── yourusername ├── archetypes ├── assets ├── content ├── de ├── posts ├── Hunde ├── images └── index.md ├── Meerschweinchen └── index.md └── Katzen.md ├── uebermich.md └── kontakt.md └── en ├── posts ├── dogs ├── images └── index.md ├── guineapigs └── index.md └── cats.md ├── about.md └── contact.md ├── data ├── i18n ├── layouts ├── node_modules ├── public ├── resources ├── static ├── themes └── tailwind ├── gitcommit.ps1 … └── config.toml ├── .gitattributes ├── .gitmodules └── README.md A few general words:
public In this folder all the html and other public stuff Hugo creates is stored. Only this folder needs to be public. config.toml This is your configuraton file (you could also rename it to hugo.toml if you want) themes this is where you clone your themes to Now, about the multilanguage support: Ultimately we want to have two different posts with the same content, but one in english and one in german. The user can toggle on the website, which article he wants to see.
To achive this, everything is already prepared for you.
Create a new article as you did in Step 5 Write some english content copy index.md from /en/posts/yourpostname/index.md to /de/posts/yourpostname/index.md, open the file and translate it to german. Make sure that the headers of both index.md files have the identical value for translationKey= in their headers (e.g. both have translationKey=“guineapig”) Step 6: Push to github In this step, we finally get to the point where you can see the result. All you need for this is:
commit the private code to the website repository Build the public content in ./public subdirectory commit the public code to the yourusername.github.io repository open https://yourusername.github.io in your browser To do so we have to execute the following code in our terminal (on Windows use Powershell rather than cmd):
First we have to go to into our website repository
cd "C:/path/to/hugo/website/repository" commit the private code to the website repository git add -A git commit -m "Some message" git push origin main Build the public content in ./public subdirectory hugo -t tailwind cd public commit the public code to the yourusername.github.io repository git add -A git commit -m "Some message" git push origin main open https://yourusername.github.io in your browser Start-Process "https://yourusername.github.io" Step 7: Automate github interaction with script This is way too much work! We don’t want to type half a dozen lines for just pushing our code to github. Therefore I wrote a little file that does the job for us. Disclaimer: The translation from batch to ps1 and bash was done by ChatGPT.
Note, that I built the files in a way that I can easily switch between different themes, in case I ever wish to do so. If you don’t want to use that, feel free to remove the unncessary code. Also, when the script asks for the theme name, it only takes the first character of your input. If you have two themes with the same first character, you have to modify the line that starts with for /f “delims="….
Windows Batch file (not recommended) Create a new file called gitcommit.bat in your website repository and paste the following lines. Edit the pathtowebsite-Variable in line 3.
@echo off title Commit Hugo Webpage to Github set pathtowebsite = "C:/path/to/my/website" cd pathtowebsite set /p message="Enter Comment Message: " set /p theme="Enter Theme Name : Tailwind (default) or Blonde: " echo --- website Repository --- git add -A if "%message%"=="" ( echo No message, committing changes without comment. git commit ) else ( git commit -m "%message%" ) git push origin echo Total code was successfully pushed to private repository. echo --- public Repository --- if "%theme%"=="" ( echo Theme Name cannot be empty, using default Tailwind set theme = Tailwind ) REM Convert theme to lowercase and get the first character for /f "delims=" %%i in ('echo %theme% ^| tr "[A-Z]" "[a-z]"') do set theme=%%i set theme=%theme:~0,1% if "%theme%"=="b" ( hugo -t Blonde ) else ( hugo -t tailwind ) cd public git add -A if "%message%"=="" ( echo No message, committing changes without comment. git commit ) else ( git commit -m "%message%" ) git push origin echo Public code successfully pushed. cd pathtowebsite echo Job done, everything was pushed. exit To commit your changes, type:
.\gitcommit.bat Windows Powershell (recommended) Create a new file called gitcommit.ps1 file in your website repository and paste the following lines. Edit the pathtowebsite-Variable in line 1.
$pathtowebsite = "C:/path/to/my/website" Set-Location -Path $pathtowebsite $message = Read-Host "Enter Comment Message" $theme = Read-Host "Enter Theme Name : Tailwind (default) or Blonde" Write-Host "--- website Repository ---" git add -A if ([string]::IsNullOrEmpty($message)) { Write-Host "No message, committing changes without comment." git commit } else { git commit -m $message } git push origin Write-Host "Total code was successfully pushed to private repository." Write-Host "--- public Repository ---" # Check if theme name is provided, otherwise use default if ([string]::IsNullOrEmpty($theme)) { Write-Host "Theme Name cannot be empty, using default Tailwind" $theme = "Tailwind" } # Convert theme to lowercase and get the first character $theme = $theme.ToLower() $theme = $theme.Substring(0, 1) if ($theme -eq "b") { hugo -t Blonde } else { hugo -t tailwind } Set-Location -Path "public" git add -A if ([string]::IsNullOrEmpty($message)) { Write-Host "No message, committing changes without comment." git commit -m "No comment provided" } else { git commit -m $message } git push origin Write-Host "Public code successfully pushed." Set-Location -Path $pathtowebsite Write-Host "Job done, everything was pushed." exit To commit your changes, type:
.\gitcommit.ps1 or only
.\gitcommit Linux bash file Create a new file called gitcommit.sh in your website repository and paste the following lines. Edit the pathtowebsite-Variable in line 2.
#!/bin/bash pathtowebsite="C:/path/to/my/website" cd "$pathtowebsite" git add -A read -p "Enter Comment Message: " message read -p "Enter Theme Name : Tailwind (default) or Blonde: " theme echo "--- website Repository ---" if [ -z "$message" ]; then echo "No message, committing changes without comment." git commit -m "No comment provided" else git commit -m "$message" fi git push origin echo "Total code was successfully pushed to private repository." echo "--- public Repository ---" if [ -z "$theme" ]; then echo "Theme Name cannot be empty, using default Tailwind" theme="Tailwind" fi # Convert theme to lowercase and get the first character theme=$(echo "$theme" | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]') theme=${theme:0:1} if [ "$theme" == "b" ]; then hugo -t Blonde else hugo -t tailwind fi cd public git add -A if [ -z "$message" ]; then echo "No message, committing changes without comment." git commit else git commit -m "$message" fi git push origin echo "Public code successfully pushed." cd "$pathtowebsite" echo "Job done, everything was pushed." exit 0 To commit your changes, type:
./gitcommit.sh Step 8: Search Engine and Crawler optimization (optional) Robots.txt If you set ’enableRobotsTXT=true’ in hugo.toml, then Hugo automatically creates a ‘robots.txt’ file with the content:
User-agent: * This tells web-crawlers that it is okay, to crawl your data. Wikipedia: What is a web-crawler? If you don’t want that (which I personally prefer, but that is your own mindset of course) then you can disallow all crawling by overwriting the default. To do so, create a file called ‘robots.txt’ in the ’layouts’ folder in the private ‘website’ repository. Paste
User-agent: * Disallow: / to disallow all crawling or
User-agent: * Disallow: /contact Disallow: /about Allow: /posts to allow only partial crawling. Modify it to your liking! For more information refer to the official Hugo Documentation Search Engine Optimization SEO You can generate a sitemap, which will help your website to be better found via Google. I already added the necessary section to ‘hugo.toml’ file, so the sitemap is already created. What we now have to do is to tell Google about the sitemap. Therefore we use the Google Search Console.
In your web-browser: Open the Google Search Console https://search.google.com/search-console/about Log in with your Google Account if not already done so Select ‘Add Property’ In the pop-up type your url https://yourdomain.github.io under ‘URL-Prefix’ (not Domain) Select HTML-Tag as your url-ownership verification method and copy the line In your private ‘website’ repository. Open the file ’themes/tailwind/layouts/partials/head.html Add the copied line at the top Save your changes and push the website to github Go back to the Google Search Console website Click on the Finish button, now Google verifies that you have control over the url. If it didn’t work right away, push the website once again to git and wait a minute or two. Then try again. Click on the sitemap entry in the sidebar, fill the field behind ‘yourusername.github.io/’ with ‘sitemap.xml’. That’s it. Now feel free to play around with the Google Search Console.
Workflow This is a short summary that describes all the steps you need to do each time you want to create and publish a new article.
Open Powershell.
Use the cd command to move into your directory, where your website is stored.
Create a new content page (replace yourpostname with the actual title of your new post, but try to avoid blank spaces):
hugo new posts/yourpostname/index.md Open index.md with your favourite text editor or IDE (e.g. Visual Studio Code) and write down your world-changing thoughts.
Optional: Copy the newly created /en/posts/yourpostname/index.md to /de/posts/yourpostname/index.md and translate the text to german. Make sure to have the same values for translationKey. If you are building a multilanguage webpage, I definitely recommend that you translate every blog entry. However, it is not technically required to do so.
To test that everything looks correct, execute the following in PowerShell:
run the hugo server hugo server or
hugo server --buildDrafts if you want all drafts to be compiled as well.
open localhost:1313/ in your browser. (Every time you update content, the change will instantly be shown on localhost. ) If everything is looking good on localhost and you want to make the changes public, execute our script:
Windows ./gitcommit.ps1 Linux ./gitcommit.sh Your website should be available at yourusername.github.io.
Further reading I recommend reading through the demo page of the tailwind theme. There you can find some nice guides on how to include images/videos, enable Emoji-Support 😎 and a Markdown Syntax Guide.
They also provide a guide about how to add add LaTeX-like support (called kaTeX ), however I recommend to follow the official Hugo Math guide . Handy tip: You can verify that you are using KaTeX (and not MathJax or nothing) in the developer tools of your internet browser. Open your website, press F12 to go to the Network-tab, reload the page and you should see a katex.min.css and katex.min.js entry.
Also read this guide if you wish your website to be available under a custom domain.
Congratulations, that’s it! Have fun with your Webpage.
Dependabot alerts After a while of running the Blog, I received Github Dependabot alerts, warning me about security issues with packages specified in the package-lock.json file. To solve those security issues you should definitely asap execute
npm update to update those dependencies. After a new add, commit and push to github via the gitcommit script the security alerts should be solved.
title: How to create a blog with Hugo (full guide)

content:
Overview Why and when do we need linear regression? In a nutshell, linear regression is used to predict data that has a linear relationship. That means all the datapoints are roughly on a straight line (in 2D), resp. on a hyperplane (in 3D). Firstly, we find some expression for the linear relationship (e.g. in 2D: $y=a\cdot x + b$; where $a$ is the slope and $b$ is the y-intercept). Secondly, we use this expression to insert some data where we only know the $x$ value, but want to predict their unknown $y$ value.
Linear regression with one variable To get a feeling and understanding what linear regression is, we start with the most simple version: The 2D linear regression with one variable.
Figure 1: Some example data with a linear relationship and a regression line.
Figure 1 shows some datapoints, which are roughly linearly lined up. The expression for the blue regression line is: $y=a\cdot x + b$; where $a$ is the slope and $b$ is the y-intercept.
Find best regression line First, we find $a$ and $b$ such that some error is minimized. Most datapoints are not on exactly on the regression line. Thus the $x$-$y$-relationship of each datapoint $i$ is not simply $y_i=a\cdot x_i + b$, but actually $y_i=a\cdot x_i + b + \varepsilon _i$ where $\varepsilon _i$ is the error we want to minimize. We can rewrite the expression and get $\varepsilon_i = y_i - (ax_i+b)$ where $y_i$ is the actual value and $- (ax_i+b)$ is the predicted value.
Now, what do we actually want to achieve? We want to minimize the total error $\varepsilon$ which is some ‘compound’ of all $\varepsilon_i$.
To get $\varepsilon$ we could try one of the following:
add up all $\varepsilon_i$ -> not a good idea as positive and negative $\varepsilon_i$ cancel each other add up the absolute value of all $\varepsilon_i$ -> not that smart, because $\varepsilon$ won’t be differentiable anymore add up the squares: $\varepsilon_i^2$ -> That is the so called least squared method (aka Euclidean Norm). Perfect! Next, we compute the least squares. We want to minimize some function $f(a,b)$.
$f(a,b) = \sum_{i=1}^n \varepsilon_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-ax_i-b)^2$, where $n$ is the number of $x$-$y$-pairs we have
We take the derivatives wrt. to $a$ and $b$ and set them equal to zero.
I) $f_a = \frac{\partial f(a,b)}{\partial a} = \sum_{i=1}^n -2 (y_i-ax_i-b) x_i = 0$
II) $f_b = \frac{\partial f(a,b)}{\partial b} = \sum_{i=1}^n -2 (y_i-ax_i-b) = 0$
Then we do the math: (Note that we simply ommit the -2 first)
I) $0 =\sum y_i x_i - \sum a x_i^2 - \sum b x_i = \sum y_i x_i - a \sum x_i^2 - b\sum x_i $
II) $0 =\sum y_i - \sum a x_i - \sum b = \sum y_i - a \sum x_i - nb $
from II) $nb = \sum y_i - a \sum x_i$
II) in I) $0 =\sum y_i x_i - \sum a x_i^2 - (\frac{\sum y_i}{n} - \frac{a\sum x_i}{n})$
$\sum y_i x_i - \frac{\sum x_i\cdot \sum y_i}{n} = a (\sum x_i^2 - \frac{\sum x_i \cdot \sum x_i}{n})$
$ a = \frac{\sum x_i \sum y_i - n \sum x_iy_i}{(\sum x_i)^2 - n \sum x_i^2}$
$ b = \frac{\sum y_i - a \sum x_i}{n}$
That’s it. Now we have two expressions how we can find $a$ and $b$ of a given dataset.
Everything we need to do is to plug in all the data into the formulas for $a$ and $b$. Then we can take some unknow datapoint where we only have $x_i$, but not the corresponding $y_i$. We insert $x_i$ into our formula of $y_i = ax_i+b$ and we will get the $y_i$ value.
In case we leave 2D and want to solve problems in higher dimensions, we could do exactly the same procedure. In 3D we have $y_i = ax + bz + c$ and can solve the three resulting equalities to find some formulas for $a$, $b$ and $c$. However, the more dimensions we get, the more computation expensive this will become. Luckily, there is a smarter way, the Normal Equation of Linear Regression. We will come to that later.
Example Consider the following five datapoints: (0,1), (1,1), (2,2), (3,2), (4,3)
$ a = \frac{\sum x_i \sum y_i - n \sum x_iy_i}{(\sum x_i)^2 - n \sum x_i^2} = \frac{5\cdot(0\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 2 + 3\cdot 2 + 4\cdot 3)- (0+1+2+3+4)\cdot (1+1+2+2+3)}{5\cdot(0^2+1^2+2^2+3^2+4^2)-(0+1+2+3+4)^2} = \frac{115-90}{150-100} = \frac{25}{50} = 0.5$
$ b = \frac{\sum y_i - a \sum x_i}{n} = \frac{1+1+2+2+3-0.5\cdot(0+1+2+3+4)}{5} = \frac{9-5}{5} = 0.8$
Thus, the final equation is $y = 0.5x+0.8$
If we want to predict the y-value for $x=2$, we get: $y=0.5\cdot2+0.8 = 1.8 \not = 2$. That is correct and makes sense, as our regression line does not perfectly go through each point, but rather minimizes the overall error.
Figure 2: Our example’s datapoints with their regression line.
Assumptions The previous example worked very well. However, our data needs to fulfill specific requirements.
Linearity: A linear relationship between all $x$ and $y$ datapoints Homoscedasticity: The variance of error is independent of $x$ Normality: The data is normally distributed Independence: Each measurements/datapoint is independent from other measurements/datapoints If those four requirements are fulfilled, we can use linear regression.
Linearization of non-linear models Some real-life data may be not linear. E.g. the growth of bacteria-population is exponentially distributed. There are some tricks, how we can linearize some non-linear data, s.t. linear regression is applicable.
The following table shows some of the more common models and their linearization.
Name of Model Formula of Model Linearized Model Explanation/Hint Exponnential distribution $y=a\cdot exp(bx)$ $ln(y) = bx + ln(a)$ Take natural logarithm on both sides of the equation. Power Model $y=ax^b$ $ln(y) = ln(a) + bln(x)$ Take natural logaritm. Saturation Model $y = \frac{ax}{b-x}$ $\frac{1}{y} = \frac{1}{x} \frac{b}{a} + \frac{1}{a}$ Reverse fractions, then simplify Polynomial Regression $y = a_nx^n+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}+a_{n-2}x^{n-2}+...+a_1x^1+a_0$ Not so simple, see below. Figure 2: Our example’s datapoints with their regression line.
The polynomial Regression needs a little bit more text.
Polynomial Regression of order m=2 (quadratic) We have $y = a_2x^2+a_1x^1+a_0 + \varepsilon$ and the next steps are actually pretty close to what we did above to find the best regression line.
The sum of the squares of the errors $S = \sum_i \big((y_i-a-bx_i-cx_i^2)^2\big)$. Differentiating wrt. $a$, $b$ and $c$ we get:
I) $an + bS_1+cS_2 = S_5$
II) $aS_1 + bS_2+cS_3 = S_6$
III) $aS_2 + bS_3+cS_4 = S_7$
where $S_1 = \sum_i x_i$
$S_2 = \sum_i x_i^2$
$S_3 = \sum_i x_i^3$
$S_4 = \sum_i x_i^4$
$S_5 = \sum_i y_i$
$S_6 = \sum_i x_iy_i$
$S_7 = \sum_i x_i^2y_i$
Do you see the pattern?
Polynomial regression for any order m If yes, then the following might be interesting to you. It is (with a small modification) taken from the Wikipedia-Article about Polynomial Regression . Solving this linear system for the $a_i$’s will work for any polynomial power $m$.
$ \left[ \begin{matrix} \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^0 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^1 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 & \cdots & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^m \\ \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^1 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^3 & \cdots & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^{m+1} \\ \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^3 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^4 & \cdots & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^{m+2} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^m & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^{m+1} & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^{m+2} & \cdots & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^{2m} \\ \end{matrix} \right] $ $ \left[ \begin{matrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_m \\ \end{matrix} \right] $ $=\left[ \begin{matrix} \sum_{i=0}^n y_i x_i^0 \\ \sum_{i=0}^n y_i x_i^1 \\ \sum_{i=0}^n y_i x_i^2 \\ \vdots \\ \sum_{i=0}^n y_i x_i^m \\ \end{matrix} \right] $
Normal Equation of Linear Regression So far we mainly talked about linear regression in 2D and realized that doing linear regression in higher dimensions is analog to 2D, but very includes a lot of work. Luckily, as already mentioned, there is an easier way to do linear regression for higher dimensions.
Normal Equation of Linear Regression in 2D Derivation $\vec u=\vec b-x\vec a$
min $\frac12 (\vec b - x\vec a)^2$
$\frac{d}{d\vec a} \frac12 (\vec b - x\vec a)^2 = (\vec b - x\vec a)\vec a = 0$
$ \vec b\cdot\vec a - x\vec a\cdot\vec a = 0$
$x = \frac{\vec b\cdot\vec a}{\vec a\cdot\vec a}$
To get the general solution for the least squares method in n-dimensions we do the following steps:
$\varepsilon(x) = (\vec a x - \vec b)^2 = (\vec a x - \vec b)^T (\vec a x - \vec b) = (\vec a x)^T (\vec a x) - (\vec a x)^T \vec b - \vec a x \vec b^T + \vec b^T\vec b = x^2\vec a^T\vec a - x(\vec a^T\vec b+\vec b^T\vec a)+\vec b^T\vec b = x^2\vec a^T\vec a - 2x\vec a^T\vec b+\vec b^T\vec b$
$\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x} = 2x\vec a^T\vec a -2\vec a^T\vec b = 0$
$x\vec a^T\vec a -\vec a^T\vec b = 0$
Th gives the general solution for the least squares method in n-dimensions:
$$x=\frac{\vec a^T\vec b}{\vec a^T\vec a}$$
Example Let $\vec a = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \\\end{pmatrix}$ and $\vec b = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \\\end{pmatrix}$ then $\varepsilon (x) = (2x-1)^2+(3x-2)^2+(4x-3)^2 \rightarrow \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x} = 2(2x-1)+2(3x-2)+2(4x-3) = 0 \Leftrightarrow (2x-1)+(3x-2)+(4x-3) = 0$. This equation can be solved for $x$ and you will get $x=\frac23$.
Normal Equation of Linear Regression in higher dimensions Derivation Example This content will follow, when I have time to continue.

Hello,
You do not know what OpenPGP is? Then read the text below .
Download my public key as a zip file and import it into your encryption programme.
↓ OpenPGP Public Key What is OpenPGP? Unfortunately, our emails are currently not encrypted by default. It is therefore very likely that government agencies (NSA and co.) and hackers can read our emails. This is not a desirable state of affairs. Encrypting email content with OpenPGP (PGP stands for ‘Pretty Good Privacy’) provides a remedy. A public key, with which the message is encrypted, and a private (secret) key, with which the message can be decrypted, are generated. As the name suggests, the public key can be made accessible to everyone, because how else can others write to you in encrypted form? The private key, on the other hand, must be kept safe so that it does not fall into the wrong hands.
You can find more information on the OpenPGP website or read their wikipedia article .
How to use OpenPGP Personally, I use Thunderbird to encrypt emails, but there are a whole range of options. It’s not too difficult to set up.
Some of the most popular solutions are mentioned below, but there are many more .
Windows Outlook Outlook does not support OpenPGP natively. But you can install the (open-source) plug-in Gpg4win .
Thunderbird In Thunderbird, simply go to Settings>Account Settings>YOUR EMAIL ACCOUNT>End-To-End-Encryption. There you can import your private key or generate a new one.
Linux The easiest way is to use Thunderbird.
Ubuntu:
sudo apt install thunderbird Arch:
sudo pacman -S thunderbird Mobile devices All the common mobile apps such as Outlook, Apple’s mail client, Gmail, Blue Mail etc. do not support OpenPGP. But no worries, there are excellent alternatives.
Android I am personally a big fan of K-9 Mail. To use OpenPGP you need to install the free open-source apps K-9 Mail and OpenKeychain. First, you import your private key into OpenKeychain, then you go to the Account Settings of K-9 Mail. There you choose your Email-Account, click on “Cryptography” and select OpenKeychain as your OpenPGP Provider. That’s it.
iOS Propably the free and open-source app PGPro is the best choice. However, I never tested this app, so I can’t give any recommendation.
Authentification You can also let the governikus GmbH (owned by BSI Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik, the german ministry for safety in information technology) authenticate your public keys. Follow the steps on their website .
title: OpenPGP

Introduction This article gives a quick overview over the feedforward phase of a Neural Network.
Nodes A Neural Network is made of many Nodes that are connected to each other. Each Node itself is a very basic unit that does only do three things.
Multiply each input with a weight and sum all inputs up. $y_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^m w_i\cdot x_i$, where $m$ is the number of inputs. Process the single, scalar input value by some activation function $y_{out}=f(y_{in})$ where $y_{in}$ is the result from step 1 and $y_{out}$ is the result from step 1 after being applied to the activation function. Output the result $y_{out}$ to the next node So, a single node pretty much looks like this: Figure 1: A single Node with its weights
Here:
$y_{in} = w_1x_1 + w_2x_2 + w_0x_0$ (where each x_i and w_i is a scalar of course) $y_{out} = f(y_{in})$ Bias One special input we have to talk about is the bias $x_0$. Per definition $x_0 = 1$ always. The bias is needed as an offset. You can think of it as the multi-dimensional equivalent to the y-intercept in 2D. Without a y-intercept each function in 2D has to go through the center of the coordinate system. The same applies for our Node: With this offset, the Neural Network can e.g. classify data even if their optimal separation hyperplane does not go through the origin.
Example Let’s say we have the following Node and the activation function $f(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > 0\\ 0 & \text{else } \end{cases}$ Figure 2: Example of single Node with its weights
$y_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^m w_i\cdot x_i = w_1x_1 + w_2x_2 + w_0x_0 = 1\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 1 = 2+8+3 = 13$ $y_{out} = f(y_{in})$. We have $y_{in} = 13 > 0$ so $y_{out} = 1$ The node outputs $y_{out} = 1$ Activation functions In the previous example we used the so called binary step function as our activation function. However, there are a few more I would like to mention here.
Function Formula Description Identify function $f(x)=x$ Very basic Binary step function $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\0 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ Bipolar step function $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\-1 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ Binary Sigmoid $f(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}} = \frac{e^x}{e^x+e^{-x}}$ Bipolar Sigmiod $f(x) = \frac{2}{1+e^{-x}}+1 = \frac{e^x-e^{-x}}{e^x+e^{-x}} = tanh(x)$ ReLu (Rectified Linear Unit ) $f(x) = max(x,0)$ Softplus $f(x) = ln(1+e^x)$ Leaky ReLu $f(x)=\begin{cases}x & \text{if } x > 0\\\alpha x, \;\alpha\text{ small} & \text{else }\end{cases}$ Softmax $f(x_i)=\frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}$ Figure 3: The Activation Functions
Architecture Now we have seen how each node works. He gets some information, processes this information and outputs the result. That is not very impressing so far, but many of those nodes are combined in a specific Architecture, then they can perform powerful tasks.
Feedforward The following plot shows a very basic Neural Network, that has 3 layers. One input layer $x$, one hidden layer $z$ and one output layer $y$. Between each consecutive layer we have connections $w$ and $v$. For the moment, we don’t care about how the weights were computed, as this is done by the training process. The feedforward part is used for predicting, the output for a given input. If we are testing the Neural Network, we use the feedforward method, as shown in this article. In the training part, we also use feedforward, but that is a topic for another article: Backpropgation
Figure 4: Neural Network with 3 layers
Each connection $w_{ij}$ is the weight from node $x_i$ to $z_j$. So, for example $w_{01}$ is the weight from $x_0$ (the bias!) to $z_1$.
Steps of Feedforward For each layer (starting at the first hidden layer) For each node in the layer $z_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^m w_i\cdot x_i$, where $m$ is the number of inputs. Resp. $y_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^p v_i\cdot z_i$, where $p$ is the number of inputs. Apply the activation function $z_{out}=f(z_{in})$, resp. $y_{out}=f(y_{in})$ Output the result $z_{out}$ to the next layer, resp. $y_{out}$ to the output End End Example Let’s consider the following Neural Network. Each node has the binary step function $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\0 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ applied to it.
Figure 5: Example of a Neural Network with 3 layers
We begin with the hidden layer First we calculate all $z_{in}$ (Note that $x_0$ and $z_0$ are the biases, so $x_0=1$ and $z_0=1$ always. ) $z_{1,in} = w_{01}x_0+w_{11}x_1+w_{21}x_2 = 1\cdot 1+2\cdot 2+(-0.5)\cdot 3 = 3.5$ $z_{2,in} = w_{02}x_0+w_{12}x_1+w_{22}x_2 = 0\cdot 1+3\cdot 2+(-2)\cdot 3 = 0$ $z_{3,in} = w_{03}x_0+w_{13}x_1+w_{23}x_2 = 4\cdot 1+(-4)\cdot 2+1\cdot 3 = -1$ Apply the activation function $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\0 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ $z_{1,out} = f(z_{1,in}) = f(3.5)= 1$ $z_{2,out} = f(z_{2,in}) = f(0) = 0$ $z_{3,out} = f(z_{3,in}) = f(-1)= 0$ We repeat the same for the output layer Calculate all $y_{in}$ $y_{1,in} = v_{01}\cdot z_0+v_{11}\cdot z_{1,out}+v_{21}\cdot z_{2,out}+v_{31}\cdot z_{3,out} =0.25\cdot 1+2\cdot 1+1.5\cdot 0+0.75\cdot 0 = 2.25$ $y_{2,in} = v_{02}\cdot z_0+v_{12}\cdot z_{1,out}+v_{22}\cdot z_{2,out}+v_{32}\cdot z_{3,out} =(-1)\cdot 1 + 0.5\cdot 1+1\cdot 0 + 1.25\cdot 0 = -0.5$ Apply the activation function $y_{1,out} = f(y_{1,in}) = f(2.25)= 1$ $y_{2,out} = f(y_{2,in}) = f(-0.5) = 0$ Tadaa! This is our final output. Our net predicts $y_1 = 1$ and $y_2 = 0$
Feedforward in Matrix Calculus As you already may have guessed the whole process we did before can be simplified by using Matrix Calculus.
Figure 6: Simplified Neural Network to demonstrate the Matrix Calculus
The steps are pretty much the same:
For each layer (starting at the first hidden layer) $z_{in} = W^T x$ Apply the activation function $z_{out}=f(z_{in})$ Output the result $z_{out}$ End So, the little bit more detailed steps for our Neural Network in Figure 6 are:
$z_{in} = W^Tx$ $z_{out} = f(z_{in})$ $y_{in} = V^Tz$ $y_{out} = f(y_{in})$ As you can see this looks way more handsome than taking the sum over all input value multipiled with their weights. Dimensions Hereby we have to consider the dimensions of the different layers. If $x\in \R^{m\times 1}$, $z\in \R^{p\times 1}$ and $y\in \R^{n\times 1}$ then $W\in \R^{m\times p-1}$ and $V\in \R^{p\times n}$.
Example Let’s do the example from Figure 5 another time. We use the identity activation function again, but do the math in matrix form this time.
First of all, let’s determine $W$ and $V$.
$W=\begin{bmatrix} w_{01} & w_{02} & w_{03} \\ w_{11} & w_{12} & w_{13} \\ w_{21} & w_{22} & w_{23} \end{bmatrix} \text{, } V=\begin{bmatrix} v_{01} & v_{02} \\ v_{11} & v_{12} \\ v_{21} & v_{22} \\ v_{31} & v_{32} \end{bmatrix}$
Just for clarity, we check the dimensions: $x\in \R^{3\times 1}$, $z\in \R^{4\times 1}$ and $y\in \R^{2\times 1}$ then $W\in \R^{3\times 3}$ and $V\in \R^{4\times 2}$.
If we fill in the values, then we get $W=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & -4 \\ -0.5 & -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{, } V=\begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & -1 \\ 2 & 0.5 \\ 1.5 & 1 \\ 0.75 & 1.25 \end{bmatrix}$
Let’s do the steps:
$z_{in} = W^T x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & -4 \\ -0.5 & -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1\\ 2\\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.5 \\ 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$
$z_{out}=f(z_{in})= \begin{bmatrix} f(z_{1,in})\\ f(z_{2,in})\\ f(z_{3,in}) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f(3.5)\\ f(0)\\ f(-1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1\\ 0\\ 0\\ \end{bmatrix} $
$y_{in} = V^T z_{out} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 2 & 1.5 & 0.75 \\ -1 & 0.5 & 1 & 1.25 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1\\1\\0\\0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.25\\-0.5 \end{bmatrix} $
Note that in step 3 we used the same $z_{out}$ as in step 2, but added the bias $z_0$ at the “top”.
$y_{out} = f(y_{in}) = \begin{bmatrix} f(y_{1,in})\\ f(y_{2,in})\\ \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f(2.25)\\ f(-0.5)\\ \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1\\ 0\\ \end{bmatrix} $
This is the same result as if we had used the normal way, without matrix calculations.

Soweit ich das verstanden habe gilt mit Hinblick auf selbstgebaute Drohnen folgendes, je nachdem welchen Drohnenführeschein man hat. Die offiziellen Informationen gibt es auf: https://lba-openuav.de/onlinekurs/lehrmaterial/luftrecht-und-sicherheit/uas-klassifizierungen/ Ohne Drohnenführerschein:
Nur gekaufte Drohnen unter 250g Fliegen auch in Wohngebieten erlaubt vereinzelt über Menschen fliegen erlaubt Überfliegen von Menschenansammlungen (generell) verboten -> Für selbstgebaute Drohnen braucht man also zwingend mindestens den kleinen Drohnenführerschein.
Mit dem kleinen Drohnenführerschein: Selbstgebaute Drohnen dürfen in der Kategorie A3 fliegen, d.h.
für alle Drohnen zwischen 250g und 25kg Flüge nur auf freiem Feld Abstand von mindestens 150m zu Wohngebieten horizontaler Abstand zu unbeteiligten Personen min. 30m min. 1:1 Abstand-Flughöhe Verhältnis und 2s Regel zu Wohngebieten, Industrie, Erholungsgebieten, etc. Mit dem großen (A2) Drohnenführeschein: Selbstgebaute Drohen dürfen auch in der Kategorie A2 fliegen, d.h.
Drohnen bis 4kg Flug über Wohngebieten erlaubt in langsamen Flug ($<3\frac{m}{s}$): Abstand von 5m zu unbeteiligten Personen in schnellerem Flug: Abstand von 30m zu unbeteiligten Personen Generell gelten hier die Anforderungen der offenen Kategorie (v.a. Flug nur auf Sicht, AGL < 120m und nicht über Menschenansammlungen)
Ergo, wenn man den großen Drohnenführerschein hat, darf man entweder in der Kategorie A2 oder A3 fliegen. Z.B. eine 3kg schwere Drohnen auch in Wohngebieten, eine 5kg schwere Drohne aber nur auf dem Feld (A2).
Für den großen Drohnenführeschein muss der Fernpilot zur Qualifikation:
den Online-Kurs und die Online-Theorieprüfung bestehen (= kleiner Drohnenführerschein) praktisches Training in Eigenregie Theorieprüfung vor Ort Luftfahrtverwaltung Begriffe EASA: European Union Aviation Safety Agency -> EU weit (plus Schweiz und Norwegen, nicht Großbritannien) in Deutschland: Luftfahrtbundesamt (LBA) in Österreich: Austrocontrol Nach Unfällen (und anderen Begebenheiten) Störungsmeldungen unter: https://onl-meldung.bfu-web.de/onlmeldung/ Diese Meldung ist nur zur Information der Behörden. Es ist der Behörde nicht gestattet basierend auf dieser Meldung den Meldenden zu verfolgen und zu sanktionieren.
Gebiete Die EU-Mitgliedsländer sind dazu anghalten den Luftraum in verschiedene Zonen einzuteilen. In manchen Zonen liegen Beschränkungen/Flugverboten vor, z.B. um Flughäfen, militärische Einrichtungen, …
Daher ist es zwingend nötig, dass man vor jedem Flug nachschaut, ob Beschränkungen vorliegen. Hierzu gibt es Internetseiten (einfach googlen) oder auch Apps.
Österreich: Austro Control Dronespace (Google Play )
Deutschland: Droniq App (Google Play )
Faktor Mensch Alkohol für der Betrieb von UAS: unter 0,2 Promille, kein Alkohol ab 8h vor dem Flug (Richtwerte) keine Müdigkeit

content:
Hallo,
Wenn du dich fragst, was OpenPGP eigentlich ist, dann lese unten weiter.
Lade meinen öffentlichen Schlüssel als Zip-Datei herunter und importiere ihn in dein Verschlüsselungsprogramm.
↓ OpenPGP Public Key Was ist OpenPGP? Leider sind unsere E-Mails standardmäßig nicht verschlüsselt. Es ist daher sehr wahrscheinlich, dass staatliche Stellen (NSA und Co.) und Hacker unsere E-Mails lesen können. Dies ist kein wünschenswerter Zustand. Die Verschlüsselung von E-Mail-Inhalten mit OpenPGP (PGP steht für ‘Pretty Good Privacy’, dt. “Ziemlich gute Verschlüsselung”) schafft Abhilfe. Es werden ein öffentlicher Schlüssel, mit dem die Nachricht verschlüsselt wird, und ein privater (geheimer) Schlüssel, mit dem die Nachricht entschlüsselt werden kann, erzeugt. Wie der Name schon sagt, kann der öffentliche Schlüssel jedem zugänglich gemacht werden, denn wie sonst könnten andere dir verschlüsselt schreiben? Der private Schlüssel hingegen muss sicher aufbewahrt werden, damit er nicht in die falschen Hände gerät.
Weitere Informationen gibt es auf der OpenPGP-Website oder im Wikipedia-Artikel .
Wie verwende ich OpenPGP? Ich persönlich benutze Thunderbird, um E-Mails zu ver- und entschlüsseln, aber es gibt eine ganze Reihe von Möglichkeiten. Es ist nicht allzu schwierig, es einzurichten.
Einige der populärsten Lösungen sind unten aufgeführt, aber es gibt viele weitere .
Windows Outlook Outlook unterstützt OpenPGP nicht von Haus aus. Das (Open-Source-)Plugin Gpg4win rüstet diese Funktion aber nach.
Thunderbird Die notwendigen Einstellungen und Optionen sind in Thunderbird unter Einstellungen>Kontoeinstellungen>DEIN EMAIL KONTO>Ende-zu-Ende-Verschlüsselung. Dort können man den privaten Schlüssel importieren oder ein neues Schlüsselpaar (einen geheimen und einen öffentlichen) generieren.
Linux Der einfachste Weg ist Thunderbird zu verwenden.
Ubuntu:
sudo apt install thunderbird Arch:
sudo pacman -S thunderbird Mobile devices Die üblichen Verdächtigen, wie die Smartphone-Apps Outlook, Apples Mailapp, Gmail, Blue Mail etc. unterstützen leider kein OpenPGP. Aber das ist kein Problem, denn es gibt einige sehr gute Alternativen.
Android Ich persönlich nutze K-9 Mail. Um OpenPGP verwenden zu können, muss man die kostenlosen open-source Apps K-9 Mail und OpenKeychain installieren. Zuerst importiert man den privaten Schlüssel in OpenKeychain, dann wählt man in den Account-Settings von K-9 Mail unter “Kryptographie” des jeweiligen Email-Kontos OpenKeychain as OpenPGP Provider aus. Und los geht’s.
iOS Die vermutlich beste Wahl ist die kostenlose open-source App PGPro. Allerdings habe ich sie noch nie getestet und kann daher keine genaue Empfehlung abgeben.
Authentifizierung Man kann sich den öffentlichen Schlüssel auch von der governikus GmbH (Eigentum des BSI Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik) authentifizieren lassen. Dazu einfach den Schritten auf ihrer Website folgen.
title: OpenPGP

content: Dieser Artikel wird demnächst die Backpropagationsphase eines neuronalen Netzes in Matrixschreibweise erklären.
title: Backpropagation in Matrixschreibweise

content:
Einleitung Dieser Artikel gibt einen kurzen Überblick über die Feedforward-Phase eines Neuronalen Netzes.
Disclaimer: Der Artikel wurde teilweise unter Verwendung von deepl.com aus dem englischen Original übersetzt.
Knoten Ein Neuronales Netz besteht aus vielen Knoten, die miteinander verbunden sind. Jeder Knoten selbst ist eine sehr einfache Einheit, die nur drei Dinge tut.
Jede Eingabe mit einem Gewicht multiplizieren und alle Eingaben aufsummieren. $y_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^m w_i\cdot x_i$, wobei $m$ die Anzahl der Eingänge ist. Verarbeite den einzelnen, skalaren Eingangswert durch eine Aktivierungsfunktion $y_{out}=f(y_{in})$, wobei $y_{in}$ das Ergebnis aus Schritt 1 und $y_{out}$ das Ergebnis aus Schritt 1 nach Anwendung auf die Aktivierungsfunktion ist. Ausgabe des Ergebnisses $y_{out}$ an den nächsten Knoten Ein einzelner Knoten sieht also in etwa so aus: Figure 1: A single Node with its weights
Hier:
$y_{in} = w_1x_1 + w_2x_2 + w_0x_0$ (wobei jedes x_i und w_i natürlich ein Skalar ist) $y_{out} = f(y_{in})$ Verzerrung Eine spezielle Eingabe, über die wir sprechen müssen, ist der Bias $x_0$. Per Definition ist $x_0 = 1$ immer. Die Vorspannung wird als Offset benötigt. Man kann ihn sich als das mehrdimensionale Äquivalent zum y-Achsenabschnitt in 2D vorstellen. Ohne einen y-Achsenabschnitt muss jede Funktion in 2D durch den Mittelpunkt des Koordinatensystems gehen. Das Gleiche gilt für unseren Knotenpunkt: Mit diesem Offset kann das Neuronale Netz z. B. Daten auch dann klassifizieren, wenn ihre optimale Trennungshyperfläche nicht durch den Ursprung geht.
Beispiel Nehmen wir an, wir haben den folgenden Knoten und die Aktivierungsfunktion $f(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > 0\\ 0 & \text{else } \end{cases}$ Abbildung 2: Beispiel eines einzelnen Knotens mit seinen Gewichten
$y_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^m w_i\cdot x_i = w_1x_1 + w_2x_2 + w_0x_0 = 1\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 1 = 2+8+3 = 13$ $y_{out} = f(y_{in})$. Wir haben $y_{in} = 13 > 0$ also $y_{out} = 1$ Der Knoten gibt $y_{out} = 1$ Aktivierungsfunktionen Im vorigen Beispiel haben wir die so genannte binäre Schrittfunktion als Aktivierungsfunktion verwendet. Es gibt jedoch noch ein paar weitere, die ich hier erwähnen möchte.
Funktion Formel Beschreibung Identifizierungsfunktion $f(x)=x$ Sehr einfach Binäre Schrittfunktion $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\0 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ Bipolare Schrittfunktion $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\-1 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ Binäres Sigmoid $f(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}} = \frac{e^x}{e^x+e^{-x}}$ Bipolar Sigmiod $f(x) = \frac{2}{1+e^{-x}}+1 = \frac{e^x-e^{-x}}{e^x+e^{-x}} = tanh(x)$ ReLu (Rectified Linear Unit ) $f(x) = max(x,0)$ Softplus $f(x) = ln(1+e^x)$ Leaky ReLu $f(x)=\begin{cases}x & \text{if } x > 0\\\\alpha x, \;\alpha\text{ small} & \text{else }\end{cases}$ Softmax $f(x_i)=\frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}$ Abbildung 3: Die Aktivierungsfunktionen
Architektur Jetzt haben wir gesehen, wie jeder Knoten funktioniert. Er erhält einige Informationen, verarbeitet diese Informationen und gibt das Ergebnis aus. Das ist bis jetzt nicht sehr beeindruckend, aber viele dieser Knoten sind in einer bestimmten Architektur kombiniert, dann können sie mächtige Aufgaben erfüllen.
Feedforward Das folgende Diagramm zeigt ein sehr einfaches Neuronales Netz, das aus 3 Schichten besteht. Eine Eingabeschicht $x$, eine versteckte Schicht $z$ und eine Ausgabeschicht $y$. Zwischen den aufeinanderfolgenden Schichten gibt es die Verbindungen $w$ und $v$. Im Moment interessiert es uns nicht, wie die Gewichte berechnet wurden, da dies durch den Trainingsprozess geschieht. Der Feedforward-Teil wird für die Vorhersage der Ausgabe für eine gegebene Eingabe verwendet. Wenn wir das neuronale Netz testen, verwenden wir die Feedforward-Methode, wie in diesem Artikel gezeigt. Im Trainingsteil verwenden wir ebenfalls die Feedforward-Methode, aber das ist ein Thema für einen anderen Artikel: Backpropagation Abbildung 4: Neuronales Netz mit 3 Schichten
Jede Verbindung $w_{ij}$ ist das Gewicht von Knoten $x_i$ zu $z_j$. So ist z.B. $w_{01}$ das Gewicht von $x_0$ (die Vorspannung!) zu $z_1$.
Schritte der Vorwärtskopplung Für jede Schicht (beginnend mit der ersten versteckten Schicht) Für jeden Knoten in der Schicht $z_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^m w_i\cdot x_i$, wobei $m$ die Anzahl der Eingänge ist. Resp. $y_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^p v_i\cdot z_i$, wobei $p$ die Anzahl der Eingänge ist. Wenden Sie die Aktivierungsfunktion $z_{out}=f(z_{in})$ bzw. $y_{out}=f(y_{in})$ an. Ausgabe des Ergebnisses $z_{out}$ an die nächste Schicht, bzw. $y_{out}$ an den Ausgang Ende Ende Beispiel Betrachten wir das folgende Neuronale Netz. Auf jeden Knoten wird die binäre Schrittfunktion $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\0 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ auf ihn angewendet.
Abbildung 5: Beispiel für ein neuronales Netz mit 3 Schichten
Wir beginnen mit der versteckten Schicht
Zuerst berechnen wir alle $z_{in}$ (Man beachte, dass $x_0$ und $z_0$ die Biases sind, also $x_0=1$ und $z_0=1$ immer. ) $z_{1,in} = w_{01}x_0+w_{11}x_1+w_{21}x_2 = 1\cdot 1+2\cdot 2+(-0.5)\cdot 3 = 3.5$ $z_{2,in} = w_{02}x_0+w_{12}x_1+w_{22}x_2 = 0\cdot 1+3\cdot 2+(-2)\cdot 3 = 0$ $z_{3,in} = w_{03}x_0+w_{13}x_1+w_{23}x_2 = 4\cdot 1+(-4)\cdot 2+1\cdot 3 = -1$ Wenden Sie die Aktivierungsfunktion $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\0 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ $z_{1,out} = f(z_{1,in}) = f(3.5)= 1$ $z_{2,out} = f(z_{2,in}) = f(0) = 0$ $z_{3,out} = f(z_{3,in}) = f(-1)= 0$ Wir wiederholen das Gleiche für die Ausgabeschicht
Berechnen Sie alle $y_{in}$ $y_{1,in} = v_{01}\cdot z_0+v_{11}\cdot z_{1,out}+v_{21}\cdot z_{2,out}+v_{31}\cdot z_{3,out} =0.25\cdot 1+2\cdot 1+1.5\cdot 0+0.75\cdot 0 = 2.25$ $y_{2,in} = v_{02}\cdot z_0+v_{12}\cdot z_{1,out}+v_{22}\cdot z_{2,out}+v_{32}\cdot z_{3,out} =(-1)\cdot 1 + 0.5\cdot 1+1\cdot 0 + 1.25\cdot 0 = -0.5$ Wenden Sie die Aktivierungsfunktion an $y_{1,out} = f(y_{1,in}) = f(2.25)= 1$ $y_{2,out} = f(y_{2,in}) = f(-0.5) = 0$ Tadaa! Dies ist unsere endgültige Ausgabe. Unser Netz sagt $y_1 = 1$ und $y_2 = 0$ voraus.
Feedforward in der Matrixberechnung Wie Sie vielleicht schon erraten haben, kann der gesamte Prozess, den wir zuvor durchgeführt haben, durch die Verwendung der Matrixberechnung vereinfacht werden.
Abbildung 6: Vereinfachtes Neuronales Netz zur Veranschaulichung der Matrixrechnung
Die Schritte sind im Wesentlichen die gleichen:
Für jede Schicht (beginnend mit der ersten versteckten Schicht)1. $z_{in} = W^T x$ 2. Anwenden der Aktivierungsfunktion $z_{out}=f(z_{in})$ 3. Ausgabe des Ergebnisses $z_{out}$ Ende Die etwas detaillierteren Schritte für unser Neuronales Netzwerk in Abbildung 6 sind also:
$z_{in} = W^Tx$ $z_{out} = f(z_{in})$ $y_{in} = V^Tz$ $y_{out} = f(y_{in})$ Wie Sie sehen, sieht das viel ansehnlicher aus als die Summe aller Eingabewerte multipliziert mit ihren Gewichten. Abmessungen Dabei müssen wir die Dimensionen der verschiedenen Schichten berücksichtigen. Wenn $x\in \R^{m\times 1}$, $z\in \R^{p\times 1}$ und $y\in \R^{n\times 1}$ dann $W\in \R^{m\times p-1}$ und $V\in \R^{p\times n}$.
Beispiel Führen wir das Beispiel aus [Abbildung 5] (/de/posts/#NNExample) noch einmal durch. Wir verwenden wieder die Identitäts-Aktivierungsfunktion, rechnen aber diesmal in Matrixform.
Bestimmen wir zunächst $W$ und $V$.
$W=\begin{bmatrix} w_{01} & w_{02} & w_{03} \\ w_{11} & w_{12} & w_{13} \\ w_{21} & w_{22} & w_{23} \end{bmatrix} \text{, } V=\begin{bmatrix} v_{01} & v_{02} \\ v_{11} & v_{12} \\ v_{21} & v_{22} \\ v_{31} & v_{32} \end{bmatrix}$
Zur Verifizierung überprüfen wir die Dimensionen: $x\in \R^{3\mal 1}$, $z\in \R^{4\mal 1}$ und $y\in \R^{2\mal 1}$ dann $W\in \R^{3\mal 3}$ und $V\in \R^{4\mal 2}$.
Wenn wir die Werte ausfüllen, erhalten wir
$W=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & -4 \\ -0.5 & -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{, } V=\begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & -1 \\ 2 & 0.5 \\ 1.5 & 1 \\ 0.75 & 1.25 \end{bmatrix}$
Let’s do the steps:
$z_{in} = W^T x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & -4 \\ -0.5 & -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1\\ 2\\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.5 \\ 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$
$z_{out}=f(z_{in})= \begin{bmatrix} f(z_{1,in})\\ f(z_{2,in})\\ f(z_{3,in}) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f(3.5)\\ f(0)\\ f(-1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1\\ 0\\ 0\\ \end{bmatrix} $
$y_{in} = V^T z_{out} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 2 & 1.5 & 0.75 \\ -1 & 0.5 & 1 & 1.25 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1\\1\\0\\0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.25\\-0.5 \end{bmatrix} $ Man beachte, dass wir in Schritt 3 die gleichen $z_{out}$ wie in Schritt 2 verwendet haben, aber die Verzerrung $z_0$ am „oberen“ Ende hinzugefügt haben.
$y_{out} = f(y_{in}) = \begin{bmatrix} f(y_{1,in})\\ f(y_{2,in})\\ \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f(2.25)\\ f(-0.5)\\ \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1\\ 0\\ \end{bmatrix} $
Dies ist das gleiche Ergebnis, als wenn wir die normale Methode ohne Matrixen verwendet hätten.
title: Feedforward Phase

content:
Einleitung Dieser Artikel erklärt die Backpropagation eines Neuronalen Netzes in normaler Notation. Wenn Sie wissen wollen, wie die Backpropagation in Matrixform erfolgt, lesen Sie diesen Artikel: Backpropagation in Matrixschreibweise Lernprozess Nehmen wir an, wir haben ein neuronales Netz mit 2 Schichten, wie unten gezeigt. Wir wollen das Netz so trainieren, dass die Trainingsdaten $x$ korrekt zu den erwarteten Label $t$ führen. Für das Training haben wir einen Trainingsdatensatz, der viele $x,t$ Paare enthält. Jedes dieser Paare wird verwendet, um das Netz so zu trainieren, dass der Fehler zwischen der Vorhersage des Netzes $y$ und dem tatsächlich richtigen Wert $t$ minimiert wird. Wenn man sich das Netz als eine Black-Box-Funktion $y=f(x)$ vorstellt, bedeutet dies, dass man eine Funktion $f(x)$ finden möchte, die den Fehler zwischen $y$ und $t$ minimiert. Ein kleiner Fehler bedeutet, dass die Vorhersage nahe am tatsächlichen Wert liegt.
Beispiel: Abbildung eines neuronalen Netzes mit 2 Schichten
Das Training dieses Neuronalen Netzes besteht also im Wesentlichen darin, die besten Gewichte zu finden, die Ausgaben $y=f(x)$ erzeugen, die so nahe wie möglich am korrekten Zielwert $t$ liegen. Insbesondere auch für zuvor nicht gesehene Daten.
Trainingsalgorithmus Nachfolgend ein Pseudocode, der den groben Trainingsprozess zeigt.
Schleife über Epochen Schleife über alle Trainingsdaten 1. Feedforward Phase 2. Vergleich von y und t und Ermittlung des Fehlers 3. Backpropagation des Fehlers Schleife beenden Schleife beenden Die Feedforward-Phase wird in dem Artikel über die Feedforward-Phase erklärt. Der Fehler kann mit Hilfe der Euklidischen Norm berechnet werden: $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_i (t_i-y_i)^2$ wobei in unserem Beispiel $i = 1,2$. Jeder Ausgangsknoten hat einen Fehler von $E_1 = \frac{1}{2}(t_i-y_i)^2$, z.B. hat Knoten 1 $E_1 = \frac{1}{2}(t_1-y_1)^2$. Sie fragen sich vielleicht, woher das $\frac{1}{2}$ kommt. Der Grund ist einfach: Wir wollen die Faktoren bei der Ableitung $\frac{\partial E}{\partial y}$ zum Verschwinden bringen. Die Ableitung ist gegeben durch: $\frac{\partial E_i}{\partial y_i} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot (t_i-y_i)\cdot(-1) = -(t_i-y_i)$ Der Prozess der Backpropagation besteht darin, die Kettenregel rückwärts anzuwenden. Im folgenden wird erklärt, was das genau bedeutet.
title: Backpropagation

This is a short summary of the essentials of the keybord layout neo-layout variant bone for faster and healtier text (and math) typing on your PC.
Windows To use it on Windows follow these steps.
Install Copy the kbdbone.dll file in System32 to C:\Windows\System32 and the one in SysWOW64 to C:\Windows\SysWOW64 respectively. Grant access rights. (On 64-bit Windows you have to copy it with a 64-bit Programm, best by the Windows Explorer)
Execute the kbdneo_win10_install.reg file, which will add the layout to the registry for you. Again, grant access.
Reboot your system
In System Settings under Time&Language/Language choose the System Language and choose Bone as your keyboard layout.
At each boot: execute reneo.exe (e.g. via Tasksheduler)
Uninstall The uninstallation is best to be performed in reversed order of the installation
Stop reneo.exe
In the System Settings, remove the layout from the list
Execute the Registry uninstall file kbd_win10_uninstall.reg
Delete both kbdneo.dll files from C:\Windows\System32 and _C:\Windows\SysWOW64_s
Reboot your computer
Linux In most Distro’s you can choose Neolayout Bone in your System Settings.
If not (or if it get’s reset at every reboot) you can use the following command:
setxkbmap -layout de -variant bone or set it back to the normal german
setxkbmap -layout de I found that it is most convenient to add aliases to my bashrc
nano ~/.bashrc add the lines (doesn’t matter where, e.g. put it in the first lines)
alias setbone=setxkbmap -layout de -variant bone alias lfshgjf=setxkbmap -layout de -variant bone alias setde=setxkbmap -layout de alias lfswf=setxkbmap -layout de Finally source the bashrc
source ~/.bashrc Learn the Layout Well, the beginning will be tough, but definitely worth it.
For the start, I recommend tipp10 which has the option to learn bone in the online-version (but not in the desktop version). That is definitely a good start for the first few days, where you have to look up every second character.
Afterwards you can set the keymapping of the first layer as your Desktop background image, so you can always look characters up.
Good luck and stay with it!
title: Use Neoylayout

content:
What is OOBE? OOBE is the Windows Out of Box Experience, that you have to click through after a fresh installation of Windows on your Computer. There you can set the system language, keyboard layout, connect to the internet and
Problem 1: Windows wants to force you to use a Microsoft Account Microsoft wants you to use a Microsoft Account that is connected to their Cloud - to use all the amazing online Microsoft Features (and conveniently collect your data. Mpf). But instead of adding a Button “Use local account instead” they lay stones in your path. What a surprise! Solution 1 helps
Problem 2: You don’t have a internet connection at hand If you don’t have an Internet connection at hand, Solution 1 works for you as well. Note: Now you have to create a Local Account, a Microsoft Account is not possible without an Internet connection. (But you can always switch/convert to a Microsoft Account in the account settings once you have finished setting Windows up - for whatever reason you should like to do that. )
Solution 1 & 2: Install Windows with a Local Account Once booted a screen will show up to ask you to connect to your Wifi.
Instead, press Ctrl + F10 (or Fn + Ctrl + F10 if Fn always needs to be pressed for the function keys).
This will showm up a Command Prompt window. There type:
oobe \bypassnro The computer will restart automatically. Afterwards, there will be a button Continue with limited setup in the step where you should setup the wifi. Click it and continue with setting up your Local Account.
Problem 3: Bypass OOBE temporarily If you want to setup the computer with a Local Account, follow solution 1. This guide is for a different use case: You want to sell / give away your computer. You have Windows freshly installed and want to enter it (temporarily) to install drivers, updates or just see that everything is working fine. But you want that the person who gets the computer enters the OOBE and can setup everything else for themselve - with a fresh Feeling and no leftover config / wifi connection passwords from your side. In that case you can bypass OOBE.
Solution 3: Bypass OOBE temporarily Method 1 - Key combination Press the key combination Ctrl+Shift+F3. That should normally be the easiest way.
Note: Sometimes the Fn key is locked in the Bios Settings, then you would have to press Ctrl+Fn+Shift+F3.
Method 2 On the first OOBE screen, press Ctrl+F10 (or Ctrl+Fn+F10)
This will show up a Command Prompt window. There type:
cd \Windows\System32\oobe msobe.exe /bypass Restart the computer
shutdown /r /t After the reboot, Windows should directly take you to the login screen.
Method 3 - Registry Entry You can also try to set a OOBE entry in the Windows Registry.
Press Shift+F10 to open the command prompt during the Windows setup. Add a registry dword
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Setup" /v "OOBEComplete" /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f Restart your computer with
shutdown /r /t 0 On the next boot Windows should skip OOBE and log you in as a temporary administrator.
After you’re done, open Command Prompt (e.g. via the Startmenu) and execute
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Setup" /v "OOBEComplete" /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f or delete the key entirely
reg delete "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Setup" /v "OOBEComplete" /f Restart the computer and you should be directly taken back to the OOBE.
title: OOBE

content:
This post is my personal SQL Cheat Sheet, based on the SQL tutorial by W3Schools .
tl;dr SQL let’s you access and manipulate databases.
Why SQL SQL can execute queries, retrieve data, insert records, update records, delete record to/from a database as well as create/delete a database, create/delete tables in a database, create views and stored procedures in a database and finally set permissions on tables, procedures and views.
Many database programs build on SQL, but add some (proprietary) functionalities.
If you want to embedd database information via SQL on a website you use a database program such as MySQL. Besides you will need a server-side scripting language (PHP) and HTML and CSS for the frontend.
Basics Column = A Feature or observation criteria Row = A Record or observation Tipp: SQL keywords are not case sensitive
SELECT column1, column2 FROM mytable WHERE condition; SELECT * FROM mytable; select column1, column2 from mytable; Some of the most important SQL commands Command Description SELECT extracts data from a database UPDATE updates data in a database DELETE deletes data from a database INSERT INTO inserts new data into a database CREATE DATABASE creates a new database ALTER DATABASE modifies a database CREATE TABLE cretes a new table ALTER TABLE modifies a table DROP TABLE deletes a table CREATE INDEX creates an index (search key) DROP INDEX deletes an index SELECT DISCTINCT SELECT DISTINCT returns only distinct (non-duplicate) values
SELECT DISTINCT column1 FROM mytable; You can combine it with the COUNT argument, to count how many distinct entries are there, e.g. in the column Country.
SELECT COUNT (DISTINCT Country) FROM mytable; ORDER BY Use the ORDER BY statement to order the order of the rows, e.g. py ascending price.
SELECT column1, column2 FROM mytable ORDER BY column1, column2 ASC|DESC; SELECT ProductName, Price FROM mytable WHERE Price>=50 ORDER BY Price DESC, ProductName ASC; WHERE Appending WHERE you can filter the returned entities. E.g. the following example only returns records where the Country feature is Germany. (Of course the table has to have columns called ‘Country’ and ‘Revenue’. ) Use single quotes for Text / “Strings”.
SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Country='Germany'; SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Revenue > 100000; As in other programming languages, you can use
Operator Description = equal > greater than < less than >= greater than or equal <= less than or equal <> not equal != not equal (in some SQL versions) BETWEEN between a certain range LIKE search for a pattern IN to specify multiple possible values for a column AND, OR, NOT operator The WHERE clause can contain one or multiple AND, OR and NOT operators. AND and OR are rather self explaining. NOT is used with other operators to invert their result. You can combine multiple operators and use brackets for semantics.
SELECT column1, column2, FROM mytable WHERE condition1 AND (condition2 OR condition3) AND NOT condition4; SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Country='Germany' AND Revenue > 1000000; Some Examples: First, select all entries from countries that start with G (Gambia, Germany, Greece, etc.) or F (Finnland, France, Fidji, etc.) and where the revenue is greater than 100.
SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Revenue > 100 AND Country LIKE 'G%' OR Country LIKE 'F%'; SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Country IN ('Germany', 'France'); Example: All entrise with a revenue that is not between specific boundaries shall be returned.
SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Revenue NOT BETWEEN 100 AND 1000; INSERT INTO INSERT INTO is used to insert new records in a table. There are two different ways to write it:
By using the first command you can specify only specific columns, the rest is left blank (null value). INSERT INTO mytable (column1, column2, column3) VALUES (value1, value2, value3); And with this command you can enter multiple lines (here it’s three) with one call. INSERT INTO mytable VALUES (value11, value12, value13), (vaule21, value22, value23), (value31, value32, value33); NULL Value A field with a NULL is a field without a value. You can use the IS NULL and IS NOT NULL operators to check for nulls. Something like column1 == NULL does not work.
SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE column1 IS NULL; SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE column1 IS NOT NULL; UPDATE SET Use the UPDATE to modify an existing record. Be careful that the WHERE condition is exact (e.g. use some distinct ID)!
UPDATE mytable SET column1 = value1, column2 = value2 WHERE condition; DELETE Of course it is also possible to delete records again. To clear a table, just ommit the WHERE condition.
DELETE FROM mytable WHERE condition; DROP TABLE (Delete Table) To delete a table again, use
DROP TABLE mytable; SELECT TOP The SELECT TOP clause is used to specify the number of records to return. (E.g. the table has 100 records but you want just the first three returned. )
Note: This command may differ for different database systems.
SELECT TOP 3 * FROM mytable; SELECT TOP 50 PERCENT * FROM mytables; In MySQL for example the syntax is
SELECT * FROM mytable LIMIT 3; Aggregate Functions An aggregate function performs a calculation on a set of values and returns a single value. They are often used together with GROUP BY which splits the return into groups first. Aggregate functions ignore null values.
Aggregate Function Description MIN() returns the smallest value within the selected column MAX() returns the largest value within the selected column COUNT() returns the number of rows in a set SUM() retruns the total sum of a numerical column AVG() returns the average value of a numerical column MIN() and MAX() SELECT MIN(columnname) FROM mytable WHERE condition; SELECT MIN(Price) FROM mytable; SELECT MAX(Price) FROM mytable; AS When using MIN() or MAX() the returned column does not have a descriptive name, but with the AS keyword one can set a name.
SELECT MIN(Price) AS SmallestPrice FROM Products; COUNT() Use COUNT to count the number of rows that match a specific condition. If you ommit WHERE and use a column without NULLs (e.g. an ID column) this will effectively return the length of the table. Add DISTINCT to don’t count duplicates in.
SELECT COUNT(columnname) FROM mytable WHERE condition; SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT columnname) FROM mytable WHERE condition; You can even use an ALIAS to name the returned column “Number of rows”
SELECT COUNT(*) AS [Number of rows] FROM mytable; Often it is useful to combine COUNT and GROUP BY. This example will return how many entries are there for each country.
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM mytable GROUP BY Country; SUM() The SUM clause is used to sum all entries in a numeric column.
SELECT SUM(Price) AS [Total Cost] FROM mytable; SELECT OrderID, SUM(Price) AS [Cost per Order] FROM mytable GROUP BY OrderID; AVG() The AVG clause takes the average of a numeric column. You can use it e.g. to find all rows where with a value greater than the average.
SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Price > (SELECT AVG(Price) FROM mytable); LIKE The LIKE operator is used in a WHERE clause to search for a specified pattern in a column, often in combination with a Wildcard.
The percent sign % represents zero, one or multiple characters The underscore sign _ represents one and only one single character E.g. select all rows where the country’s name starts with G. % is a wildcard for th rest of the name (otherwise SQL would only return countries whose whole name is ‘G’).
SELECT column1, column2 FROM mytable WHERE column1 LIKE pattern; SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE Country LIKE 'G%';
title: SQL

content:
Why Conda? Conda is an environment and package management system, widely used in scientific computing and data science. Very handy if you program in Python. Conda features:
Cross-Platform Compatibility Package Management Environment Management (you can switch easily) Version control Dependency Management The handy thing about conda is that you can create a new environment for each project that you have. Then you install all the packages for each project in it’s own dedicated environment. Then you can swap back and forth to another project that might need totally different packages and versions. The different packages and versions are kind of ‘containerized’ and don’t interfer with each other.
Installation You can install Anaconda (GUI) or miniconda (minimalistic). However, I recommend the community-driven miniforge ‘version’.
Download the Miniforge Installer from the conda-forge website or use
wget https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge/releases/latest/download/Miniforge3-Linux-x86_64.sh Then run the script
bash Miniforge3-Linux-x86_64.sh -p /path/where/to/install/miniforge This will add the path to PATH. If interested, take a look at the ~/.bashrc file.
Test Conda is installed
conda info conda --version conda --help # To get an overview Customization You can skip this chapter, if you don’t want to customize the installation. In most cases the default is all you need.
If you want to manually specify where conda should create new environments and store packages, write it into a .condarc file.
Depending on the scope you want this file to have you can either put it into a project folder or place it under root. For global:
nano ~/.condarc Paste
envs_dirs: - /path/to/your/custom/environments pkgs_dirs: - /path/to/your/custo/packages You can also use specify channels there, just append the following:
channels: - conda-forge conda-forge is the (only) default channel.
To verify your configuration:
conda config --show Environments List Environments To see which environments are installed let conda list them
conda env list Create a new environment Environments are handy! You can create a new environment for each project you have and each environment will be independent from the other environments. E.g. you can download for a specific project python 3.9 and matplotlib and for another python 13.3 and tensorflow. Just create two different environments and install all the packages. See the introducion why Conda is useful.
You can create a new environment (here called my_environment) with one of the following commands:
conda create -n my_environment # Specify -n or --name conda create -n my_environment python=3.9 # Specify python version There are a bunch of possibilites how to specify the version, for both the python version and the package version.
Constraint Specification Result Fuzzy python=3.9 3.9.0, 3.9.1, 3.9.2, … Exact python==3.9.0 3.9.0 Greater than or equal to python>=3.9 3.9.0 or higher, e.g. 3.10 OR python=3.9.1 3.9.22 AND python>=3.9,<3.10" 3.9.x, 3.12.x, but not 3.10.x You can also clone an already existing environment.
conda create --clone my_environment --name my_environment2 It is also possible to specify where the environment should be created (environment will be called according to the deepest subfolder, in this case my_environment).
conda create -p /path/to/where/to/install/my_environment Furthermore, you can save an existing environment to a .txt file
conda list --explicit > saved_environment.txt or create an environment from such a .txt file.
conda create --name my_environment saved_environment.txt You can delete an environment and all it’s installed packages
conda env remove --name my_environment Enter/Leave an environment In your console you see on the very left of each input line in which environment you currently are. E.g. on Linux
(base) jokobus@MyComputer:-$ (my_environment) jokobus@MyComputer:-$ and Windows
(base) PS C:\Users\jokobus> (my_environment) PS C:\Users\jokobus> You can activate an environment
conda activate my_environment and switch back to the base environment.
conda deactivate Disable Base Environment You can permanentely disable the base environment with:
conda config --set auto_activate_base false Rename/Delete an environment You can rename a conda environment with
conda rename --name old_name new_name An alternative (the old) approach is to clone the repository and remove the original. Create the clone
conda cerate --name new_name --clone old_name Remove the environment
conda remove --name old_name --all Update/Install specific python version If you have forgotten to install the right python version at the creation of the environment or want to update, then you can install a different python version in your existing conda environment. Just keep in mind that the packages need to be compatible with the new python version. First, let’s see which python version you are currently using.
python --version You can update python to the latest version
conda install python Alternatively you can install a specific python version (e.g. 3.12) with
conda install python=3.12 After installing a new python version I recommend to conda list all packages and see that all packages you need are still there. Also it might be a good idea to update the packages or reinstall them if they were removed. Continue reading for more info how to do it.
Packages Install/Uninstall packages Now comes the interesting part. The Installation of packages!
It is as simple as
conda install mypackagename You can even stack the packages.
conda install mypackagename1 mypackagename2 mypackagename3 You can also specify the version you want to use.
conda install mypackage=3.12 If you want to uninstall a package
conda remove mypackage Update installed packages You can update a package
conda update mypackage or install (update/downgrade) a specific version.
conda install mypackage=1.1.1 or update all packages
conda update --all List installed packages You can list the packages that are installed in your current environment
conda list That’s basically all you need to know for using conda. Have fun coding - conda will help you with the necessary package management.
title: Conda

content:
There are actually two possible methods, I recommend method 1.
Method 1 Create a new temporary orphan branch, delete the main branch and rename the temporary branch to main.
git pull origin main # Make sure your on the last version git checkout --orphan temporary_branch # Create new branch git add -A # Add all current content to git commit -am "Initial commit" # Commit all content to temporary_branch git branch -D main # Remove main branch git branch -m main # Rename current temporary_branch to main git push -f origin main # Force push to main git remote prune origin # Prune old associations All done!
Method 2 Delete the .git folder. I don’t recommend it.
