content:
Today, I came accross my old Notes from my German Class at School. I decided to learn the stylistic devices (dt. “Stilmittel”) again. To do so, I want to use Anki and create a custom card with the three fields Name, Definition and Example. In this guide, I will explain how to create a custom Anki card (template) with multiple fields and cross-learning. When the cards are created, I have to learn each three times.
Name is given, find Definition and Example Definition is given, find Name and Example Example is given, find Name and Definition 1. Create Template Go to Tools>Manage Note Types (Ctrl+Shift+N) Click Add Choose Add:Basic (and reversed card) Name it something like Cross Learning (Name, Definition, Example) Select the newly created Note Type in the list (scroll down) Click Fields Rename and Add, so that you have 1:Name, 2:Definition, 3:Example. Save Click Cards Choose Card Type 1
Front Template
{{Name}} is a placeholder for the content written in the Name field.
Wrapping it by {{#Name}} … {{/Name}} ensures that the card with the Name of the stylistic device on the front is only created if Name is non-empty.
{{#Name}} {{Name}} {{/Name}} Back Template
{{Name}}<br><br> {{Definition}}<br><br> {{Example}} Choose Card Type 2
Front Template {{#Definition}} <br><br> {{Definition}} {{/Definition}} Back Template {{Name}}<br><br> {{Definition}}<br><br> {{Example}} Click on Options (upper right corner) and Add Card Type
Choose Card Type 3
Front Template {{#Example}} <br><br><br><br> {{Example}} {{/Example}} Back Template {{Name}}<br><br> {{Definition}}<br><br> {{Example}} Save all 2. Create a new Card using the Template Choose Cross Learning (Name, Definition, Example) as your Card Type
Add context
Save
Repeat for more Cards
3. Learn When you are done with creating cards you can start to learn them. You will be asked every card three times, with only the Name or the Definition or the Example given. (This sentence is a Polysyndeton by the way). You can also leave fields empty, then Anki won’t ask you those fields.
Have fun learning stylistic devices or any other content with the same structure.
title: Custom Anki Card

content:
Git Basics Find Version (Often used to test if git is installed)
git --version Set username and email address git config --global user.name "jokobus" git config --global user.email "jokobus@example.com" If you want to use the username only for the git repository of the current directory, remove the global option.
If you want to see which username and email is currently set, use
git config --global user.name git config --global user.email If you are using a credential manager, you might need to clear the stored credentials
git credential-cache exit Init Initiate empty git repository (in your directory). Afterwards one can add/remove/edit files/folders.
git init Status Status Gives an overview over tracked and untracked entities.
git status Stage Add (=stage) untracked files to git
git add <filename> git add --all # add all (incl. deleting files) git add -A # shorthand for --all git add . # Will add all modifications and new files/folders, but not delete files/folders Commit git commit -m "Some message" git commit -a -m "Some message" # -a commits all changes directly, skipping stage (for small changes, not recommended), or use -am Log & Help Git log
git log Git help
git command -help # help for a command git --help # general git help git help --all # very, very long list Branches Create a branch (~copy) of the main branch, edit your branch and merge it with the main branch.
git branch fix-small-error # create new branch git branch # verify in which branch you are (* main) git checkout fix-small-error # therefore switch to new branch # alternative: create and move to new branch in one line git checkout -b fix-small-error After making changes, add, commit and merge.
git add -A git commit -m "Some message" # Commit to fix-small-error git checkout master # Switch back to master git merge fix-small-error git branch -d fix-small-error # Delete temporary branch after successful merge If the merge fails due to a conflict, edit the file and commit again.
Use Git with Github Important: Stay up to date!! Otherwise you and your teammembers might work on different versions and merging your branches can become a nightmare.
Make a repository available locally Remote add: Upload content of an existing git repository to a new github repository.
Log in to github and create a new (empty) repository cd into your local directory Add origin and push your local files to github git remote add origin https://github.com/jokobus/hello-world.git git push --set-upstream origin master # push current main branch to origin url and set it as default for the remote branch Or Clone an existing repository
git clone https://github.com/jokobus.hello-world.git Difference between git remote add origin and git clone
remote add origin creates a remote connection to a repository clone copies a github repository, you cannot push directly Useful commands git fetch origin # download latest version from github git pull origin # pull = fetch + merge git status # show status of current repositiory git branch -a # -a show local and remote repositories, -r for remote only git log origin/main # show the log git diff origin/main # show differences between current branch and main You can also create a new branch directly on https://github.com/yourrepository and then pull again, change to that branch and edit locally.
Workflow Your own repository Create a new Branch Make changes and add Commits Open a Pull Request Review Deploy Merge Someone else’s repository Fork a repo on Github Clone your fork locally (will be called origin) mkdir C:/Users/me/wherever/myproject # create a new directory cd C:/Users/me/wherever/myproject # enter that repository git init # initialize git in git clone https://github.com/yourusername/my-forked-repo.git # clone the repository git pull origin main # pull the content Add the original repo as upstream (into the same directory) git remote add upstream https://github.com/someoneelsesusername/original-repository.git git pull upstream main Work on your fork and commit all your changes there Pull updates from the original repository When done, create a pull request to the original repository Further useful commands git remote -v # See version git remote rename origin upstream # rename from origin to upstream git Pull Request Create pull Requests A pull request is a request to merge your fork with the original. Somebody (else or you yourself) has to review and verify the request.
Go to your fork on Github Click on pull request (above the code) Add a message and send Review pull Requests (If you have the necessary rights)
On the Github webpage go to the tab Pull requests Select the pull request Add a comment and merge .gitignore Create a .gitignore file in your repository. In the .gitignore file you can specify what parts of a project won’t be shared with others, i.a.
log files temporary files hidden files personal files Note:
You can create a .gitignore file in a subdirectory; .gitignore files only apply down the directory tree. The .gitignore though will always be shared. You specify ignores in the .git/info/exclude file. Works as .gitignore, but won’t be shown anyone else Example: In the .gitignore file add:
# ignore ALL .log files except main.log *.log !main.log # ignore ALL files in ANY directory named temps temp/ Useful options to put into the .gitignore: https://www.w3schools.com/git/git_ignore.asp?remote=github SSH If you want to use SSH instead of HTTPS.
Create a keypair Create a keypair with your email address ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -C "jokobus@example.com" Enter directory where to save the key (e.g. C:/Users/user/.ssh/id_rsa) Enter a passphrase (twice), secure this passphrase in your password manager Add the SSH key pair to the SSH-Agent ssh-add C:/Users/user/.ssh/id_rsa Add public key to Github Go to the Github webpage In your profile settings select SSH and GPG keys Click the New SSH key button, select a title and paste the key into the key field Test connection in shell ssh -T git@github.com Now the console prints some text. If the last line contains your github username, you’re authenticated. Add new Github SSH remote On the Github site in your repository click the green Code button. Select SSH and copy the link Add the repository (replace git@github… with the link) git remote add ssh-origin git@github.com:myusername/my-repository.git Now you can edit your repository via SSH! Note: You can change a remote origin from HTTPS to SSH with:
git remote set-url origin git@github:myusername/my-repository.git Git Revert We use the revert command to undo a previous commit, while keeping the log intact.
Find the commit you want to undo git log --oneline Revert the change If you only want to revert the last commit git revert HEAD --no-edit # --no-edit option automatically commits If you want to go back more commits git revert HEAD~x # x is number of commits to go back (count in log) Git Reset We use reset to move the repository back to a previous commit, discarding any changes made after that commit. The log will be interrupted.
Caution: Use the reset command with caution, especially if you work together with others on the same repository.
Find the commit to whose version (the version before you messed up) you want to return to in the log Copy the first seven characers (the commit hash, eg. 12a34bc) Reset git reset 12a34bc Check the log again Git Amend commit –amend is used to modify the most recent commit. The amended commit replaces the last commit entirely.
Modify the commit message First, commit some changes with a message. Afterwards you can change the message by:
git commit --amend -m "New message for last commit. " Disconnect from a Github repository In case you have cloned a Github repository to a directory, but want to remove it again, follow these steps.
git remote remove origin # or upstream etc. git remote -v # if nothing is printed, your disconnected Then delete the directory.
title: Git

content:
This is my personal Java Summary. There’s nothing special about it, I just published it, because I already have it in markdown format, so why not?
A huge Thank You to https://www.w3schools.com/java/java_scope.asp . Most examples are more or less directly from the public Java Tutorial. Some of the more interesting I copied into my summary.
1. Basics Compile & Run C:\Users\Yourname\Path>javac Main.java	// Compile C:\Users\Yourname\Path>java Main	// Execute IO int number = 5; System.out.println("bla"+number); Type casting int x = 5; double z = x; // Widening cast: z = 5.0, done automatically int y = (int) z; // Narrowing cast: x = 5, need to specify the cast manually Strings String firstname = "Peter "; String lastname = "Pan"; firstname.toUpperCase(); //PETER firstname.toLowerCase(); //peter firstname.length(); // 5 String name = firstname + lastname; // Peter Pan, or String name = firstname.concat(lastname); // Peter Pan String <-> Integer Conversion String mystring = String.valueOf(myint); // if int myint = 5; -> mystring = "5" int myint = Integer.valueOf(mystring); // if mystring = "10"; -> myint = 10; Math helpers Math.max(myint); Math.max(5,10); //10 Math.min(myint); Math.abs(myint); Math.sqrt(myint); int randomNum = (int)(Math.random() * 101); // 0 to 100 2. Conditions & Loops If, else, elseif if, else, else if as in C
if (x>y){ // do … } else if (x==y){ // do … } else { // (x<y) // do … } The available logical conditions are
Symbol Meaning == equal to < less than <= less than or equal to > greater than >= greater than or equal to != not equal to Ternary operators if (x > y) { System.out.prinln("x>y"); } else { System.out.println("x<=y"); } Can be written as:
(x>y) ? System.out.println("x>y") : System.out.println("x<=y"); while loop while(condition){ // do … } do while loop do { // do … } while (condition); Switch switch (condition) { switch x: // do … break; switch y: // do … // if here no break, then condition z will also be executed switch z: // do … break; default: // do … }	For loop for (int i = 0; i < i; i++){ // do … } For-each loop String cars[] = {"Volvo", "BMW", "Audi"}; for (i : cars){ System.out.println(i); } Arrays String[] cars; String[] cars = {"Volvo", "BMW", "Audi"}; int[] myNum = {10, 20, 30}; 3. Methods Basic example Methods must always be declared inside a class.
public class Main { static void myMethod(String fname, String lname, int age) { System.out.println(fname + " " + lname + " is " + age); } static int myAddition(int a, int b) { return a + b; } public static void main(String[] args) { myMethod("Liam", "Miller", 5); myMethod("Jenny","Barker", 8); myMethod("Anja", "Meier", 31); myMethod("Peter", "the Pointer", 0); int sum = myAddition(5,3); //5+3=8 } } Method overloading You can declare multiple methods with the same name, but different parameters. Depending on the provided parameters the consequent method will be called.
int myMethod(int x) float myMethod(float x) double myMethod(double x, double y) Hint: Widening casting will be done automatically, but narrowing casting not.
4. Classes A class is a template for objects, and an object is an instance of a class. When the individual objects are created, they inherit all the variables and methods from the class.
public class Main { int x = 0; public static void main(String[] args) { Main myObj1 = new Main(); Main myObj2 = new Main(); myObj1.x = 5; myObj2.x = 3; System.out.println(myObj1.x); // 5 System.out.println(myObj2.x); // 3 } } Multiple Classes Each java class has to be stored in a file with the name of that class. So the Main class is stored in a file called Main.java and the class Second has to be stored in a different file called Second.java. One needs to compile both.
static vs public functions static functions can be called without creating an object (but creating an object is also okay) public functions must be called by creating objects public class Main() { static void myStaticFunction(){ // do … } public void myPublicFunction(){ // do … } public static void main(String[] args) { myStaticFunction(); // myPublicFunction(); // would throw a compile error Main myObj = new Main(); myObj.myPublicFunction(); } } Constructor The constructor is used to generate a new object. The constructor is autogenerated by java. If you explicitely write down the constructor, you can specify parameters (inputs at constrution and default values) public class Main(){ // This is the constructor public Main(int y){ int x = y; } public static void main(String[] args){ Main myObj = new Main(5); System.out.println(myObj.x); //5 } } Modifiers Access-Modifiers Access-Modifiers modifies access level of a class.
For classes
public: The class is accessible by another class. default: The class is only accessible by other classes in the same package. For attributes, functions & constructors
public: The code is accessible for all classes. private: The code is only accessible within the declared class. default: The code is only accessible in the same package. protected: The code is accessible in the same package and subclass. Non-access Modifiers Non-Access Modifiers do not control access level, but provide other functionality.
For classes
final: The class cannot be inherited by other classes. abstract: The class cannot be used to create objects. (To access an abstract class, it must be inherited from another class.) For attributes and methods
final: Attributes and methods cannot be overriden/modified. (final int x = 5; x = 10; // Compile error) static: Attributes and methods belongs to the class, rather than an object. abstract: Can only be used in an abstract class, and can only be used on methods. The method does not have a body, for example abstract void run();. The body is provided by subclass (inherited from). transient: Attributes and methods are skipped when serializing the object containing them. synchronized: Methods can only be accessed by one thread at a time volatile: The value of an attribute is not cached thread-locally, and is always read from the “main memory”. Encapsulation Method to ensure that sensitive data cannot be read by any class. Declare variables as private Write a public get-method which returns the variable value, and a public set-method which sets the value. Packages & API import package.name.Class; // Import a single class import package.name.*; // Import the whole package E.g. the useful Scanner-package, used to read user input on the console.
import java.util.scanner; class MyClass{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner myObj = new Scanner(System.in); System.out.println("Enter username: "); String userName = myObj.nextline(); System.out.println("Your username is: "+userName); } } Declare custom packages package mypack; // Package name lowercase to avoid confusion with classnames class MyPackageClass { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("This is my package!"); } } Compile the MyPackageClass.java
C:\Users\Yourname\Path>javac MyPackageClass.java Compile the package
C:\Users\Yourname\Path>javac -d . MyPackageClass.java -d keyword specifies the directory where the package should be stored, . means same directory.
Run the package
C:\Users\Yourname\Path>java mypack.MyPackageClass This is my package! Inheritance (Subclass and Superclass) handy for code reusability: reuse attributes and methods of an existing class when creating a new class.
// Superclass class Vehicle { protected String brand = "BMW"; // … } // Subclass inherits Attributes/Methods from Superclass class Car extends Vehicle { private String modelName = "X6"; public static void main(String[] args) { Car myCar = new Car(); System.out.println("I have a "+myCar.brand+" "+myCar.modelName); } } If you don’t want a class to be inheritable, use the final keyword, e.g. final class Vehicle.
Polymorphism The following example is very interesting. It uses inheritance and method overloading.
class Animal { public void animalSound() { System.out.println("The animal makes a sound"); } } class Cat extends Animal { public void animalSound() { System.out.println("The cat says: miau"); } } class Dog extends Animal { public void animalSound() { System.out.println("The dog says: wau"); } } class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Animal myAnimal = new Animal(); // Create a Animal object Animal myCat = new Cat(); // Create a Pig object Animal myDog = new Dog(); // Create a Dog object myAnimal.animalSound(); // The animal makes a sound myCat.animalSound(); // The cat says: miau myDog.animalSound(); // The dog says: wau } } Nested classes class OuterClass { int x = 1; class InnerClass{ int y = 2; } } public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { OuterClass myOuterObject = new OuterClass(); // New outer object OuterClass.InnerClass myInnerObject = new OuterClass.InnerClass(); // New inner object System.out.println(myOuterObject.x + myInnerObject.y) } } If you declare the InnerClass as private, an outside class can’t access it. When you use static, you don’t need to create an inner object. Abstract class Abstraction is the process of hiding certain details and showing only essential information to the user.
Abstract class: cannot be used to create objects, must be inherited Abstract method: can only be used in an abstract class, doesn’t have a body. The body is provided by the subclass. abstract class Animal { public abstract void animalSound(); // does not have a body } class Cat extends Animal { public void animalSound() { System.out.println("The cat says: miau"); } } class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { // Animal myAnimal = new Animal(); // Create a Animal object not possible Animal myCat = new Cat(); // Create a Pig object // myAnimal.animalSound(); // Execute the abstract function is not possible myCat.animalSound(); // The cat says: miau } } Interfaces Notes on Interfaces from w3schools .
Like abstract classes, interfaces cannot be used to create objects (in the example above, it is not possible to create an “Animal” object in the MyMainClass) Interface methods do not have a body - the body is provided by the “implement” class On implementation of an interface, you must override all of its methods Interface methods are by default abstract and public Interface attributes are by default public, static and final An interface cannot contain a constructor (as it cannot be used to create objects) Why And When To Use Interfaces?
To achieve security - hide certain details and only show the important details of an object (interface).
Java does not support “multiple inheritance” (a class can only inherit from one superclass). However, it can be achieved with interfaces, because the class can implement multiple interfaces. Note: To implement multiple interfaces, separate them with a comma (see example below).
interface FirstInterface { public void myMethod(); // interface method, no body } interface SecondInterface { public void myOtherMethod(); // interface method, no body } class DemoClass implements FirstInterface, SecondInterface { public void myMethod() { System.out.println("Some text…"); } public void myOtherMethod() { System.out.println("Some other text…"); } } class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { DemoClass myObj = new DemoClass(); myObj.myMethod(); myObj.myOtherMethod(); } } Enums An enum represents a group of constants (public, final, static variables). Used for known, fix values, such as month names, weekdays, colors, etc. Handy if combined with the switch statement.
public class Main { enum Level { LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH } public static void main(String[] args){ Level myVar = Level.MEDIUM; System.out.println(myVar); //MEDIUM for (Level myVar : Level.values()){ System.out.print(myVar+", "); //LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, } } } Scanner import java.util.Scanner; class Main { public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner myScanner = new Scanner(System.in); String inputString = myObj.nextLine(); } } Method Description nextBoolean() Reads a boolean value from the user nextByte() Reads a byte value from the user nextDouble() Reads a double value from the user nextFloat() Reads a float value from the user nextInt() Reads a int value from the user nextLine() Reads a String value from the user nextLong() Reads a long value from the user nextShort() Reads a short value from the user Date & Time import java.time.LoclaDate; public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { LocalDate myDateObj = LocalDate.now(); // Create a date object LocalTime myTimeObj = LocalTime.now(); LoclaDateTime myDateTimeObj = LocalDateTime.now(); System.out.println(myDateObj); // 2025-04-01 System.out.println(myTimeObj); // 20:20:01.123456 System.out.println(myDateTimeObj); // 2025-04-01T20:20.01.123456 DateTimeFormatter myFormatObj = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("dd-MM-yy HH:mm:ss"); String formattedDate = myDateObj.format(myFormatObj); System.out.println(formattedDate); // 01-04-2025 20:20:01 } } 5. Java Data Structures ArrayList ArrayList class is a resizable arry in the java.util package (normal arrays are not resizable), where you can add/remove elements on the go.
Use Collections in the java.util package to sort lists. Will sort by default in ascending order. Use option Collections.reverseOrder() for descending order.
import java.util.ArrayList; import java.util.Collections; // Used to sort the ArrayList (alphabetically, ascending, etc. ) public class Main { public static void main(String[] args){ ArrayList<String> animals = new ArrayList<String>(); // Create new ArrayList object storing Strings // Instead of <String> one can use <Boolean>, <Character>, <Double>, <Integer>, etc. animals.add("Zebra"); animals.add("Mephitidae"); animals.add("Cat") System.out.println(animals); // [Zebra, Mephitidae, Cat] animals.add(2,"Elephant"); // Specify position, start with 0 System.out.println(animals); // [Zebra, Mephitidae, Elephant, Cat] System.out.println(animals.get(2)); // Elephant animals.set(2,"Dog"); // Overwrite Elephant animals.size(); for (int i = 0; i < animals.size(); i++){ // do … } Collections.sort(animals); System.out.println(animals); // [Cat, Dog, Mephitidae, Zebra] Collections.sort(animals, Collections.reverseOrder()); // Reverse the order System.out.println(animals); // [Zebra, Mephitidae, Dog, Cat] animals.remove(1); // Delete Mephitidae animals.clear(); // Empty ArrayList } } List of all useful ArrayList commands LinkedList Built as a linked list (like in C: structs referenced by pointers), whereas an ArrayList is a real array and if the array’s size is not sufficient anymore, a new, bigger array is reserved and all content copied over. Frontend-functionality is mostly the same.
List of all usefull LinkedList commands HashMap Hash maps are key/value pairs of same or different type, e.g. HashMap<String, String>, HashMap<String, boolean>, Hashmap<int, double> etc.
import java.util.HashMap(); public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ HashMap<String, String> Capitals = new HashMap<String, String>(); Capitals.put("Germany", "Berlin"); Capitals.put("England", "London"); Capitals.put("France", "Paris"); Capitals.put("Austria", "Vienna"); System.out.println(Capitals); // {Germany=Berlin, England=London, France=Paris} System.out.println(Capitals.get("England")); // London System.out.println(Capitals.remove("Austria")); System.out.println(Capitals.size()); // 3 for (String myCountry : Capitals.keySet()){ System.out.println(myCountry); // Germany, England, France System.out.println(myCountry+" is in "+myCountry.get(myCountry)); } for (String myCapital : Capitals.value()){ System.out.println(myCapital); // Berlin, London, Paris } } } HashSet Purpose: Like an array, where each element exists only once, even if added multiple times.
Iterator Used to iterate through an array, arraylist, hashmap, etc.
import java.util.Iterator; Iterator<String> myiterator = animals.Iterator(); System.out.println(myiterator.next()); // Print first item System.out.pirtnln(myiterator.next()); // Print second item while (myiterator.hasNext() == true){ currentanimal = myiterator.next(); // [Dog, Gnu, Zebra, ...] if(currentanumal == "Gnu"){ myiterator.remove() } } System.out.println(myiterator); // [Dog, Zebra, ...] but no Gnu Using a for loop or for-each loop would not work, as we are removing items from the array in situ (thus change it’s length), while we try to loop through it.
Wrapper Classes Wrapper classes provide a way to use primitive variables as objects. The first character of the type classifier is written Uppercase, e.g. Char, Int, Double, etc. Useful if you work with arrays, arraylists where you can only store objects.
// ArrayList<int> mylist = new Arraylist<int>(); // Error ArrayList<Integer> mylist = new ArrayList<Integer>(); // Works // Create a new wrapper object Integer myint = 5; To get the object wrapper’s value: intValue(), byteValue(), shortValue(), longValue(), floatValue(), doubleValue(), charValue(), booleanValue().
To convert it to a String: .toString().
To get the length of the string
Integer myint = 1000; System.out.println(myint.toString().length()); // 4, as "1000" has four characters 6. Advanced stuff Java Exceptions try
If the block of code fails to be executed then run some failback code.
public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { try { // Initial code tried to get executed int[] myNumbers = {1, 2, 3}; System.out.println(myNumbers[10]); // Out of bound error } catch (Exception e) { // error in try{} so execute this: System.out.println("Something went wrong."); } finally { // executed when try-catch is finished System.out.println("The 'try catch' is finished."); } } } throw
Use throw to throw a custom error of type ArithmeticException, FileNotFoundException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, SecurityException.
throw new ArithmeticException("Some error message");
title: Java

content:
You may know the popular LaTeX package graphicx which you can use as:
\includegraphics[options]{filename} graphicx does not support svg, but there is the package called svg that does the job.
How to use the svg package: Install Inkscape On Windows you can use winget.
winget install -e --id Inkscape.Inkscape Copy your image.svg file into your document root folder (= right next to your .tex file)
This is a MWE for your .tex file:
\documentclass{article} \includepackage{svg} \begin{document} \begin{figure} \includesvg[]{image} \end{figure} \end{document} Notes:
File ending is optional (image or image.svg both work) There are many parameters one can set in the square brackets (e.g. width=1cm, height=1cm, scale=0.5, and many more) Download and read the svg package documentation on CTAN for more information Oh no! It would have been too easy. If you click on Build, you will run into an error:
example.tex: error: 120: Package svg Error: File `image_svg-tex.pdf' is missing. \includesvg{image} You can solve this error by opening (in TeXstudio) Settings>Commands and adding the flag
--shell-escape to your PdfLaTeX run command. The command now should look like:
pdflatex.exe -synctex=1 -interaction=nonstopmode --shell-escape %.tex If you are using XeLaTeX, LulaTeX, etc. you should add the flag there (probably, I didn’t test it).
If you are using Texmaker, the whole idea is the same, but the exact settings may be somewhere else.
Save the configuration and run again.
More oh no! We are not there yet. We need to solve the error message
example.tex: error: 119: Package svg Error: Inkscape version not detected. ...ncludesvg{image} This error means that LaTeX doesn’t know where to find Inkscape. Therefore we add the path whereever inkscape was installed as a custom command. To do so, first locate the right folder (on Windows the default is: C:\Program Files\Inkscape\bin) then open Settings>Build and add the path to Commands.
That’s it, now it should run.
title: How to include Scalable Vector Graphics (svg) in $\LaTeX$

Achtung Disclaimer Dies sind meine persönlichen Notizen zum LBA Kompetenznachweis, der in Europa zum Führen von unbemannten Luftfahrzeugen (v.a. im Hobby-Bereich) berechtigt. Es besteht kein Anspruch auf Richtigkeit. Alle Informationen - soweit nicht anderweitig gekennzeichnet - stammen von https://lba-openuav.de/onlinekurs/ .
Checkliste Kompetenznachweis (Drohnenführerschein) Betreiberregistrierung Versicherung Checke, dass du mit der richtigen Drohnenklasse in der richtigen Kategorie unterwegs bist Der einfachste Weg, um legal loslegen zu können ist, die Website lba-openuav.de zu besuchen, dort den Online-Kurs zu machen, den A1/A3 (kleinen) Drohnenführerschein und sich dort nach bestehen des kurzen Tests gleich noch als UAV-Betreiber eintragen. Zuletzt noch kurz checken, dass die Haftpflichtversicherung Drohnen mit abdeckt und los kann es gehen.
Begriffe Betrieb außerhalb der direkten Sicht ist nicht erlaubt, dabei geht es um die tatsächliche Sicht (ohne Hilfsmittel wie Fernglas) Wenn FPV, dann ist eine zweite Person als Spotter nötig, Pilot weiter voll verantwortlich für den Flug und etwaige Schäden Für die Gewichtsgrenzen sind ist das Höchstzulässige Gesamtgewicht (Max Take-Off Gewicht) ausschlaggebend Alle nicht 100% für den Flug notwendigen Bauteile zählen als Nutzlast (darunter fallen auch Kameras, Fahrwerk, etc.) UAS-Klassifizierungen Offen Speziell Zulassungspflichtig Alle Kriterien müssen erfüllt sein Sobald ein Kriterium erfüllt ist Sobald ein Kriterium erfüllt ist < 25 kg > 25 kg nicht über Menschenansammlungen über Menschenansammlungen < 120 m Flughöhe AGL (Above Ground Level) >120 m AGL VLOS (Visual Line of Sight) BVLOS (Beyond VLOS) Kein Gefahrengut Gefahrengut u.o. Personentransport Offene Kategorie Legal, wenn:
UAS entspricht den technischen Anforderungen Fernpilot erfüllt die Betriebsregeln Fernpilot ist adäquat qualifiziert Betreiber ist registriert ist (sofern notwendig) Kategorie und UAS-Klasse Infografik zum Thema Einsatzbereichkategorie und UAS-Klasse, Quelle: https://lba-openuav.de/onlinekurs/lehrmaterial/luftrecht-und-sicherheit/uas-klassifizierungen/
Noch eine Infografik zum Thema Einsatzbereichkategorie und UAS-Klasse, Quelle: https://lba-openuav.de/onlinekurs/lehrmaterial/luftrecht-und-sicherheit/uas-klassifizierungen/
Selbstgebaute Drohnen Um nun herauszufinden, was/wie/wo man mit einer selbstgebauten Drohne fliegen darf, ist flogende Grafik hilfreich: Infografik zur Risikobewertung des UAS, Quelle: https://www.lba.de/DE/Drohnen/Allgemeine_Informationen/Risikobewertung/Risikobewertung.html;jsessionid=FCF4DC1A8549232036749D1DF2DD4FA7.live11294?nn=2995260
Soweit ich das verstanden habe gilt mit Hinblick auf selbstgebaute Drohnen folgendes, je nachdem welchen Drohnenführeschein man hat. Die offiziellen Informationen gibt es auf: https://lba-openuav.de/onlinekurs/lehrmaterial/luftrecht-und-sicherheit/uas-klassifizierungen/ Ohne Drohnenführerschein:
Nur gekaufte Drohnen unter 250g Fliegen auch in Wohngebieten erlaubt vereinzelt über Menschen fliegen erlaubt Überfliegen von Menschenansammlungen (generell) verboten -> Für selbstgebaute Drohnen braucht man also zwingend mindestens den kleinen Drohnenführerschein.
Mit dem kleinen Drohnenführerschein: Selbstgebaute Drohnen dürfen in der Kategorie A3 fliegen, d.h.
für alle Drohnen zwischen 250g und 25kg Flüge nur auf freiem Feld Abstand von mindestens 150m zu Wohngebieten horizontaler Abstand zu unbeteiligten Personen min. 30m min. 1:1 Abstand-Flughöhe Verhältnis und 2s Regel zu Wohngebieten, Industrie, Erholungsgebieten, etc. Mit dem großen (A2) Drohnenführeschein: Selbstgebaute Drohen dürfen auch in der Kategorie A2 fliegen, d.h.
Drohnen bis 4kg Flug über Wohngebieten erlaubt in langsamen Flug ($<3\frac{m}{s}$): Abstand von 5m zu unbeteiligten Personen in schnellerem Flug: Abstand von 30m zu unbeteiligten Personen Generell gelten hier die Anforderungen der offenen Kategorie (v.a. Flug nur auf Sicht, AGL < 120m und nicht über Menschenansammlungen)
Ergo, wenn man den großen Drohnenführerschein hat, darf man entweder in der Kategorie A2 oder A3 fliegen. Z.B. eine 3kg schwere Drohnen auch in Wohngebieten, eine 5kg schwere Drohne aber nur auf dem Feld (A2).
Für den großen Drohnenführeschein muss der Fernpilot zur Qualifikation:
den Online-Kurs und die Online-Theorieprüfung bestehen (= kleiner Drohnenführerschein) praktisches Training in Eigenregie Theorieprüfung vor Ort Luftfahrtverwaltung Begriffe EASA: European Union Aviation Safety Agency -> EU weit (plus Schweiz und Norwegen, nicht Großbritannien) in Deutschland: Luftfahrtbundesamt (LBA) in Österreich: Austrocontrol Nach Unfällen (und anderen Begebenheiten) Störungsmeldungen unter: https://onl-meldung.bfu-web.de/onlmeldung/ Diese Meldung ist nur zur Information der Behörden. Es ist der Behörde nicht gestattet basierend auf dieser Meldung den Meldenden zu verfolgen und zu sanktionieren.
Gebiete Die EU-Mitgliedsländer sind dazu anghalten den Luftraum in verschiedene Zonen einzuteilen. In manchen Zonen liegen Beschränkungen/Flugverboten vor, z.B. um Flughäfen, militärische Einrichtungen, …
Daher ist es zwingend nötig, dass man vor jedem Flug nachschaut, ob Beschränkungen vorliegen. Hierzu gibt es Internetseiten (einfach googlen) oder auch Apps.
Österreich: Austro Control Dronespace (Google Play )
Deutschland: Droniq App (Google Play )
Faktor Mensch Alkohol für der Betrieb von UAS: unter 0,2 Promille, kein Alkohol ab 8h vor dem Flug (Richtwerte) keine Müdigkeit
title: LBA Kompetenznachweis

If you try to use git you can get the following error:
fatal: unable to access 'https://github.com/yourusername/yourrepository.git/': Could not resolve host: github.com This error can happen, if you are using a VPN.
The solution is to either toggle your VPN and try again or (if you don’t want to toggle your VPN) to execute:
If your VPN is currently OFF:
git config --global --unset http.proxy If your VPN is currently ON:
git config --global --set http.proxy This should resolve the error. Unfortunately you have to execute this command each time you are toggling your VPN.
title: How to solve git VPN issue

content:
This is a short guide how to create a Hugo blog, using the tailwind theme, which is the one I’m currently using myself. This guide was written with unexperienced users in mind. If you follow this guide 1:1 you should get a running instance (no guarantee of course). If you are confident with the topic, you can use this guide to get inspired and modify the steps to your own preference.
Although the following steps are for Windows, the whole process is the same for Linux/MacOS. Only some of the console commands are slightly different. E.g. instead of the powershell command Start-process you can use nano or vim and instead of winget you may use your distribution’s package manager or homebrew.
Important concept to understand The blog will be hosted on github and will be stored in two different repositories. The first repository (let’s call this repo website) is private and stores all the code and content needed to build the blog. The second repository has to be named according to your github username yourusername.github.io, where you replace yourusername with your actual github username (Please also replace yourusername in all the following code by your actual username. In my case the second repository is called jokobus.github.io). The yourusername.github.io repository contains all the public html (and other stuff) that Hugo created and is nested inside your website repository. Most of your code can be private, but the subdirectory called public (yes, that is it’s name) has to be public. Once the website is built by Hugo, we only need to publish the stuff in the public subdirectory. We publish it by pushing the content to the yourusername repository on github. All other content outside the public folder is just needed to build the content of the public folder. Finally your blog will be available on https://yourusername.github.io .
Prerequisites On Windows, we should use the Windows Powershell and not the built in Command Prompt. If you don’t have Powershell installed, you can follow this guide . The quick way via winget is:
winget install -e --id Microsoft.PowerShell On Linux and Mac your default Terminal should do the job.
Secondly you need to have a Github account and git installed . Create a new github account here and install git by executing this in the powershell.
winget install -e --id Git.Git Then authenticate git to Github by one of the options mentioned in the official article .
The easiest way is to install the Github Desktop Client and authenticate there:
winget install -e --id GitHub.GitHubDesktop Follow the installation guide and open the Github Desktop Application. Login with your Github credentials there. You will automatically be able to use your credentials with the git command.
All prerequistes met? Let’s go!
Step 1: Install Hugo Follow the official Hugo guide . As a service I added the steps for Windows below. However, it is definitely a good idea to look at the official guide as well.
If you use winget, it is as simple as:
winget install Hugo.Hugo.Extended Step 2: Create github repositories Go to https://github.com and log in to your account. Create a new private repository called website and a public repository called yourusername.github.io (where you off course replace yourusername with your actual username).
Private repository First, we clone the website repository.
Move into the folder where you want to store your website and clone the repo. cd "C:/path/to/where/you/want/to/store/your/website" git clone https://github.com/yourusername/website.git Create a new Hugo instance. cd website hugo new site yourusername cd yourusername Optional: To see all the folders that were created, type:
ls Do you see a config.toml or hugo.toml file? This is the configuration file, where all the settings are stored. Later, we will modify it.
Public repository Now we clone the public yourusername.github.io repository and add it as a submodule. (Here is some unofficial, but good explanation what a submodule is. )
Clone the public repository next to your private one, add a readme and push it to github cd "C:/path/to/where/you/want/to/store/your/website" git clone https://github.com/yourusername/yourusername.github.io.git cd yourusername.github.io git checkout -b main notepad Readme.md Add a short description like “This is the public content for my Hugo website. " to the Readme file.
git add . git commit -m "First commit to add Readme. " Switch back into your private repository cd ../ cd website/yourusername and add a submodule.
git submodule add -b main https://github.com/yourusername/yourusername.github.io.git public Check that everything worked by: cd public ls You should see a file called Readme.md and if you open it with
notepad Readme.md you will find exactly the text you wrote in the Readme.md file a few lines above.
Step 3: Install theme We use the Tailwind theme , but you can pick whichever you want. There is an extensive list of free themes on the hugo website.
cd "C:/path/to/where/you/want/to/store/your/website/website/yourusername" git submodule add https://github.com/tomowang/hugo-theme-tailwind.git themes/tailwind You can always update the theme by:
git submodule update --remote Step 4: Adjust to your needs First, we open the config.toml file and paste some prebuilt configuration.
Start-process config.toml Paste the following text (originally from tailwind and extended by me). Play around with it and adjust to your needs.
baseURL = 'https://yourusername.github.io' title = 'My website' subtitle ="A Blog about random topics. " author = "Your Name" enableInlineShortcodes = true theme = "tailwind" # Enables the robots.txt file, default is to allow all crawling, see step 8 on how to modify it. enableRobotsTXT = true # Please enable this feature if the site is available in Japanese, Chinese or Korean. #hasCJKLanguage = true jsonLD = true # If you're using Google Analytics, you can enable this feature. #googleAnalytics = "G-1234567890" # Disqus Comment System #disqusShortname = "" #Emoji support enableEmoji = true #Paginate, how many sites before break paginate=50 [markup] [markup.highlight] # lineNos = true noClasses = false [markup.goldmark] [markup.goldmark.renderer] unsafe = true [markup.goldmark.extensions] [markup.goldmark.extensions.cjk] enable = true eastAsianLineBreaks = true escapedSpace = true [markup.goldmark.extensions.passthrough] enable = true [markup.goldmark.extensions.passthrough.delimiters] block = [['\[', '\]'], ['$$', '$$']] inline = [['\(', '\)'], ['$', '$']] [params] # Base color of the website. # https://tailwindcss.com/docs/background-color base_color = "bg-gray-800" subtitle = "A Blog about random topics" #Site description description = "My Website" #Enable LaTeX math support math=true # this enables KaTeX, see https://katex.org/ for how to use it # Number of tags to be displayed. tagsLimit = 20 # Show or not to show the reading time showReadingTime = true [params.author] name = "Your Name" email = "" [params.header] logo = "logo.jpg" # Place logo.jpg in static folder sticky = false [params.footer] since = 2024 poweredby = true [params.ad] # Google AdSense # ex. ca-pub-0000000000000000 googleAdSense = "" # ex. 000000000 adslot = "" # Placement of ads postTop = false postBottom = false [params.post] # Show thumbnails thumbnail = true # Disable the "NO IMAGE" image. noimage = true # See the Tailwind CSS documentation to set it up. # Recommended settings are h-32 to h-48. # https://tailwindcss.com/docs/height thumbnail_height = "lg:h-44" # https://tailwindcss.com/docs/width thumbnail_width = "lg:w-auto" # Number of characters in description description = "130" [params.widgets] # Display widgets display = true # Order of widgets to display widgets = ["search", "archive", "category", "tags", "recent"] # If you're using Yandex.Metrica, you can enable this feature. #[params.analytics.yandexmetrica] # enabled = true # tagNumber = '' [[params.social_media.items]] enabled = false title = 'Github' icon = 'brand-github' link = 'https://github.com/yourusername' [languages] [languages.en] contentDir = 'content/en' languageCode = 'en-US' languageName = 'English' weight = 1 disabled = false [languages.en.params] reading_speed = 228 [[languages.en.menu.main]] identifier="home" name = 'Home' url = '/en' weight = 1 [[languages.en.menu.main]] identifier="about" name="About" url='/about/' weight = 2 [[languages.en.menu.main]] identifier="contact" url='/contact/' name="Contact" weight = 3 [languages.de] contentDir = 'content/de' languageCode = 'de-DE' languageName = 'Deutsch' weight = 2 disabled = false [languages.de.params] reading_speed = 179 [[languages.de.menu.main]] identifier="home" name = 'Start' url='/de' weight = 1 [[languages.de.menu.main]] identifier="about" name="Über mich" url='/de/ueber/' weight = 2 [[languages.de.menu.main]] identifier="contact" name="Kontakt" url='/de/kontakt/' weight = 3 [taxonomies] category = "categories" tag = "tags" archive = "archives" [privacy] [privacy.vimeo] disabled = false simple = true [privacy.twitter] disabled = false enableDNT = true simple = true [privacy.instagram] disabled = false simple = true [privacy.youtube] disabled = false privacyEnhanced = true [services] [services.instagram] disableInlineCSS = true [services.twitter] disableInlineCSS = true [sitemap] changefreq = "weekly" priority = 0.5 Also, it is a good idea to personalize the default post header. This is the header that will be created by default each time you create a new post.
Start-process /archetypes/default.md Replace the code in the file by the following code:
+++ title = '{{ replace .File.ContentBaseName "-" " " | title}}' description = "" translationKey = '{{ replace .File.ContentBaseName "-" " " | title | lower}}' author = "Your Name" date = {{ .Date }} archives = ["{{ dateFormat "2006-01" .Date }}"] tags = ["", ""] draft = false toc = true +++ Step 5: Add content To add content in markdown format you have to do the following:
Create a new blog entry via hugo new /en/posts/yourpostname.md or hugo new /en/posts/yourpostname/index.md both methods work the same, but in the second variant you can also store images inside the /yourpostname/ folder. Use your favourite text editor to open the newly created markdown file and add content. You may look at some examples in /themes/tailwind/exampleSite/content/ and read this useful guide about the Markdown syntax. All the content is written in the simple Markdown format. Multilanguage support Tailwind supports a blog with multiple languages. That’s a really nice feature. The code for the config.toml file I provided makes your website ready for multiple languages (in this case english and german, but you can simply add another language).
The important step is, to store two .md files in the correct directories with the same translation key in their headers.
The following graphic shows the most important parts of the file-tree inside your website repository.
├── yourusername ├── archetypes ├── assets ├── content ├── de ├── posts ├── Hunde ├── images └── index.md ├── Meerschweinchen └── index.md └── Katzen.md ├── uebermich.md └── kontakt.md └── en ├── posts ├── dogs ├── images └── index.md ├── guineapigs └── index.md └── cats.md ├── about.md └── contact.md ├── data ├── i18n ├── layouts ├── node_modules ├── public ├── resources ├── static ├── themes └── tailwind ├── gitcommit.ps1 … └── config.toml ├── .gitattributes ├── .gitmodules └── README.md A few general words:
public In this folder all the html and other public stuff Hugo creates is stored. Only this folder needs to be public. config.toml This is your configuraton file (you could also rename it to hugo.toml if you want) themes this is where you clone your themes to Now, about the multilanguage support: Ultimately we want to have two different posts with the same content, but one in english and one in german. The user can toggle on the website, which article he wants to see.
To achive this, everything is already prepared for you.
Create a new article as you did in Step 5 Write some english content copy index.md from /en/posts/yourpostname/index.md to /de/posts/yourpostname/index.md, open the file and translate it to german. Make sure that the headers of both index.md files have the identical value for translationKey= in their headers (e.g. both have translationKey=“guineapig”) Step 6: Push to github In this step, we finally get to the point where you can see the result. All you need for this is:
commit the private code to the website repository Build the public content in ./public subdirectory commit the public code to the yourusername.github.io repository open https://yourusername.github.io in your browser To do so we have to execute the following code in our terminal (on Windows use Powershell rather than cmd):
First we have to go to into our website repository
cd "C:/path/to/hugo/website/repository" commit the private code to the website repository git add . git commit -m "Some message" git push origin main Build the public content in ./public subdirectory hugo -t tailwind cd public commit the public code to the yourusername.github.io repository git add . git commit -m "Some message" git push origin main open https://yourusername.github.io in your browser Start-Process "https://yourusername.github.io" Step 7: Automate github interaction with script This is way too much work! We don’t want to type half a dozen lines for just pushing our code to github. Therefore I wrote a little file that does the job for us. Disclaimer: The translation from batch to ps1 and bash was done by ChatGPT.
Note, that I built the files in a way that I can easily switch between different themes, in case I ever wish to do so. If you don’t want to use that, feel free to remove the unncessary code. Also, when the script asks for the theme name, it only takes the first character of your input. If you have two themes with the same first character, you have to modify the line that starts with for /f “delims="….
Windows Batch file (not recommended) Create a new file called gitcommit.bat in your website repository and paste the following lines. Edit the pathtowebsite-Variable in line 3.
@echo off title Commit Hugo Webpage to Github set pathtowebsite = "C:/path/to/my/website" cd pathtowebsite set /p message="Enter Comment Message: " set /p theme="Enter Theme Name : Tailwind (default) or Blonde: " echo --- website Repository --- git add -A if "%message%"=="" ( echo No message, committing changes without comment. git commit ) else ( git commit -m "%message%" ) git push origin echo Total code was successfully pushed to private repository. echo --- public Repository --- if "%theme%"=="" ( echo Theme Name cannot be empty, using default Tailwind set theme = Tailwind ) REM Convert theme to lowercase and get the first character for /f "delims=" %%i in ('echo %theme% ^| tr "[A-Z]" "[a-z]"') do set theme=%%i set theme=%theme:~0,1% if "%theme%"=="b" ( hugo -t Blonde ) else ( hugo -t tailwind ) cd public git add -A if "%message%"=="" ( echo No message, committing changes without comment. git commit ) else ( git commit -m "%message%" ) git push origin echo Public code successfully pushed. cd pathtowebsite echo Job done, everything was pushed. exit To commit your changes, type:
.\gitcommit.bat Windows Powershell (recommended) Create a new file called gitcommit.ps1 file in your website repository and paste the following lines. Edit the pathtowebsite-Variable in line 1.
$pathtowebsite = "C:/path/to/my/website" Set-Location -Path $pathtowebsite $message = Read-Host "Enter Comment Message" $theme = Read-Host "Enter Theme Name : Tailwind (default) or Blonde" Write-Host "--- website Repository ---" git add -A if ([string]::IsNullOrEmpty($message)) { Write-Host "No message, committing changes without comment." git commit } else { git commit -m $message } git push origin Write-Host "Total code was successfully pushed to private repository." Write-Host "--- public Repository ---" # Check if theme name is provided, otherwise use default if ([string]::IsNullOrEmpty($theme)) { Write-Host "Theme Name cannot be empty, using default Tailwind" $theme = "Tailwind" } # Convert theme to lowercase and get the first character $theme = $theme.ToLower() $theme = $theme.Substring(0, 1) if ($theme -eq "b") { hugo -t Blonde } else { hugo -t tailwind } Set-Location -Path "public" git add -A if ([string]::IsNullOrEmpty($message)) { Write-Host "No message, committing changes without comment." git commit -m "No comment provided" } else { git commit -m $message } git push origin Write-Host "Public code successfully pushed." Set-Location -Path $pathtowebsite Write-Host "Job done, everything was pushed." exit To commit your changes, type:
.\gitcommit.ps1 or only
.\gitcommit Linux bash file Create a new file called gitcommit.sh in your website repository and paste the following lines. Edit the pathtowebsite-Variable in line 2.
#!/bin/bash pathtowebsite="C:/path/to/my/website" cd "$pathtowebsite" git add -A read -p "Enter Comment Message: " message read -p "Enter Theme Name : Tailwind (default) or Blonde: " theme echo "--- website Repository ---" if [ -z "$message" ]; then echo "No message, committing changes without comment." git commit -m "No comment provided" else git commit -m "$message" fi git push origin echo "Total code was successfully pushed to private repository." echo "--- public Repository ---" if [ -z "$theme" ]; then echo "Theme Name cannot be empty, using default Tailwind" theme="Tailwind" fi # Convert theme to lowercase and get the first character theme=$(echo "$theme" | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]') theme=${theme:0:1} if [ "$theme" == "b" ]; then hugo -t Blonde else hugo -t tailwind fi cd public git add -A if [ -z "$message" ]; then echo "No message, committing changes without comment." git commit else git commit -m "$message" fi git push origin echo "Public code successfully pushed." cd "$pathtowebsite" echo "Job done, everything was pushed." exit 0 To commit your changes, type:
./gitcommit.sh Step 8: Search Engine and Crawler optimization (optional) Robots.txt If you set ’enableRobotsTXT=true’ in hugo.toml, then Hugo automatically creates a ‘robots.txt’ file with the content:
User-agent: * This tells web-crawlers that it is okay, to crawl your data. Wikipedia: What is a web-crawler? If you don’t want that (which I personally prefer, but that is your own mindset of course) then you can disallow all crawling by overwriting the default. To do so, create a file called ‘robots.txt’ in the ’layouts’ folder in the private ‘website’ repository. Paste
User-agent: * Disallow: / to disallow all crawling or
User-agent: * Disallow: /contact Disallow: /about Allow: /posts to allow only partial crawling. Modify it to your liking! For more information refer to the official Hugo Documentation Search Engine Optimization SEO You can generate a sitemap, which will help your website to be better found via Google. I already added the necessary section to ‘hugo.toml’ file, so the sitemap is already created. What we now have to do is to tell Google about the sitemap. Therefore we use the Google Search Console.
In your web-browser: Open the Google Search Console https://search.google.com/search-console/about Log in with your Google Account if not already done so Select ‘Add Property’ In the pop-up type your url https://yourdomain.github.io under ‘URL-Prefix’ (not Domain) Select HTML-Tag as your url-ownership verification method and copy the line In your private ‘website’ repository. Open the file ’themes/tailwind/layouts/partials/head.html Add the copied line at the top Save your changes and push the website to github Go back to the Google Search Console website Click on the Finish button, now Google verifies that you have control over the url. If it didn’t work right away, push the website once again to git and wait a minute or two. Then try again. Click on the sitemap entry in the sidebar, fill the field behind ‘yourusername.github.io/’ with ‘sitemap.xml’. That’s it. Now feel free to play around with the Google Search Console.
Workflow This is a short summary that describes all the steps you need to do each time you want to create and publish a new article.
Open Powershell.
Use the cd command to move into your directory, where your website is stored.
Create a new content page (replace yourpostname with the actual title of your new post, but try to avoid blank spaces):
hugo new posts/yourpostname/index.md Open index.md with your favourite text editor or IDE (e.g. Visual Studio Code) and write down your world-changing thoughts.
Optional: Copy the newly created /en/posts/yourpostname/index.md to /de/posts/yourpostname/index.md and translate the text to german. Make sure to have the same values for translationKey. If you are building a multilanguage webpage, I definitely recommend that you translate every blog entry. However, it is not technically required to do so.
To test that everything looks correct, execute the following in PowerShell:
run the hugo server hugo server or
hugo server --buildDrafts if you want all drafts to be compiled as well.
open localhost:1313/ in your browser. (Every time you update content, the change will instantly be shown on localhost. ) If everything is looking good on localhost and you want to make the changes public, execute our script:
Windows ./gitcommit.ps1 Linux ./gitcommit.sh Your website should be available at yourusername.github.io.
Further reading I recommend reading through the demo page of the tailwind theme. There you can find some nice guides on how to include images/videos, enable Emoji-Support 😎 and a Markdown Syntax Guide.
They also provide a guide about how to add add LaTeX-like support (called kaTeX ), however I recommend to follow the official Hugo Math guide . Handy tip: You can verify that you are using KaTeX (and not MathJax or nothing) in the developer tools of your internet browser. Open your website, press F12 to go to the Network-tab, reload the page and you should see a katex.min.css and katex.min.js entry.
Also read this guide if you wish your website to be available under a custom domain.
Congratulations, that’s it! Have fun with your Webpage.
Dependabot alerts After a while of running the Blog, I received Github Dependabot alerts, warning me about security issues with packages specified in the package-lock.json file. To solve those security issues you should definitely asap execute
npm update to update those dependencies. After a new add, commit, push to github via the gitcommit script the security alerts should be solved.
title: How to create a blog with Hugo (full guide)

content:
Overview Why and when do we need linear regression? In a nutshell, linear regression is used to predict data that has a linear relationship. That means all the datapoints are roughly on a straight line (in 2D), resp. on a hyperplane (in 3D). Firstly, we find some expression for the linear relationship (e.g. in 2D: $y=a\cdot x + b$; where $a$ is the slope and $b$ is the y-intercept). Secondly, we use this expression to insert some data where we only know the $x$ value, but want to predict their unknown $y$ value.
Linear regression with one variable To get a feeling and understanding what linear regression is, we start with the most simple version: The 2D linear regression with one variable.
Figure 1: Some example data with a linear relationship and a regression line.
Figure 1 shows some datapoints, which are roughly linearly lined up. The expression for the blue regression line is: $y=a\cdot x + b$; where $a$ is the slope and $b$ is the y-intercept.
Find best regression line First, we find $a$ and $b$ such that some error is minimized. Most datapoints are not on exactly on the regression line. Thus the $x$-$y$-relationship of each datapoint $i$ is not simply $y_i=a\cdot x_i + b$, but actually $y_i=a\cdot x_i + b + \varepsilon _i$ where $\varepsilon _i$ is the error we want to minimize. We can rewrite the expression and get $\varepsilon_i = y_i - (ax_i+b)$ where $y_i$ is the actual value and $- (ax_i+b)$ is the predicted value.
Now, what do we actually want to achieve? We want to minimize the total error $\varepsilon$ which is some ‘compound’ of all $\varepsilon_i$.
To get $\varepsilon$ we could try one of the following:
add up all $\varepsilon_i$ -> not a good idea as positive and negative $\varepsilon_i$ cancel each other add up the absolute value of all $\varepsilon_i$ -> not that smart, because $\varepsilon$ won’t be differentiable anymore add up the squares: $\varepsilon_i^2$ -> That is the so called least squared method (aka Euclidean Norm). Perfect! Next, we compute the least squares. We want to minimize some function $f(a,b)$.
$f(a,b) = \sum_{i=1}^n \varepsilon_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-ax_i-b)^2$, where $n$ is the number of $x$-$y$-pairs we have
We take the derivatives wrt. to $a$ and $b$ and set them equal to zero.
I) $f_a = \frac{\partial f(a,b)}{\partial a} = \sum_{i=1}^n -2 (y_i-ax_i-b) x_i = 0$
II) $f_b = \frac{\partial f(a,b)}{\partial b} = \sum_{i=1}^n -2 (y_i-ax_i-b) = 0$
Then we do the math: (Note that we simply ommit the -2 first)
I) $0 =\sum y_i x_i - \sum a x_i^2 - \sum b x_i = \sum y_i x_i - a \sum x_i^2 - b\sum x_i $
II) $0 =\sum y_i - \sum a x_i - \sum b = \sum y_i - a \sum x_i - nb $
from II) $nb = \sum y_i - a \sum x_i$
II) in I) $0 =\sum y_i x_i - \sum a x_i^2 - (\frac{\sum y_i}{n} - \frac{a\sum x_i}{n})$
$\sum y_i x_i - \frac{\sum x_i\cdot \sum y_i}{n} = a (\sum x_i^2 - \frac{\sum x_i \cdot \sum x_i}{n})$
$ a = \frac{\sum x_i \sum y_i - n \sum x_iy_i}{(\sum x_i)^2 - n \sum x_i^2}$
$ b = \frac{\sum y_i - a \sum x_i}{n}$
That’s it. Now we have two expressions how we can find $a$ and $b$ of a given dataset.
Everything we need to do is to plug in all the data into the formulas for $a$ and $b$. Then we can take some unknow datapoint where we only have $x_i$, but not the corresponding $y_i$. We insert $x_i$ into our formula of $y_i = ax_i+b$ and we will get the $y_i$ value.
In case we leave 2D and want to solve problems in higher dimensions, we could do exactly the same procedure. In 3D we have $y_i = ax + bz + c$ and can solve the three resulting equalities to find some formulas for $a$, $b$ and $c$. However, the more dimensions we get, the more computation expensive this will become. Luckily, there is a smarter way, the Normal Equation of Linear Regression. We will come to that later.
Example Consider the following five datapoints: (0,1), (1,1), (2,2), (3,2), (4,3)
$ a = \frac{\sum x_i \sum y_i - n \sum x_iy_i}{(\sum x_i)^2 - n \sum x_i^2} = \frac{5\cdot(0\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 2 + 3\cdot 2 + 4\cdot 3)- (0+1+2+3+4)\cdot (1+1+2+2+3)}{5\cdot(0^2+1^2+2^2+3^2+4^2)-(0+1+2+3+4)^2} = \frac{115-90}{150-100} = \frac{25}{50} = 0.5$
$ b = \frac{\sum y_i - a \sum x_i}{n} = \frac{1+1+2+2+3-0.5\cdot(0+1+2+3+4)}{5} = \frac{9-5}{5} = 0.8$
Thus, the final equation is $y = 0.5x+0.8$
If we want to predict the y-value for $x=2$, we get: $y=0.5\cdot2+0.8 = 1.8 \not = 2$. That is correct and makes sense, as our regression line does not perfectly go through each point, but rather minimizes the overall error.
Figure 2: Our example’s datapoints with their regression line.
Assumptions The previous example worked very well. However, our data needs to fulfill specific requirements.
Linearity: A linear relationship between all $x$ and $y$ datapoints Homoscedasticity: The variance of error is independent of $x$ Normality: The data is normally distributed Independence: Each measurements/datapoint is independent from other measurements/datapoints If those four requirements are fulfilled, we can use linear regression.
Linearization of non-linear models Some real-life data may be not linear. E.g. the growth of bacteria-population is exponentially distributed. There are some tricks, how we can linearize some non-linear data, s.t. linear regression is applicable.
The following table shows some of the more common models and their linearization.
Name of Model Formula of Model Linearized Model Explanation/Hint Exponnential distribution $y=a\cdot exp(bx)$ $ln(y) = bx + ln(a)$ Take natural logarithm on both sides of the equation. Power Model $y=ax^b$ $ln(y) = ln(a) + bln(x)$ Take natural logaritm. Saturation Model $y = \frac{ax}{b-x}$ $\frac{1}{y} = \frac{1}{x} \frac{b}{a} + \frac{1}{a}$ Reverse fractions, then simplify Polynomial Regression $y = a_nx^n+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}+a_{n-2}x^{n-2}+...+a_1x^1+a_0$ Not so simple, see below. Figure 2: Our example’s datapoints with their regression line.
The polynomial Regression needs a little bit more text.
Polynomial Regression of order m=2 (quadratic) We have $y = a_2x^2+a_1x^1+a_0 + \varepsilon$ and the next steps are actually pretty close to what we did above to find the best regression line.
The sum of the squares of the errors $S = \sum_i \big((y_i-a-bx_i-cx_i^2)^2\big)$. Differentiating wrt. $a$, $b$ and $c$ we get:
I) $an + bS_1+cS_2 = S_5$
II) $aS_1 + bS_2+cS_3 = S_6$
III) $aS_2 + bS_3+cS_4 = S_7$
where $S_1 = \sum_i x_i$
$S_2 = \sum_i x_i^2$
$S_3 = \sum_i x_i^3$
$S_4 = \sum_i x_i^4$
$S_5 = \sum_i y_i$
$S_6 = \sum_i x_iy_i$
$S_7 = \sum_i x_i^2y_i$
Do you see the pattern?
Polynomial regression for any order m If yes, then the following might be interesting to you. It is (with a small modification) taken from the Wikipedia-Article about Polynomial Regression . Solving this linear system for the $a_i$’s will work for any polynomial power $m$.
$ \left[ \begin{matrix} \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^0 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^1 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 & \cdots & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^m \\ \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^1 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^3 & \cdots & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^{m+1} \\ \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^3 & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^4 & \cdots & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^{m+2} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^m & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^{m+1} & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^{m+2} & \cdots & \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^{2m} \\ \end{matrix} \right] $ $ \left[ \begin{matrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_m \\ \end{matrix} \right] $ $=\left[ \begin{matrix} \sum_{i=0}^n y_i x_i^0 \\ \sum_{i=0}^n y_i x_i^1 \\ \sum_{i=0}^n y_i x_i^2 \\ \vdots \\ \sum_{i=0}^n y_i x_i^m \\ \end{matrix} \right] $
Normal Equation of Linear Regression So far we mainly talked about linear regression in 2D and realized that doing linear regression in higher dimensions is analog to 2D, but very includes a lot of work. Luckily, as already mentioned, there is an easier way to do linear regression for higher dimensions.
Normal Equation of Linear Regression in 2D Derivation $\vec u=\vec b-x\vec a$
min $\frac12 (\vec b - x\vec a)^2$
$\frac{d}{d\vec a} \frac12 (\vec b - x\vec a)^2 = (\vec b - x\vec a)\vec a = 0$
$ \vec b\cdot\vec a - x\vec a\cdot\vec a = 0$
$x = \frac{\vec b\cdot\vec a}{\vec a\cdot\vec a}$
To get the general solution for the least squares method in n-dimensions we do the following steps:
$\varepsilon(x) = (\vec a x - \vec b)^2 = (\vec a x - \vec b)^T (\vec a x - \vec b) = (\vec a x)^T (\vec a x) - (\vec a x)^T \vec b - \vec a x \vec b^T + \vec b^T\vec b = x^2\vec a^T\vec a - x(\vec a^T\vec b+\vec b^T\vec a)+\vec b^T\vec b = x^2\vec a^T\vec a - 2x\vec a^T\vec b+\vec b^T\vec b$
$\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x} = 2x\vec a^T\vec a -2\vec a^T\vec b = 0$
$x\vec a^T\vec a -\vec a^T\vec b = 0$
Th gives the general solution for the least squares method in n-dimensions:
$$x=\frac{\vec a^T\vec b}{\vec a^T\vec a}$$
Example Let $\vec a = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \\\end{pmatrix}$ and $\vec b = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \\\end{pmatrix}$ then $\varepsilon (x) = (2x-1)^2+(3x-2)^2+(4x-3)^2 \rightarrow \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x} = 2(2x-1)+2(3x-2)+2(4x-3) = 0 \Leftrightarrow (2x-1)+(3x-2)+(4x-3) = 0$. This equation can be solved for $x$ and you will get $x=\frac23$.
Normal Equation of Linear Regression in higher dimensions Derivation Example This content will follow, when I have time to continue.

Hello,
You do not know what OpenPGP is? Then read the text below .
Download my public key as a zip file and import it into your encryption programme.
↓ OpenPGP Public Key What is OpenPGP? Unfortunately, our emails are currently not encrypted by default. It is therefore very likely that government agencies (NSA and co.) and hackers can read our emails. This is not a desirable state of affairs. Encrypting email content with OpenPGP (PGP stands for ‘Pretty Good Privacy’) provides a remedy. A public key, with which the message is encrypted, and a private (secret) key, with which the message can be decrypted, are generated. As the name suggests, the public key can be made accessible to everyone, because how else can others write to you in encrypted form? The private key, on the other hand, must be kept safe so that it does not fall into the wrong hands.
You can find more information on the OpenPGP website or read their wikipedia article .
How to use OpenPGP Personally, I use Thunderbird to encrypt emails, but there are a whole range of options. It’s not too difficult to set up.
Some of the most popular solutions are mentioned below, but there are many more .
Windows Outlook Outlook does not support OpenPGP natively. But you can install the (open-source) plug-in Gpg4win .
Thunderbird In Thunderbird, simply go to Settings>Account Settings>YOUR EMAIL ACCOUNT>End-To-End-Encryption. There you can import your private key or generate a new one.
Linux The easiest way is to use Thunderbird.
Ubuntu:
sudo apt install thunderbird Arch:
sudo pacman -S thunderbird Mobile devices All the common mobile apps such as Outlook, Apple’s mail client, Gmail, Blue Mail etc. do not support OpenPGP. But no worries, there are excellent alternatives.
Android I am personally a big fan of K-9 Mail. To use OpenPGP you need to install the free open-source apps K-9 Mail and OpenKeychain. First, you import your private key into OpenKeychain, then you go to the Account Settings of K-9 Mail. There you choose your Email-Account, click on “Cryptography” and select OpenKeychain as your OpenPGP Provider. That’s it.
iOS Propably the free and open-source app PGPro is the best choice. However, I never tested this app, so I can’t give any recommendation.
Authentification You can also let the governikus GmbH (owned by BSI Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik, the german ministry for safety in information technology) authenticate your public keys. Follow the steps on their website .

content:
Introduction This article gives a quick overview over the feedforward phase of a Neural Network.
Nodes A Neural Network is made of many Nodes that are connected to each other. Each Node itself is a very basic unit that does only do three things.
Multiply each input with a weight and sum all inputs up. $y_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^m w_i\cdot x_i$, where $m$ is the number of inputs. Process the single, scalar input value by some activation function $y_{out}=f(y_{in})$ where $y_{in}$ is the result from step 1 and $y_{out}$ is the result from step 1 after being applied to the activation function. Output the result $y_{out}$ to the next node So, a single node pretty much looks like this: Figure 1: A single Node with its weights
Here:
$y_{in} = w_1x_1 + w_2x_2 + w_0x_0$ (where each x_i and w_i is a scalar of course) $y_{out} = f(y_{in})$ Bias One special input we have to talk about is the bias $x_0$. Per definition $x_0 = 1$ always. The bias is needed as an offset. You can think of it as the multi-dimensional equivalent to the y-intercept in 2D. Without a y-intercept each function in 2D has to go through the center of the coordinate system. The same applies for our Node: With this offset, the Neural Network can e.g. classify data even if their optimal separation hyperplane does not go through the origin.
Example Let’s say we have the following Node and the activation function $f(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > 0\\ 0 & \text{else } \end{cases}$ Figure 2: Example of single Node with its weights
$y_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^m w_i\cdot x_i = w_1x_1 + w_2x_2 + w_0x_0 = 1\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 1 = 2+8+3 = 13$ $y_{out} = f(y_{in})$. We have $y_{in} = 13 > 0$ so $y_{out} = 1$ The node outputs $y_{out} = 1$ Activation functions In the previous example we used the so called binary step function as our activation function. However, there are a few more I would like to mention here.
Function Formula Description Identify function $f(x)=x$ Very basic Binary step function $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\0 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ Bipolar step function $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\-1 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ Binary Sigmoid $f(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}} = \frac{e^x}{e^x+e^{-x}}$ Bipolar Sigmiod $f(x) = \frac{2}{1+e^{-x}}+1 = \frac{e^x-e^{-x}}{e^x+e^{-x}} = tanh(x)$ ReLu (Rectified Linear Unit ) $f(x) = max(x,0)$ Softplus $f(x) = ln(1+e^x)$ Leaky ReLu $f(x)=\begin{cases}x & \text{if } x > 0\\\alpha x, \;\alpha\text{ small} & \text{else }\end{cases}$ Softmax $f(x_i)=\frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}$ Figure 3: The Activation Functions
Architecture Now we have seen how each node works. He gets some information, processes this information and outputs the result. That is not very impressing so far, but many of those nodes are combined in a specific Architecture, then they can perform powerful tasks.
Feedforward The following plot shows a very basic Neural Network, that has 3 layers. One input layer $x$, one hidden layer $z$ and one output layer $y$. Between each consecutive layer we have connections $w$ and $v$. For the moment, we don’t care about how the weights were computed, as this is done by the training process. The feedforward part is used for predicting, the output for a given input. If we are testing the Neural Network, we use the feedforward method, as shown in this article. In the training part, we also use feedforward, but that is a topic for another article: Backpropgation
Figure 4: Neural Network with 3 layers
Each connection $w_{ij}$ is the weight from node $x_i$ to $z_j$. So, for example $w_{01}$ is the weight from $x_0$ (the bias!) to $z_1$.
Steps of Feedforward For each layer (starting at the first hidden layer) For each node in the layer $z_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^m w_i\cdot x_i$, where $m$ is the number of inputs. Resp. $y_{in} = \sum_{i=0}^p v_i\cdot z_i$, where $p$ is the number of inputs. Apply the activation function $z_{out}=f(z_{in})$, resp. $y_{out}=f(y_{in})$ Output the result $z_{out}$ to the next layer, resp. $y_{out}$ to the output End End Example Let’s consider the following Neural Network. Each node has the binary step function $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\0 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ applied to it.
Figure 5: Example of a Neural Network with 3 layers
We begin with the hidden layer First we calculate all $z_{in}$ (Note that $x_0$ and $z_0$ are the biases, so $x_0=1$ and $z_0=1$ always. ) $z_{1,in} = w_{01}x_0+w_{11}x_1+w_{21}x_2 = 1\cdot 1+2\cdot 2+(-0.5)\cdot 3 = 3.5$ $z_{2,in} = w_{02}x_0+w_{12}x_1+w_{22}x_2 = 0\cdot 1+3\cdot 2+(-2)\cdot 3 = 0$ $z_{3,in} = w_{03}x_0+w_{13}x_1+w_{23}x_2 = 4\cdot 1+(-4)\cdot 2+1\cdot 3 = -1$ Apply the activation function $f(x) = \begin{cases}1 & \text{if } x > 0\\0 & \text{else }\end{cases}$ $z_{1,out} = f(z_{1,in}) = f(3.5)= 1$ $z_{2,out} = f(z_{2,in}) = f(0) = 0$ $z_{3,out} = f(z_{3,in}) = f(-1)= 0$ We repeat the same for the output layer Calculate all $y_{in}$ $y_{1,in} = v_{01}\cdot z_0+v_{11}\cdot z_{1,out}+v_{21}\cdot z_{2,out}+v_{31}\cdot z_{3,out} =0.25\cdot 1+2\cdot 1+1.5\cdot 0+0.75\cdot 0 = 2.25$ $y_{2,in} = v_{02}\cdot z_0+v_{12}\cdot z_{1,out}+v_{22}\cdot z_{2,out}+v_{32}\cdot z_{3,out} =(-1)\cdot 1 + 0.5\cdot 1+1\cdot 0 + 1.25\cdot 0 = -0.5$ Apply the activation function $y_{1,out} = f(y_{1,in}) = f(2.25)= 1$ $y_{2,out} = f(y_{2,in}) = f(-0.5) = 0$ Tadaa! This is our final output. Our net predicts $y_1 = 1$ and $y_2 = 0$
Feedforward in Matrix Calculus As you already may have guessed the whole process we did before can be simplified by using Matrix Calculus.
Figure 6: Simplified Neural Network to demonstrate the Matrix Calculus
The steps are pretty much the same:
For each layer (starting at the first hidden layer) $z_{in} = W^T x$ Apply the activation function $z_{out}=f(z_{in})$ Output the result $z_{out}$ End So, the little bit more detailed steps for our Neural Network in Figure 6 are:
$z_{in} = W^Tx$ $z_{out} = f(z_{in})$ $y_{in} = V^Tz$ $y_{out} = f(y_{in})$ As you can see this looks way more handsome than taking the sum over all input value multipiled with their weights. Dimensions Hereby we have to consider the dimensions of the different layers. If $x\in \R^{m\times 1}$, $z\in \R^{p\times 1}$ and $y\in \R^{n\times 1}$ then $W\in \R^{m\times p-1}$ and $V\in \R^{p\times n}$.
Example Let’s do the example from Figure 5 another time. We use the identity activation function again, but do the math in matrix form this time.
First of all, let’s determine $W$ and $V$.
$W=\begin{bmatrix} w_{01} & w_{02} & w_{03} \\ w_{11} & w_{12} & w_{13} \\ w_{21} & w_{22} & w_{23} \end{bmatrix} \text{, } V=\begin{bmatrix} v_{01} & v_{02} \\ v_{11} & v_{12} \\ v_{21} & v_{22} \\ v_{31} & v_{32} \end{bmatrix}$
Just for clarity, we check the dimensions: $x\in \R^{3\times 1}$, $z\in \R^{4\times 1}$ and $y\in \R^{2\times 1}$ then $W\in \R^{3\times 3}$ and $V\in \R^{4\times 2}$.
If we fill in the values, then we get $W=\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & -4 \\ -0.5 & -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{, } V=\begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & -1 \\ 2 & 0.5 \\ 1.5 & 1 \\ 0.75 & 1.25 \end{bmatrix}$
Let’s do the steps:
$z_{in} = W^T x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & -4 \\ -0.5 & -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1\\ 2\\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.5 \\ 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$
$z_{out}=f(z_{in})= \begin{bmatrix} f(z_{1,in})\\ f(z_{2,in})\\ f(z_{3,in}) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f(3.5)\\ f(0)\\ f(-1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1\\ 0\\ 0\\ \end{bmatrix} $
$y_{in} = V^T z_{out} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 2 & 1.5 & 0.75 \\ -1 & 0.5 & 1 & 1.25 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1\\1\\0\\0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.25\\-0.5 \end{bmatrix} $
Note that in step 3 we used the same $z_{out}$ as in step 2, but added the bias $z_0$ at the “top”.
$y_{out} = f(y_{in}) = \begin{bmatrix} f(y_{1,in})\\ f(y_{2,in})\\ \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f(2.25)\\ f(-0.5)\\ \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1\\ 0\\ \end{bmatrix} $
This is the same result as if we had used the normal way, without matrix calculations.
